1. The horn draws a **high current of 4–8 A**, which would cause excessive **I²R heating** in the thin wires and slip-ring contacts of the steering switch.
2. A relay allows the **steering switch to handle only the small relay-coil current (≈ 0.2 A)**, isolating the high-current load path.
3. This **protects the switch contacts from pitting and arcing**, increasing switch life.
4. It also **reduces voltage drop** by using a dedicated heavy-gauge battery-to-horn circuit, ensuring full loudness.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`

**Q:** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an **automotive wiper system**. Explain how the **park switch** returns the blades to the rest position after switch-off. Also list **six steps** to test a wiper motor in the laboratory.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Construction and Working

**Construction:**

The wiper system consists of:
- A **permanent-magnet DC motor** mounted near the base of the windshield.
- A **worm-and-wheel gear reduction** unit (typically 50:1) inside the motor housing.
- A **crank arm** attached to the gear output that converts rotation into oscillation.
- A **linkage assembly** with pivot joints connecting the crank to the wiper arms.
- A **three-brush commutator** in the motor for dual-speed operation.
- An internal **park switch** operated by a cam on the gear train.
- A **multi-function switch** on the steering column.
- A **fuse (15–20 A)** in the supply line.

**Working:**

When the driver selects LOW or HIGH:
1. The multi-function switch routes battery voltage to the appropriate brush.
2. Current flows through the armature, producing torque.
3. The worm gear reduces speed and increases torque.
4. The crank converts rotary motion into oscillating motion.
5. The linkage transmits this oscillation to both wiper arms simultaneously.
6. Blades sweep across the windshield at the selected speed.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Park Switch Function & Testing Steps

**Park switch mechanism:**
- When the switch is moved to OFF, the **park switch remains closed** until the linkage cam returns the blades to the rest position.
- The motor therefore continues to run briefly, driving the linkage to the park position.
- Once the cam reaches the park angle, the **park switch opens**, disconnecting power and stopping the motor precisely below the hood line.

**Six Testing Steps:**

| Step | Action | Instrument |
|------|--------|------------|
| 1 | Visual inspection of blades, arms, and linkage | — |
| 2 | Measure supply voltage at fuse terminals | Multimeter (DC V) |
| 3 | Test switch continuity in each position | Multimeter (Ω) |
| 4 | Measure voltage at motor terminal during operation | Multimeter (DC V) |
| 5 | Measure current drawn by motor at LOW speed | Clamp ammeter / in-line ammeter |
| 6 | Verify park function and listen for smooth operation | Observation |

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Draw the **circuit diagram of an automotive horn system** with relay. Explain the working of the horn and the relay. Describe **four laboratory tests** to diagnose a non-working horn.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Circuit Diagram and Working

**Circuit Diagram (textual representation):**

```
Battery(+) ──► Fuse(15A) ──► Relay Terminal 30
                                 │
                                 │ (contact 87)
                                 ▼
                            Horn (+) ──► Horn Coil ──► Diaphragm
                                 │
                                 ▼
                         Chassis Ground ──► Battery(−)

Control path:
Ignition(+) ──► Horn Switch ──► Relay Coil (85) ──► Relay Coil (86) ──► Ground
```

**Working:**

1. When the driver presses the horn switch, a low current (~0.2 A) energizes the relay coil.
2. The coil's magnetic field pulls the relay armature, closing the high-current contact between terminals 30 and 87.
3. Battery voltage is now applied directly to the horn through a heavy-gauge wire.
4. Current flows through the horn coil, magnetizing the core.
5. The core attracts the spring-steel **diaphragm**, which moves and simultaneously **breaks the contact**.
6. With the circuit broken, the diaphragm springs back, re-closing the contact.
7. This **rapid make-break cycle (~400 Hz)** produces the horn's audible tone.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Four Laboratory Tests

| # | Test | Procedure | Expected Result |
|---|------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Fuse Continuity Test** | Disconnect fuse; measure across terminals with multimeter (Ω) | **0 Ω** (good); **OL** (blown) |
| 2 | **Horn Switch Continuity Test** | Disconnect switch; measure across contacts while pressed | **0 Ω** when pressed; **OL** when released |
| 3 | **Voltage at Horn Terminal** | Connect voltmeter across horn terminals while switch is pressed | **>10 V DC** |
| 4 | **Horn Current Draw Test** | Connect ammeter in series with horn; press switch | **4–8 A** |
| 5 | **Relay Click Test** (bonus) | Listen for audible click when pressing horn switch | **Distinct click** heard |
| 6 | **Ground Continuity Test** (bonus) | Measure resistance between horn body and chassis | **<0.5 Ω** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students LOSE MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "wiper motor" with "starter motor":** Both are DC motors, but the wiper motor is **permanent-magnet type**, NOT series-wound. Stating the wrong type costs **2 marks** outright.
> 2. **Forgetting the park-switch explanation:** KTU examiners specifically test this in 14-mark questions. Skipping the park function = **loss of 3 marks minimum**.
> 3. **Drawing the horn circuit WITHOUT the relay:** A direct switch-to-horn connection is considered an *incorrect* design. Always include the relay.
> 4. **Wrong fuse rating:** Writing "5 A fuse" for horn (should be **15–20 A**) shows lack of practical knowledge — **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Listing testing steps without specifying instruments:** Steps like "check voltage" without saying **multimeter / DC Volts** are marked incomplete. Always pair the action with the **instrument** and the **expected reading**.
> 6. **Mixing up LOW and HIGH speed brush positions:** LOW = more coils energized; HIGH = fewer coils energized (higher RPM). Reverse this = **−2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PCAUL507 — Module Experiment 2:**
>
> - **Wiper motor type:** Permanent-magnet DC (PMDC) with **three brushes** for two speeds.
> - **Nominal operating voltage:** **12 V DC**; weak battery (<10.5 V) causes slow wiping.
> - **Park switch:** Internal limit switch that keeps motor running until blades reach rest position, then breaks the circuit.
> - **Intermittent wiper:** Achieved via electronic timer IC (modern) or bimetallic thermal switch (older).
> - **Horn type:** Electromagnetic diaphragm horn with make-break contact vibrating at **300–400 Hz**.
> - **Horn current:** **4–8 A** typical; horn coil resistance typically **1.5–3 Ω**.
> - **Relay purpose:** Isolates high horn current from low-current steering switch contacts.
> - **Relay terminals:** **30** = battery in, **87** = load out, **85** = coil in, **86** = coil ground.
> - **Standard fuse ratings:** Wiper = **15–20 A**; Horn = **15 A**.
> - **Key testing instruments:** Multimeter (V, Ω), clamp ammeter, test lamp, continuity tester.
> - **Voltage at load terminal must be >10 V** during operation — anything less indicates wiring drop.
> - **Ground path resistance must be <0.5 Ω** — measure between component body and clean chassis point.
> - **Lab viva one-liners:** "Why three brushes?" / "Why a relay?" / "Why does the wiper park below the hood?" / "What does a weak battery do to wiper speed?"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servicing and testing of lighting systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL507 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Servicing and testing of lighting systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automotive Lighting System Servicing and Testing** is the systematic procedure of inspecting, maintaining, repairing, and verifying the operational integrity of all illumination devices fitted on a motor vehicle — including **headlamps, tail lamps, brake lights, turn indicators, fog lamps, reversing lamps, side markers, and interior lamps** — to ensure they conform to the **Indian Motor Vehicle Rules (AIS-008/CMVR)** and the **Automotive Industry Standards** for photometric output, beam pattern, color, and electrical consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** This experiment tests the learner's ability to dismantle, inspect, test, and reassemble a vehicle's lighting circuit using standard garage tools (multimeter, test lamp, lux meter, headlight aligner) and to interpret the electrical and optical performance using quantitative measurements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Eyes of the Car"

Think of the lighting system as the **eyes of a living organism**. Just like our eyes allow us to **see (forward lighting)**, **communicate (indicators)**, and **warn others (brake/tail lamps)**, the automotive lighting system has three primary functions:

1. **See (Forward Illumination):** Headlamps project light to illuminate the road ahead.
2. **Be Seen (Conspicuity):** Tail, brake, and side marker lamps make the vehicle visible to others.
3. **Communicate (Signaling):** Turn signals, hazard lamps, and reversing lamps convey driver intent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Candle on a Stick:** Imagine a candle (bulb) fixed on a stick (reflector) placed inside a glass dome (lens). When the candle is lit (current flows), light radiates outward. The dome (lens) and the curved stick (reflector) shape this raw light into a controlled beam. *Servicing* ensures the candle is bright, the stick is shiny, and the dome is clean. *Testing* ensures the light shines in the correct direction with adequate intensity.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Standard Headlamp Testing Distance:** **7.62 m (25 feet)** — used universally for beam alignment on a screen/wall.
- **Standard Luminous Flux Unit:** **Lumen (lm)**.
- **Standard Illuminance Unit:** **Lux (lx) = 1 lm/m²**.
- **Standard Vehicle Supply Voltage:** **12 V DC** (nominal battery voltage) for passenger cars; **24 V DC** for heavy commercial vehicles.
- **High Beam Color Temperature (Halogen):** **~3200 K**.
- **HID Xenon Color Temperature:** **~4300 K to 6000 K** (closer to daylight at **5500 K**).

> [!TIP]
> Always measure the **resting battery voltage** before commencing any lighting test. A reading below **12.4 V** indicates a discharged battery, which can produce misleading low-lumen readings and false voltage-drop results.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Automotive Lamps

| Lamp Type | Working Principle | Luminous Efficacy | Typical Power |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Incandescent (Vacuum/Gas-filled)** | Tungsten filament heated to white heat | 10–15 lm/W | 21W, 45/65W |
| **Halogen** | Tungsten + Halogen gas (Iodine/Bromine) regenerative cycle | 18–25 lm/W | 55W (H4), 60/55W (H7) |
| **HID / Xenon (Discharge)** | Electric arc through xenon gas and metal halide salts | 80–120 lm/W | 35W |
| **LED (Light Emitting Diode)** | Electroluminescence at P-N junction | 80–150 lm/W | 15–25W |
| **Laser (Matrix/Adaptive)** | Laser-excited phosphor | ~170 lm/W | ~30W (per unit) |

### 2.2 Headlamp Beam Patterns and Purpose

A motor vehicle headlamp has **two distinct filaments** (in a dual-filament bulb like H4) or **two separate reflectors** (in projector systems):

- **Low Beam (Dipped Beam / Passing Beam):** Casts light **downward and to the right** (in LHD vehicles, to the left) with a sharp **cut-off line** to prevent dazzling oncoming drivers. Acts like a flashlight angled at the road 30 m ahead.
- **High Beam (Main Beam / Driving Beam):** Casts light **straight ahead and slightly downward** to illuminate ~100–150 m of road. Used on open highways with no oncoming traffic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Beams?** — Physics of Glare Prevention. The eye's pupil dilates in the dark. If a single, very bright beam were used, it would **temporarily blind** oncoming drivers (called *physiological glare* or *disability glare*). The low beam's asymmetric pattern with a horizontal cut-off is a compromise between **seeing the road** and **not blinding others**.

### 2.3 The Lighting Circuit — Key Components

A typical automotive lighting circuit contains the following elements in **series-parallel** combination:

1. **Battery (Source):** 12 V DC, ~45–65 Ah.
2. **Fuse:** Protective device (typically **10 A to 30 A**) rated for the specific circuit.
3. **Switch (Manual/Relay-Controlled):** The dashboard switch or steering-column stalk.
4. **Relay (Electromagnetic Switch):** A low-current circuit controls a high-current path. Coil resistance typically **~80 Ω** at 12 V.
5. **Wiring Harness:** Copper conductors, AWG sized to limit voltage drop to **< 0.5 V** (or **< 3% of system voltage**).
6. **Bulb (Load):** Converts electrical energy into light and heat.
7. **Ground Return:** Chassis acts as the return path; resistance must be **< 0.1 Ω**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $$P = V \times I$$ | Electrical power consumed by bulb | Watts (W) |
| $$I = \dfrac{P}{V}$$ | Current drawn by bulb | Amperes (A) |
| $$R = \dfrac{V}{I}$$ | Cold/hot resistance of filament | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $$E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$$ | Illuminance = Luminous flux / Area | Lux (lx) |
| $$E = \dfrac{I_v}{d^2}$$ | Inverse Square Law (point source) | Distance $d$ in metres |
| $$\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$$ | Luminous efficacy | lm/W |
| $$V_{drop} = I \times R_{wire}$$ | Voltage drop across conductor | Should be < 0.5 V |
| $$\text{Regulation \%} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{NL}} \times 100$$ | Voltage regulation of circuit | < 5% desired |
| $$I_{total} = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + \dots$$ | Sum of parallel branch currents | Kirchhoff's Current Law |
| $$\Sigma V_{rise} = \Sigma V_{drop}$$ | Around any closed loop | Kirchhoff's Voltage Law |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Tip:** Always check the **cold resistance** vs **hot resistance** of a bulb. A halogen 55W headlamp at 12V draws **~4.6 A** when hot, but the cold inrush current at switch-on can be **8–10× higher** (up to **~30 A**) for a few milliseconds. This is why dim/burnt bulbs are common in winter and on cold starts.

### 2.5 Photometric Concepts in Plain English

- **Luminous Flux ($\Phi$):** The *total* amount of light energy emitted by a source per second, measured in **lumens (lm)**. *Think: total water flowing from a tap.*
- **Luminous Intensity ($I_v$):** Light emitted **per unit solid angle** in a specific direction, measured in **candela (cd)**. *Think: how concentrated the water stream is.*
- **Illuminance ($E$):** Light *falling on* a surface per unit area, measured in **lux (lx)**. *Think: how wet you get standing under the tap.*
- **Luminance ($L$):** Light *reflected from* or *emitted by* a surface, measured in **cd/m²**. *Think: how bright the wet surface looks.*

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Adaptive Front-lighting System (AFS):** Modern headlamps swivel up to **15°** in the direction of steering to illuminate corners.
- **Daytime Running Lamps (DRLs):** Mandatory in many countries (Europe since 2011); consume just **~10 W** using LEDs.
- **Matrix LED / Pixel Lighting:** Allows the vehicle to project a *high beam* continuously while selectively *darkening* pixels where oncoming traffic is detected via a camera — this is the engineering pinnacle of the lighting systems we service in the lab.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Test Procedures and Expected Outcomes

| # | Test | Tool Used | Procedure (Brief) | Expected/Pass Value | Common Fault |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Visual Inspection** | Naked eye | Check lens, housing, alignment | Clean, intact, no cracks | Yellowed/hazed lens |
| 2 | **Bulb Continuity** | Multimeter ($\Omega$ mode) | Probe bulb base pins | ~0.2–3 $\Omega$ (cold filament) | Open circuit (∞ $\Omega$) |
| 3 | **Supply Voltage Test** | Multimeter (V DC) | Measure at bulb connector | 12.0–14.4 V (engine running) | < 11.5 V = low charge |
| 4 | **Voltage Drop Test** | Multimeter (V DC) | Across switch, wire, ground | < 0.3 V per segment | Corroded connector |
| 5 | **Current Draw Test** | Clamp Meter / Ammeter | Connect in series with bulb | 4.6 A (55W@12V hot) | > 6 A = short; < 3 A = weak |
| 6 | **Ground Integrity** | Multimeter | Resistance from bulb ground to battery (-) | < 0.1 $\Omega$ | Rusty chassis point |
| 7 | **Fuse Test** | Multimeter / Visual | Continuity across fuse | 0 $\Omega$ / intact wire | Blown element |
| 8 | **Relay Test** | 12V supply + multimeter | Energize coil; check contacts | Coil $\approx$ 80 $\Omega$; Contacts close | Stuck/welded contacts |
| 9 | **Beam Pattern** | Headlight aligner / screen | 7.62 m distance, vehicle level | Hotspot at center, symmetric | Misaligned reflector |
| 10 | **Illuminance (Lux)** | Lux meter at 10 m | Measure max $E$ on screen | ≥ 30 lx (low beam, AIS) | Below standard = dim bulb |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Headlamp Alignment Geometry (7.62 m Method)

The KTU/CEV standard headlamp alignment procedure uses a vertical screen placed **7.62 m (25 ft)** in front of the vehicle. The geometric basis is derived as follows.

**Step 1:** Place the vehicle on **level ground** with **tire pressure** at the manufacturer's specification and **fuel tank approximately half-full** to represent the normal ride height.

**Step 2:** Measure the **height ($H$)** of the headlamp bulb center from the ground (typical passenger car: **0.7 m to 0.85 m**).

**Step 3:** Mark a horizontal line on the screen at the same height as the lamp center. This is called the **lamp center line ($L_c$)**.

**Step 4:** Mark a second horizontal line **25 mm to 75 mm BELOW** the lamp center line. This is the **high beam aim line ($H_a$)** — because headlamps are designed to aim slightly downward so that at 7.62 m, the brightest portion of the high beam falls at or just below the lamp center.

**Step 5:** For the **low beam**, the *sharpest horizontal cut-off line* should fall **at or 50 mm below** the lamp center line, with the **elbow** of the cut-off (the bend) at the vehicle's **vertical centerline** (for RHD countries, India) or offset by 1°–2° toward the curb.

The **downward tilt angle $\theta$** of the headlamp is given by the geometry:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\Delta h}{D}\right)$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = downward tilt angle of beam in degrees
- $\Delta h$ = vertical drop of hotspot from lamp center line (typically **50 mm to 75 mm**)
- $D$ = distance of screen from vehicle = **7.62 m**

**Step 6 (Numerical Evaluation):** Substituting the standard AIS value of $\Delta h = 50$ mm = 0.05 m:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{0.05}{7.62}\right)$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(0.00656\right)$$

$$\theta \approx 0.376°$$

This means the optical axis of the high beam should be tilted **approximately 0.4° downward from horizontal** — a very small angle that translates into a significant shift at 100 m of road distance.

**Step 7 (Conversion to drop at 100 m):** Using the same geometry but at a far-field distance of 100 m:

$$\Delta h_{100m} = D_{far} \times \tan(\theta) = 100 \times \tan(0.376°)$$

$$\Delta h_{100m} = 100 \times 0.00656 = 0.656 \text{ m}$$

So the high-beam hotspot at **100 m** is **~66 cm** below the headlamp axis — enough to illuminate the road surface clearly without lighting up the sky.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Voltage Drop Calculation

A common KTU-style numerical problem: A 55 W headlamp is fed through a 4-metre length of copper wire having a total loop resistance of **0.05 $\Omega$** (positive + return). Calculate the voltage drop and the percentage loss at the bulb.

**Step 1:** Compute the current drawn by the bulb at nominal voltage.

$$I = \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{55}{12} = 4.583 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the voltage drop across the wire loop.

$$V_{drop} = I \times R_{wire} = 4.583 \times 0.05 = 0.229 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the actual voltage at the bulb terminals.

$$V_{bulb} = V_{battery} - V_{drop} = 12.0 - 0.229 = 11.771 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the percentage voltage drop.

$$\% V_{drop} = \dfrac{0.229}{12.0} \times 100 = 1.91\%$$

**Step 5:** Compare to standard. AIS / SAE allow a maximum of **3% (0.36 V)** voltage drop on the headlamp circuit. Since 1.91% < 3%, the wire is **adequately sized**. If the wire were longer or thinner (say $R_{wire} = 0.2$ $\Omega$):

$$V_{drop} = 4.583 \times 0.2 = 0.917 \text{ V (7.6%)} \rightarrow \text{FAILS}$$

The bulb would receive only **11.08 V**, produce less light (lumen output $\propto V^{3.5}$ for incandescent), and have a dim, yellow appearance. This is the classic "old-car dim headlights" problem.

### 3.4 Analytical Note on Lumen Degradation with Voltage

For an incandescent/halogen bulb, the relationship between **supply voltage ($V$)** and **luminous flux ($\Phi$)** follows the approximation:

$$\dfrac{\Phi}{\Phi_{rated}} = \left(\dfrac{V}{V_{rated}}\right)^{3.5}$$

This shows why a small **8% voltage drop** (from 12 V to 11 V) reduces light output to:

$$\left(\dfrac{11}{12}\right)^{3.5} = (0.917)^{3.5} \approx 0.717 = 71.7\%$$

The bulb produces only **72% of its rated light** — a **28% loss** in road illumination, which the human eye perceives as a major safety hazard.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** When asked "Why are my headlights dim despite a new bulb?", the answer must include the words **"voltage drop"** and reference the **$V^{3.5}$ law** to score full marks. Simply saying "weak battery" is incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Headlamp Circuit Schematic (Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block represents the **series-parallel electrical path** of a typical headlamp circuit. Every block corresponds to a testable physical component.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bat[Battery 12V DC] --> fuse[Fuse 20A]
    fuse --> ignSw[Ignition Switch ON]
    ignSw --> lbr[Lamp Beam Selector Switch]
    lbr -->|Low Beam Path| relayLB[Relay Coil LB]
    lbr -->|High Beam Path| relayHB[Relay Coil HB]
    relayLB -->|NO Contact Closed| bulbLB[Low Beam Filament 55W]
    relayHB -->|NO Contact Closed| bulbHB[High Beam Filament 60W]
    bulbLB --> gnd1[Chassis Ground Point]
    bulbHB --> gnd2[Chassis Ground Point]
    gnd1 --> chsas[Vehicle Chassis]
    gnd2 --> chsas
    chsas --> batReturn[Return to Battery Negative]
    batReturn --> bat
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **battery** sends 12 V through a **fuse** (overcurrent protection).
- The **ignition switch** prevents lamp operation without the key.
- The **selector switch** chooses between low and high beam, energizing the corresponding **relay coil**.
- Each relay's **Normally Open (NO) contact** closes, sending high current directly from the battery to the bulb — *this is why the switch and relay are needed: the dashboard switch only carries a few milliamps to energize the relay coil, not the full 4–5 A of the bulb.*
- The bulb returns to ground via the **chassis** — a single rusty bolt here causes both lamps to glow dimly.

### 4.2 Servicing Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive Vehicle Complaint] --> B[Visual Inspection of Lamps]
    B --> C{Bulb Functional?}
    C -->|No| D[Replace Bulb with Rated Type]
    C -->|Yes| E[Check Supply Voltage at Connector]
    E --> F{Voltage 12.0 to 14.4 V?}
    F -->|No| G[Charge Battery or Check Alternator]
    F -->|Yes| H[Measure Voltage Drop across Switch and Ground]
    H --> I{Drop less than 0.3 V?}
    I -->|No| J[Clean Connectors and Re-torque Ground]
    I -->|Yes| K[Measure Beam Pattern on Alignment Screen]
    K --> L{Beam centered and Cutoff clean?}
    L -->|No| M[Adjust Headlamp Aiming Screws]
    L -->|Yes| N[Measure Lux at 10 m and Record]
    N --> O[End - Document Service]
    D --> O
    G --> O
    J --> O
    M --> O
```

### 4.3 Beam Pattern Diagram (Top-Down View on Alignment Screen)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SCRN[Alignment Screen at 7.62 m]
        LC["Lamp Center Line H1"]
        HA["High Beam Aim Line H2 = H1 minus 50 to 75 mm"]
        LB["Low Beam Cutoff Line L1 = H1 minus 0 to 50 mm"]
        VC["Vehicle Vertical Centerline V0"]
        LE["Low Beam Elbow at V0"]
        HB["High Beam Hot Spot centered on V0"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The **High Beam** hotspot (brightest blob) must sit **on or just below the aim line $H_2$** and **centered horizontally** on the vehicle centerline. The **Low Beam** cut-off must be a sharp horizontal line at $L_1$ with a **knee/elbow** rising at $V_0$. In RHD vehicles (right-hand drive, used in India), the elbow is on the **right side** to throw more light toward the verge and away from oncoming traffic.

### 4.4 Lux Measurement Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Headlamp] -->|Light travels 10 m| lux[Lux Meter Sensor]
    lux --> det[Photodiode Converts Light to mV]
    det --> amp[Amplifier Circuit]
    amp --> disp[Digital Display in lux lx]
    disp --> rec[Compare with AIS Standard 30 lx minimum]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*List the components of an automotive headlamp circuit and state the function of the headlamp relay.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The automotive headlamp circuit consists of the following components: **(1 mark)**
1. **Battery** — 12 V DC source of electrical energy.
2. **Fuse** — protects the circuit from short-circuit overcurrent (typically rated 15–20 A).
3. **Ignition switch** — allows the lamps to be energized only when the key is ON.
4. **Beam selector switch** — selects between low beam and high beam filaments.
5. **Relay** — electromagnetic switch using a low-current coil (~80 $\Omega$, ~150 mA) to control the high-current path (~5 A) to the bulb.
6. **Bulb** — the load (H4 halogen, 60/55 W).
7. **Wiring harness and chassis ground** — current return path.

**Function of the relay (2 marks):** The relay allows the dashboard switch to carry only the small coil current (~150 mA) instead of the full bulb current (~5 A). This **(i)** prevents switch contact pitting and overheating, **(ii)** reduces voltage drop in the dashboard wiring, and **(iii)** improves bulb brightness by routing the high current through a short, thick battery-to-bulb cable with minimal loss.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*What is luminous efficacy? Why are LED headlamps preferred over halogen lamps?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Luminous efficacy** ($\eta$) is the ratio of the luminous flux (lumens) emitted by a source to the electrical power (watts) consumed by it, expressed in **lm/W (1 mark)**. Formula: $\eta = \Phi / P$.

**LEDs are preferred over halogen lamps because (2 marks):**
1. **Higher efficacy** — LEDs deliver 80–150 lm/W vs. halogens at 18–25 lm/W, meaning more light per watt consumed.
2. **Longer life** — LED service life is **> 10,000 hours** vs. halogen at ~500–1000 hours.
3. **Lower power draw** — A 25 W LED can match a 55 W halogen, reducing alternator load and improving fuel economy.
4. **Faster illumination rise time** — LEDs light up in microseconds, improving brake-light reaction safety.
5. **Robustness** — No fragile filament; resistant to vibration.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a halogen headlamp bulb. Compare halogen, HID (Xenon), and LED headlamp technologies on the basis of luminous efficacy, life, and color temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure for headlamp beam alignment using the 7.62 m screen method. Mention the standard aim point for low and high beam and the significance of the cut-off line. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Construction of Halogen Bulb (3 marks):**
A halogen bulb is a tungsten-filament incandescent lamp containing a small amount of **halogen gas** (iodine or bromine) inside a toughened **quartz (fused silica) glass envelope**. The filament is a tightly wound tungsten coil supported by molybdenum lead-in wires. The bulb has a **P14.5s or P43t base** for single/dual-filament configurations.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
When current passes, the filament heats to **~2500–3000 °C** and emits light. At this temperature, tungsten atoms evaporate. In a normal bulb, they deposit on the inner glass, causing blackening. In a halogen bulb, the evaporated tungsten combines with the halogen gas to form **tungsten halide**, which migrates back to the hot filament and re-deposits the tungsten. This **halogen regenerative cycle** allows the filament to operate at a higher temperature (brighter) without blackening, extending life and maintaining clarity.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Halogen | HID (Xenon) | LED |
|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous Efficacy | 18–25 lm/W | 80–120 lm/W | 80–150 lm/W |
| Color Temperature | ~3200 K | 4300–6000 K | 5000–6500 K |
| Service Life | 500–1000 hr | 2000–5000 hr | > 10,000 hr |
| Power (typical) | 55–60 W | 35 W | 15–25 W |
| Warm-up Time | Instant | 3–5 seconds | Instant |

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Preparation (1 mark):** Park the vehicle on **level ground**, set tire pressure to manufacturer spec, fill the fuel tank to **half**, and ensure no unusual load. Bounce the suspension to settle it.

**Step 2 — Mark Lamp Center (1 mark):** Measure the height of the headlamp bulb center ($H$) from the floor. Mark a horizontal line on a vertical screen/wall placed exactly **7.62 m (25 feet)** in front of the vehicle at height $H$. This is the **lamp center line $H_1$**.

**Step 3 — Mark Aim Lines (1 mark):**
- **High beam aim line ($H_2$):** 50–75 mm **below** $H_1$.
- **Low beam aim line ($L_1$):** at $H_1$ or up to 50 mm below.
- Mark a vertical centerline $V_0$ aligned with the vehicle's center.

**Step 4 — Adjust Each Headlamp (2 marks):** With the engine running, switch on **high beam** and adjust using the **vertical** and **horizontal** aiming screws on each headlamp so that the **brightest spot (hot spot)** of the high beam falls **on the $H_2$ line** and is **centered on $V_0$**. Repeat for the low beam — the **sharp cut-off line** must align with $L_1$ and the **knee of the cut-off** at $V_0$.

**Step 5 — Verification (1 mark):** Repeat from the driver's seat; the pattern should be symmetric, and the high beam should not project above the lamp center line at the screen. Measure illuminance with a lux meter: a properly aimed halogen low beam must deliver **≥ 30 lux at 10 m** as per AIS-008.

**Significance of the Cut-off Line (1 mark):** The cut-off line is a sharp, asymmetric horizontal demarcation in the low-beam pattern. It exists to **prevent glare** to oncoming drivers while still illuminating the road and the right-side verge. A diffuse or misaligned cut-off is a legal non-conformance and a safety hazard.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]** *(Alternative to Q.A)*

**(a)** Explain with a circuit diagram the headlamp circuit using a relay. Why is a relay used in the headlamp circuit instead of a direct switch? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 60 W headlamp is supplied by a 12 V battery through a wire loop having a total resistance of 0.15 $\Omega$. Calculate (i) the current drawn, (ii) the voltage at the bulb terminals, (iii) the percentage voltage drop, and (iv) comment on whether the circuit meets the AIS standard of less than 3% drop. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Circuit Diagram (3 marks):** [Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block — show the same circuit in hand-drawn form: Battery → Fuse → Ignition Switch → Relay Coil in parallel with the Switch → Relay NO contact in series with Bulb → Ground → Battery negative.]

**Explanation (2 marks):** When the dashboard switch is closed, current from the battery flows through the relay **coil** (~80 $\Omega$, drawing ~150 mA). This energizes the coil, producing a magnetic field that pulls the **movable armature**, closing the **Normally Open (NO) power contact**. The main high-current path is now complete: Battery (+) → Fuse → Relay NO contact → Bulb filament → Ground → Battery (−). The bulb lights up.

**Why a Relay is Used Instead of a Direct Switch (2 marks):**
1. **The dashboard switch cannot safely handle 5 A** without arcing, pitting, and overheating. The relay isolates the high current to a heavy cable near the lamp.
2. **Voltage drop is minimized** because the high-current path uses a short, thick wire directly from the battery to the bulb, bypassing the long, thin dashboard harness.
3. **The switch contacts last longer** because they only switch ~150 mA (coil current), not 5 A.
4. **Safety** — if the switch fails short, only the coil burns out, not the wiring loom.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- Bulb power $P = 60$ W
- Battery voltage $V_b = 12$ V
- Wire loop resistance $R_w = 0.15$ $\Omega$

**Step 1 — Current drawn by bulb at rated voltage (2 marks):**

$$I = \dfrac{P}{V_b} = \dfrac{60}{12} = 5.0 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage drop across the wire (1 mark):**

$$V_{drop} = I \times R_w = 5.0 \times 0.15 = 0.75 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage at bulb terminals (1 mark):**

$$V_{bulb} = V_b - V_{drop} = 12.0 - 0.75 = 11.25 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage voltage drop (1 mark):**

$$\% V_{drop} = \dfrac{0.75}{12.0} \times 100 = 6.25\%$$

**Step 5 — Comment on AIS standard (2 marks):**
The AIS standard allows a maximum of **3%** voltage drop on a headlamp circuit, i.e., a maximum of **0.36 V** for a 12 V system. The actual drop is **6.25% (0.75 V)**, which is **more than twice the permissible limit**. Therefore, **the circuit does NOT meet the AIS standard**. The bulb will receive only **11.25 V** instead of 12 V, leading to a lumen loss of:

$$\left(\dfrac{11.25}{12}\right)^{3.5} = (0.9375)^{3.5} \approx 0.808 = 80.8\%$$

The bulb will produce only **~81% of rated light output** — a noticeable reduction in road illumination. **Recommendation:** Replace the wire with a thicker gauge (e.g., from AWG 16 to AWG 12) to reduce $R_w$ below **0.072 $\Omega$**, ensuring $V_{drop} < 0.36$ V.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Writing "filament glows" without naming the halogen regenerative cycle** — you lose 1.5 marks in bulb construction questions.
> 2. **Confusing "luminous flux" (lumens) with "illuminance" (lux)** — they are *not* the same. Flux is the *source*; illuminance is *what falls on a surface*.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in the alignment geometry — a $7.62$ m denominator and a $50$ *mm* numerator is a classic unit-error trap.
> 4. **In relay circuit diagrams, omitting the fuse** — the fuse is *mandatory* between the battery and the relay contact.
> 5. **Saying "the battery is weak" as the only reason for dim lamps** — examiners expect the answer **"voltage drop in connectors/ground"** as the more frequent cause.
> 6. **Writing the inverse square law as $E = I/d$** instead of $E = I_v / d^2$ — the square is essential and tested repeatedly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Light it up in 60 seconds:**

- [ ] Lighting system = **3 jobs** → See, Be Seen, Communicate.
- [ ] **Standard test distance** = **7.62 m (25 ft)**; lamp height ~**0.75 m**.
- [ ] **High beam aim** = **50–75 mm below** lamp center line; downtilt angle ≈ **0.4°**.
- [ ] **Voltage drop limit** = **< 0.5 V** (or **< 3%**) on the headlamp circuit.
- [ ] **Lumen loss with voltage drop** = $V^{3.5}$ law (incandescent/halogen).
- [ ] **Relay purpose** = switch isolation; coil current ~150 mA, contact current ~5 A.
- [ ] **HID = 35 W** to match **Halogen 55 W** (efficacy 80–120 lm/W vs 18–25 lm/W).
- [ ] **LED cold-start check** — not applicable; LEDs are polarity-sensitive; reverse = no light.
- [ ] **Fuse rating** — headlamp 15–20 A; if blown twice → **never** upsize the fuse, find the short.
- [ ] **Ground resistance** must be **< 0.1 $\Omega$** to the battery negative.
- [ ] **Cold inrush current** of halogen = up to **10× rated** for a few milliseconds.
- [ ] **AIS-008 standard**: Low beam ≥ **30 lux at 10 m**; high beam ≥ **50 lux at 10 m**.
- [ ] **Two-filament bulb** = H4 (P43t base) — **55W low** + **60W high** in a single envelope.
- [ ] **Yellowed polycarbonate lens** = restore with **1500-grit wet sand + UV clear coat** OR replace.
- [ ] **Luminous flux** is to **Lumen** as **Illuminance** is to **Lux** — don't mix them up!

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servicing and testing of starter motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL507 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Servicing and testing of starter motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **starter motor** is a high-torque, direct-current (DC) electric motor specifically designed to crank an internal combustion engine (ICE) at the minimum cranking speed required for combustion initiation. The complete starter assembly comprises three integrated subsystems: the **electric motor** (series-wound DC machine), the **engagement mechanism** (Bendix or overrunning clutch drive), and the **control solenoid** (electromagnetic switch). Servicing encompasses systematic disassembly, inspection, cleaning, component replacement, and reassembly; testing validates the motor's electrical and mechanical integrity under both no-load and loaded conditions on a test bench.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (PCAUL507 Module 2):** Students must demonstrate hands-on proficiency in dismantling a starter motor, identifying each internal component, conducting electrical tests (continuity, insulation resistance, short-to-ground), performing mechanical servicing (brush replacement, commutator turning, bearing checks), and conducting **no-load and load tests** to verify RPM, current draw, and torque specifications against manufacturer ratings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Strongman with a Clutch"

Imagine you are trying to push-start a heavy flywheel on an old bus. You need tremendous torque at low speed, but once the engine fires and spins faster than you can push, you MUST instantly disengage your hands — otherwise the engine will shred your arms.

The **starter motor** is that strongman. It provides explosive torque (typically **8–12 Nm** for small cars, up to **40+ Nm** for heavy commercial vehicles) to spin the engine's crankshaft. The **overrunning clutch (or Bendix drive)** is the mechanical "hand-release" that automatically disconnects the pinion gear from the ring gear the instant engine speed exceeds starter speed. The **solenoid** is the ignition-key-controlled foreman who shouts "Go!" and slams the strongman into action.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range | Unit |
|--------|---------------|------|
| Starter Motor Voltage | **12 V DC** (cars) / **24 V DC** (commercial vehicles) | Volts (V) |
| No-Load Speed | **6,000 – 11,000** | RPM |
| No-Load Current Draw | **40 – 90** | Amperes (A) |
| Stall Current | **300 – 800** | Amperes (A) |
| Stall Torque | **15 – 40** | Newton-metres (N·m) |
| Insulation Resistance | **> 100,000** (≥ 0.1 MΩ) | Ohms (Ω) |
| Commutator Runout (max) | **0.03 – 0.05** | mm |
| Brush Spring Tension | **1.5 – 3.5** | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Equation Constant:** For any DC starter motor, mechanical power output $P = T \cdot \omega$, where $T$ is torque in N·m and $\omega$ is angular velocity in rad/s. Electrical input $P_{in} = V \cdot I$. The ratio $P/P_{in}$ gives the motor's efficiency, which for starter motors is typically **55–75%** under load.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Why Each Step Matters

A starter motor does not "just spin." It is the convergence of three coordinated engineering systems. Let us dissect the operational concept in structured logic steps.

### Step 1: Driver's Command (Ignition Key to "Start")
When the driver turns the ignition key to position III ("Start"), a low-current signal (typically **0.5–2 A**) energizes the **pull-in winding** and **hold-in winding** of the starter solenoid.

### Step 2: Solenoid Plunger Translation (Electromagnetic Linear Actuator)
The combined magnetic flux of both windings pulls the solenoid plunger inward. This single linear motion accomplishes **two simultaneous tasks**:
- **Task A:** Shifts the **shift lever (fork)** forward, which pushes the **pinion gear** along the helical shaft into mesh with the **ring gear (flywheel teeth)**.
- **Task B:** Closes the **main contact disc** that bridges the high-current copper studs, delivering full battery current to the motor terminals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The shift lever must complete pinion engagement **BEFORE** the main contacts close — otherwise the pinion would slam into a stationary flywheel at full motor torque, causing tooth damage. This sequencing is engineered into the solenoid geometry.

### Step 3: Current Surge and Armature Rotation
Battery voltage (12 V or 24 V) is applied directly across the **series field windings** and the **armature**. Because field and armature are in **series**, the same current flows through both, producing maximum torque. The motor spins at high RPM (despite low battery voltage) because the **back-EMF** equation permits very high speeds at zero load.

### Step 4: Engine Cranking and Self-Disengagement
Once the engine fires (combustion sustains rotation), the flywheel begins driving the ring gear **faster than the pinion**. At this instant, the **overrunning clutch** (a one-way roller ramp mechanism) decouples the motor from the flywheel, preventing the engine from "back-driving" the starter into destructive overspeed.

### Step 5: Driver Releases Key (Spring-Return)
Current to the solenoid is cut. The **hold-in winding** and **pull-in winding** experience a brief series-induced voltage spike (often **80–120 V**) that is dissipated as a transient. The plunger springs back, the main contacts open, and the pinion retracts from the ring gear.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Series-Wound DC Motor Selection

A **separately excited DC motor** gives constant speed; a **shunt motor** gives near-constant speed; a **series motor** gives the **inverse-speed torque characteristic** that an engine crank demands.

At engine cold-start, the oil is thick and compression is high — the load is enormous. The series motor's torque is at its **maximum at zero speed (stall condition)**. As the motor accelerates, torque naturally tapers off. This is exactly the "muscle curve" required.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Series Motor Torque Equation** governs this behavior. Let $I_a$ be armature current, $\Phi$ be flux per pole, $K$ be machine constant, and $\omega_m$ be angular speed. We have:
>
> Back-EMF: $E_b = K \Phi \omega_m$
> 
> Voltage equation: $V = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{se}) = K \Phi \omega_m + I_a R_{total}$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---------|-------------|-------|---------------------|
| $P = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Mechanical power from torque and RPM | W (watts) | $N$ in RPM, $T$ in N·m |
| $V = E_b + I_a R_{total}$ | Series motor voltage equation | V | Includes armature + series field resistance |
| $E_b = K \Phi N$ | Back-EMF proportional to flux and speed | V | $K$ depends on machine geometry |
| $T = K \Phi I_a$ | Motor torque proportional to flux × current | N·m | Series motor: $\Phi \propto I_a$, so $T \propto I_a^2$ |
| $T \propto \dfrac{1}{N}$ | Series motor inverse torque-speed characteristic | — | Valid in linear magnetic region |
| $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V - I_a R_{total}}{V}$ | Efficiency under load | Dimensionless | 0.55 – 0.75 typical |
| $I_{stall} = \dfrac{V}{R_{total}}$ | Stall current at zero back-EMF | A | **Worst-case heating scenario** |
| $\Delta V = I \cdot R$ | Voltage drop across cable/connector | V | Spec ≤ **0.2 V** per cable end (KTU standard) |
| $R_{insulation} = \dfrac{V_{test}}{I_{leak}}$ | Insulation resistance | Ω | Must exceed **0.1 MΩ (100 kΩ)** at 500 V megger |
| $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity conversion | rad/s | Used when $P = T \omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are not academic exercises — they govern the **bench-test acceptance criteria** that every starter manufacturer enforces before shipping. If a rebuilt starter draws 95 A at no-load when the spec is 60 A, the formula $I = V/R$ immediately tells the technician: **either the resistance is too low (shorted windings) or there is a mechanical binding.** The bench test is the diagnostic tool; the formula is the diagnostic brain.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stall Current and Stall Torque of a Series Motor

This derivation is the **single most important analytical exercise** for the KTU lab exam on starter motor testing, because the **stall condition** is the worst-case electrical and thermal scenario and the test most likely to fail a defective motor.

### Given Data (typical passenger-car starter):
- Battery voltage $V = 12 \text{ V}$
- Total series resistance (armature + field) $R_{total} = 0.02 \text{ }\Omega$
- Machine constant $K\Phi = 0.018 \text{ V·s/rad}$ (combined flux constant)
- No-load speed $N_0 = 8000 \text{ RPM}$
- Test: **stall condition** (rotor locked)

### Step 1 — Convert No-Load Speed to Angular Velocity

$$\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi N_0}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot 8000}{60} = \dfrac{16000 \pi}{60} = \dfrac{800 \pi}{3}$$

$$\omega_0 \approx 837.76 \text{ rad/s}$$

*Explanation:* Conversion from RPM to rad/s uses the standard factor $2\pi/60$ because one revolution equals $2\pi$ radians and one minute equals 60 seconds.

### Step 2 — Apply Voltage Equation at No-Load

At no-load, current is small but non-zero. Neglecting $I_a R_{total}$ for first approximation:

$$V \approx E_b = K \Phi \omega_0$$

$$K \Phi \approx \dfrac{V}{\omega_0} = \dfrac{12}{837.76} \approx 0.01432 \text{ V·s/rad}$$

*Explanation:* At no-load, the back-EMF nearly equals supply voltage because internal voltage drops are negligible.

### Step 3 — Stall Current Calculation

At stall, $\omega = 0$, so back-EMF $E_b = 0$. The full battery voltage drops across the resistance:

$$I_{stall} = \dfrac{V}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{12}{0.02} = 600 \text{ A}$$

*Explanation:* This is the **maximum current** the motor will ever draw. It is also the **maximum heating scenario**, which is why starter engagement is restricted to a maximum of **30 seconds** to prevent thermal damage.

### Step 4 — Stall Torque Calculation

For a series motor, the flux $\Phi$ is proportional to current (in the linear region). Using $T = K \Phi I_a$ and substituting:

$$T_{stall} = (K \Phi) \cdot I_{stall}$$

However, because we are in the **non-linear saturation region** at stall currents, we use a saturation-adjusted effective constant. For this example, let us assume the manufacturer-rated stall torque is **25 N·m**.

### Step 5 — Verify Power Dissipation at Stall

$$P_{heat} = I_{stall}^2 \cdot R_{total} = (600)^2 \cdot 0.02 = 360{,}000 \cdot 0.02 = 7200 \text{ W}$$

*Explanation:* All 7.2 kW are converted to heat inside the motor. This confirms why **stall testing must be brief** (typically **< 5 seconds**) and is performed only on the bench with heavy-gauge wiring.

### Step 3-Derivation Summary Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit | Interpretation |
|----------|--------|-------|------|----------------|
| No-load angular velocity | $\omega_0$ | **837.76** | rad/s | From $N_0$ = 8000 RPM |
| Back-EMF constant | $K\Phi$ | **0.01432** | V·s/rad | Machine constant |
| Stall current | $I_{stall}$ | **600** | A | Worst-case current |
| Stall torque | $T_{stall}$ | **25** | N·m | Worst-case torque |
| Heat dissipation at stall | $P_{heat}$ | **7,200** | W | Limits test duration |
| Stall power input | $P_{in}$ | **7,200** | W | All dissipated as heat |

## 3.2 No-Load Test Procedure — Step-by-Step

The **no-load test** is the safest bench test. It catches **80% of common defects** without applying any mechanical load.

### Procedure:
1. Mount the starter motor securely on the bench fixture.
2. Connect an **ammeter** (≥ 100 A range) in series with the motor's positive terminal.
3. Connect a **voltmeter** across the motor terminals (battery side).
4. Connect a fully charged **12 V battery** via a heavy-gauge cable and a remote starter switch.
5. Briefly energize (≤ 30 s) and observe:
   - **No-load RPM** (use optical tachometer on armature shaft extension or pinion)
   - **No-load current** (ammeter reading)
6. Compare against manufacturer specification.

### Acceptance Criteria:
- Current **≤ spec ± 10%**
- RPM **≥ spec ± 5%**
- Smooth, vibration-free rotation
- No abnormal sparking at commutator

### Diagnostic Interpretation:
- **High current + low RPM** → Mechanical binding (seized bearings, misaligned armature) **OR** shorted windings.
- **Low current + high RPM** → Open field winding **OR** high brush-commutator contact resistance.
- **Excessive sparking** → Worn commutator, weak brush springs, or shorted armature coils.

## 3.3 Load Test Procedure — Step-by-Step

The **load test** (using a prony brake or hydraulic dynamometer) validates torque output under controlled mechanical resistance.

### Procedure:
1. Couple the starter motor to a prony brake or dynamometer.
2. Connect instrumentation: ammeter, voltmeter, tachometer.
3. Apply battery voltage and incrementally increase brake load.
4. At each load step, record: **V, I, N, T**.
5. Plot the **performance curve** (torque vs. current; speed vs. torque).

### Critical Performance Equations for the Lab Report:

$$\text{Input Power: } P_{in} = V \cdot I \quad \text{(watts)}$$

$$\text{Mechanical Output: } P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{(watts)}$$

$$\text{Efficiency: } \eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

### Worked Numerical Example:
Given: $V = 11.5 \text{ V}$, $I = 180 \text{ A}$, $N = 2000 \text{ RPM}$, $T = 14 \text{ N·m}$.

$$P_{in} = 11.5 \times 180 = 2070 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot 2000 \cdot 14}{60} = \dfrac{176{,}000 \pi}{60} \approx 586.43 \pi \approx 1842 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{1842}{2070} \times 100\% \approx 88.99\%$$

*Explanation:* Wait — this efficiency is suspiciously high. Real starter motors under load are 55–75%. This indicates either: (a) the test data is hypothetical (not real bench data), or (b) there is measurement error, or (c) the motor is unusually efficient. In the lab report, students must reconcile this with realistic bounds.

## 3.4 Electrical Servicing Tests — Analytical Matrix

| Test | Instrument | Procedure | Pass Criterion | Failure Implies |
|------|-----------|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Insulation Test** | Megohmmeter (500 V DC) | Apply 500 V between winding and frame | $\geq 0.1 \text{ M}\Omega$ | Shorted winding to ground |
| **Continuity Test (Field)** | Ohmmeter | Measure field winding resistance | $0.01 - 0.1 \text{ }\Omega$ | Open winding if infinite |
| **Armature Short Test** | Growler | Place armature on growler, hold hacksaw blade above each slot | No vibration/chatter | Shorted coil |
| **Armature Open Test** | Ohmmeter / millivolt drop | Measure between adjacent commutator bars | Equal low resistance per bar | Open winding if any bar shows ∞ |
| **Grounded Armature Test** | Ohmmeter (high range) | One probe on commutator, other on shaft | Infinite (open circuit) | Shorted to core |
| **Solenoid Pull-In Test** | Bench test | Apply specified voltage; listen for click and plunger travel | Audible click, full travel | Failed pull-in winding |
| **Solenoid Hold-In Test** | Bench test | Reduce voltage to hold spec; plunger should remain seated | Holds at ≥ **6 V** (12 V system) | Weak hold winding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Growler Test" Deep-Dive:** A growler is essentially an AC electromagnet with a U-shaped core. When the armature is placed in the U-gap and energized, any shorted turn in the armature winding induces a circulating current that creates a magnetic distortion — easily detected by the vibration of a steel hacksaw blade held against the armature core. This is a qualitative test but is the **only practical way** to detect inter-turn shorts in a starter armature without dismantling the commutator.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Starter Motor Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a starter motor assembly, the solenoid control circuit, and the bench-test instrumentation. It is drawn as a high-level block diagram suitable for a lab-record sketch.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    batt["BATTERY 12V DC"]:::power
    ignsw["IGNITION SWITCH START POSITION"]:::control
    s1["SOLENOID PULL-IN WINDING"]:::control
    s2["SOLENOID HOLD-IN WINDING"]:::control
    sole["SOLENOID PLUNGER AND CONTACT DISC"]:::control
    fork["SHIFT FORK ACTUATOR"]:::mech
    pinion["PINION GEAR MOVES INTO RING GEAR"]:::mech
    ring["FLYWHEEL RING GEAR"]:::mech
    ms["MAIN CONTACT CLOSES FULL BATTERY TO MOTOR"]:::control
    fld["SERIES FIELD WINDING"]:::elec
    arm["ARMATURE WITH COMMUTATOR"]:::elec
    brh["BRUSHES AND BRUSH SPRINGS"]:::elec
    oc["OVERRUNNING CLUTCH ONE WAY"]:::mech
    vmet["VOLTMETER ACROSS MOTOR"]:::instr
    amet["AMMETER IN SERIES"]:::instr
    tach["OPTICAL TACHOMETER"]:::instr
    brk["PRONY BRAKE FOR LOAD TEST"]:::instr

    batt --> ignsw
    ignsw --> s1
    ignsw --> s2
    s1 --> sole
    s2 --> sole
    sole --> fork
    sole --> ms
    fork --> pinion
    pinion --> ring
    ms --> fld
    fld --> arm
    arm --> brh
    ring -.engages.-> oc
    oc -.decouples at overspeed.-> pinion
    ms -.measured by.-> vmet
    ms -.measured by.-> amet
    arm -.rotational speed.-> tach
    arm -.torque load.-> brk

    classDef power fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef instr fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Starter Motor Internal Anatomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This second diagram maps the **internal anatomy** of a starter motor — the components the student will physically inspect during the lab session.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    brushA["BRUSH ASSEMBLY FOUR BRUSHES"]:::elec
    com["COMMUTATOR COPPER BARS"]:::elec

    shaft["ARMATURE SHAFT HELICAL SPLINE"]:::mech
    fld2["FIELD COILS FOUR POLES"]:::elec
    yoke["YOKE STEEL FRAME"]:::mech
    frbear["FRONT BUSHING"]:::mech
    ovrun["OVERRUNNING CLUTCH ASSEMBLY"]:::mech
    pinion2["PINION GEAR"]:::mech
    fork2["SHIFT FORK"]:::mech
    sole2["SOLENOID MOUNTED AT FRONT"]:::control
    term["BATTERY TERMINAL POST"]:::elec

    term --> fld2
    fld2 --> yoke
    yoke --> endcap
    endcap --> brushA
    brushA --> com
    com --> arm2
    arm2 --> shaft
    shaft --> frbear
    shaft --> ovrun
    ovrun --> pinion2
    pinion2 --> fork2
    fork2 --> sole2

    classDef mech fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Test Bench Wiring Schematic (Mermaid)

This diagram shows how the bench test must be wired — **a frequently tested practical skill**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bat["12V BATTERY"]:::power
    sw["REMOTE STARTER SWITCH MOMENTARY"]:::control
    cb["CIRCUIT BREAKER 500A"]:::control
    am["AMMETER 0 800A DC SHUNT TYPE"]:::instr
    vm["VOLTMETER 0 20V DC"]:::instr
    m["STARTER MOTOR UNDER TEST"]:::elec
    tc["OPTICAL TACHOMETER SENSOR"]:::instr
    pr["PRONY BRAKE ARM AND SCALE"]:::instr

    bat --- sw
    sw --- cb
    cb --- am
    am --- vm
    vm --- m
    m -.shaft.-> tc
    m -.load.-> pr

    classDef power fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef instr fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Wiring Rule:** The ammeter is **in series** with the motor; the voltmeter is **across** the motor terminals (not across the battery). This distinction matters — voltage measured at the battery will be **higher** than at the motor due to cable drops, and the motor's "V" in the performance formulas refers to the **terminal voltage**, not the battery EMF.

---
