
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define the term "winding factor" in the context of an alternator stator winding. State its typical range for a distributed, short-pitched winding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The winding factor $K_w$ is the product of the **distribution factor ($K_d$)** and the **pitch factor ($K_p$)**:
$$K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$$
It accounts for the reduction in induced EMF caused by (a) distributing the coil sides across multiple slots per pole per phase, and (b) shortening the coil pitch from the full 180° electrical. For a typical 36-slot, 12-pole, 5/6-pitched automotive alternator stator, $K_w$ lies in the range **0.90 to 0.96**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**List any three differences between lap winding and wave winding as applied to DC starter motor armatures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Lap Winding | Wave Winding |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = P$ (parallel paths = poles) | $A = 2$ always |
| 2 | Used for high-current, low-voltage machines (e.g., starter motors) | Used for high-voltage, low-current machines |
| 3 | Equalizer rings required to equalize parallel path currents | No equalizer rings required |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions with Internal Choice (KTU ESE Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [Module 2 / KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of the field coil winding of a **series-wound DC starter motor**. List the materials used and justify the choice of wire gauge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, **wave-wound DC starter motor** armature has 540 conductors and runs at 2500 rpm with a flux per pole of 30 mWb. Calculate the generated EMF. If the armature is reconnected as **lap-wound**, what will be the new generated EMF at the same speed and flux? Comment on the practical implications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Field coil of series-wound DC starter motor (7 marks):**

- The field coil consists of a few turns of **heavy-gauge enameled copper wire (SWG 14 to 16)** wound on a **bobbin-shaped former**.
- The former is mounted on each of the **4 pole shoes** fastened inside the starter motor **yoke (steel housing)**.
- Because the field winding is in **series with the armature**, the full armature current (200–400 A) flows through it. Hence, wire gauge must be large to minimize I²R losses and prevent overheating.
- **Insulation class H (180 °C)** is used to withstand the high temperatures generated during prolonged cranking.
- The 4 coils are connected in **series aiding** (alternating polarity N-S-N-S) to produce alternate North and South poles.
- **Materials used:**
  - Enameled copper wire (ECW) Grade 2
  - Class H insulating varnish
  - Cotton / Nomex slot insulation
  - Pole shoe: Soft magnetic steel

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given (wave wound):**
- $P = 4$, $Z = 540$, $N = 2500$ rpm, $\Phi = 30 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb, $A = 2$ (wave)

**Generated EMF (wave):**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-3} \times 540 \times 2500 \times 4}{60 \times 2}$$

Numerator: $30 \times 10^{-3} \times 540 \times 2500 \times 4 = 162{,}000$
Denominator: $60 \times 2 = 120$

$$\boxed{E_{g,\text{wave}} = \dfrac{162{,}000}{120} = 1350 \text{ V}}$$

**If reconnected as lap-wound** ($A = P = 4$):

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-3} \times 540 \times 2500 \times 4}{60 \times 4}$$

$$\boxed{E_{g,\text{lap}} = \dfrac{162{,}000}{240} = 675 \text{ V}}$$

**Comment:** Halving the parallel paths from 2 to 4 reduces the EMF to half (1350 V → 675 V), but **doubles the current capacity**. For a starter motor needing high cranking torque (current driven), **lap winding is the practical choice** despite lower EMF — the battery supplies the additional voltage drop.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a)** Describe, with the help of a block diagram, the **three-phase stator winding** of a typical 12-pole automotive alternator. Explain the role of the **winding factor $K_w$** and derive an expression for it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 12-pole alternator has 36 stator slots. The winding is distributed with **3 slots per pole per phase** and the coil is **short-pitched by 1 slot** (i.e., 5/6 pitch). Calculate the distribution factor, pitch factor, and winding factor. Take the alternator frequency as 50 Hz and flux per pole as 0.05 Wb. Determine the phase EMF if each phase has 40 turns. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block diagram & winding factor derivation (7 marks):**

**Block diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.2 above (Stator with 3 phases, 12 slots each, star-connected).

**Derivation of Winding Factor:**

The induced EMF per phase in a distributed, short-pitched winding is reduced compared to a concentrated full-pitch winding by two factors:

1. **Distribution factor** $K_d$ — accounts for the fact that EMF vectors in different slots of the same phase are not in phase but displaced by the slot angle $\beta$.

$$K_d = \dfrac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \cdot \sin(\beta/2)}$$

where $m$ = number of slots per pole per phase and $\beta = 180°/m$ (electrical).

2. **Pitch factor** $K_p$ — accounts for the chording of the coil.

$$K_p = \cos(\alpha/2)$$

where $\alpha$ is the chording angle in electrical degrees.

The total reduction is the product:

$$\boxed{K_w = K_d \cdot K_p}$$

For an automotive alternator, $K_w$ is typically **0.92 to 0.96**.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- 3-phase, 12-pole, 36 slots
- 3 slots per pole per phase → $m = 3$
- Short-pitched by 1 slot → pitch = 5/6
- $f = 50$ Hz, $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb, $N_{\text{ph}} = 40$ turns

**Step 1: Calculate slot angle $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \dfrac{180°}{m} = \dfrac{180°}{3} = 60° \text{ (electrical)}$$

**Step 2: Distribution factor $K_d$:**

$$K_d = \dfrac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \cdot \sin(\beta/2)} = \dfrac{\sin(3 \times 60°/2)}{3 \cdot \sin(60°/2)} = \dfrac{\sin(90°)}{3 \cdot \sin(30°)}$$

$$K_d = \dfrac{1}{3 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{1}{1.5} = 0.6667$$

**Step 3: Pitch factor $K_p$:**

The coil is short-pitched by 1 slot out of 6 total slots per pole (= 180°), so the chording angle is:

$$\alpha = 60° \times 1 = 60°$$

$$K_p = \cos(\alpha/2) = \cos(30°) = 0.8660$$

**Step 4: Winding factor $K_w$:**

$$K_w = K_d \cdot K_p = 0.6667 \times 0.8660 = 0.5773$$

**Step 5: Phase EMF:**

$$E_{\text{ph}} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_{\text{ph}} \cdot \Phi \cdot K_w$$

$$E_{\text{ph}} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 40 \times 0.05 \times 0.5773$$

$$E_{\text{ph}} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 40 \times 0.05 \times 0.5773 = 256.5 \text{ V (RMS per phase)}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{ph}} \approx 256.5 \text{ V (RMS)}}$$

**Line voltage (assuming star connection):**
$$E_{\text{line}} = \sqrt{3} \times E_{\text{ph}} = 1.732 \times 256.5 \approx 444.3 \text{ V (line-to-line RMS)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students frequently **forget the winding factor** $K_w$ in the phase EMF equation, leading to an inflated answer (~444 V). Always multiply by $K_w$.
> 2. Confusing **electrical degrees with mechanical degrees**. In a 12-pole machine, the electrical-to-mechanical ratio is **6:1** (electrical degrees = mechanical degrees × P/2).
> 3. Mixing up the **winding factor formula** — do not multiply $K_d$ and $K_p$ into the slot angle by mistake.
> 4. **Star vs. Delta connection**: The factor of $\sqrt{3}$ only applies for star (Y) connection.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **EMF equation for DC machine**: $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — memorize with the *Z NAP / 60* mnemonic.
- ✅ **EMF equation for AC alternator**: $E_{\text{ph}} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{\text{ph}} \, \Phi \, K_w$ — the **4.44** is the peak-to-RMS form factor for sinusoids.
- ✅ **Wave winding**: $A = 2$ (regardless of poles) — high voltage, low current.
- ✅ **Lap winding**: $A = P$ — high current, low voltage (used in starter motors).
- ✅ **Frequency formula**: $f = \dfrac{PN}{120}$ — poles are always even.
- ✅ **Winding factor** $K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$ — always **less than 1** for distributed/short-pitched windings.
- ✅ **Slot fill factor** target: **0.45 to 0.65** for round wire; up to 0.75 for rectangular/hairpin wire.
- ✅ **Starter motor field coils**: Heavy-gauge wire (SWG 14–16), **Class H insulation**, connected in **series aiding**.
- ✅ **Alternator stator**: Three-phase, Y-connected, **distributed lap winding**, 36 slots, 12 poles typical.
- ✅ **Rotor field coil**: Fed via **slip rings** with DC of 3–5 A; voltage regulator controls field current for output regulation (13.8–14.4 V).
- ✅ **Wire material**: **Enameled Copper Wire (ECW)**; resistivity **1.724 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** at 20 °C.
- ✅ **Permeability of free space** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m — used in all magnetic circuit calculations.
- ✅ **Ampère's Law** governs coil magnetic behavior: $\oint H \, dl = NI$.
- ✅ **Slot insulation**: Use **Nomex 410** paper (Class H, 0.25 mm typical thickness) between conductors and core.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked about coil winding in KTU lab viva, mention the **slot fill factor, insulation class, and winding factor $K_w$** — these three keywords fetch easy 1–2 marks and demonstrate deep understanding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault detection testing of wiring harness in automobiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL507 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Fault detection testing of wiring harness in automobiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Fault Detection Testing of Wiring Harness in Automobiles

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **wiring harness** in an automobile is a pre-assembled set of wires, terminals, connectors, protective sleeves, fuses, and relays that systematically route electrical power and signal currents between various electrical and electronic components such as the battery, alternator, starter motor, ignition system, ECU, sensors, lighting circuits, infotainment modules, and body control units.

**Fault detection testing of a wiring harness** is the systematic diagnostic procedure employed to identify, locate, and characterize electrical defects within the harness assembly. These defects typically manifest as **open circuits**, **short circuits** (to ground or between conductors), **high-resistance joints**, **insulation degradation**, **corroded terminals**, and **intermittent connection failures**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The experiment is aligned with **Course Outcome CO2** of PCAUL507 — *"Diagnose common electrical faults in automotive systems using standard testing instruments."* Students are expected to demonstrate hands-on competence in identifying at least three distinct fault types in a lab-fabricated or actual vehicle harness.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine the **human nervous system**. Your brain sends signals through nerves to control muscles, organs, and senses. If a nerve gets pinched (high resistance), cut (open circuit), or shorted to another nerve (short circuit), the body malfunctions. The **automotive wiring harness is the nervous system of a car**, and **fault detection testing is the neurological examination** a doctor performs — probing each pathway with controlled stimuli to verify that the signal travels correctly from source to destination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Stat:** Modern mid-segment passenger cars contain between **1.5 km to 2.5 km** of wiring with over **600 individual connections**. A single intermittent fault in a CAN-bus twisted pair can paralyze the entire vehicle network.

### 1.3 Standard Test Parameters

The fault detection tests rely on well-defined electrical quantities:

- **Nominal system voltage:** 12 V DC (passenger cars) or 24 V DC (commercial/heavy vehicles)
- **Acceptable voltage drop across a loaded circuit:** ≤ **0.5 V** for power circuits; ≤ **0.1 V** for signal circuits
- **Minimum insulation resistance:** ≥ **100 MΩ** at 500 V DC (per IS 2464 / IEC 60204)
- **Maximum allowable loop resistance for ground return:** ≤ **0.1 Ω** for engine cranking circuits
- **Continuity test current:** typically **1 mA to 5 mA** from the multimeter

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Table

### 2.1 Classification of Wiring Harness Faults

Automotive wiring harness faults are categorized into the following principal classes:

**A. Open Circuit Faults**
- Complete discontinuity in the conductor
- Causes: broken wire strands, detached crimp terminal, pulled-out connector pin, blown fuse
- Symptom: downstream component receives zero voltage

**B. Short-to-Ground Faults**
- Unintended conductive path between a live conductor and chassis ground
- Causes: chafed insulation contacting metal body, water ingress in connectors, rodent damage
- Symptom: blown fuse, tripped protection device, heat generation

**C. Short-to-Power Faults**
- Unintended connection between a signal wire and a constant 12 V/24 V source
- Causes: pinched wires, misrouted harness post-repair
- Symptom: false triggering of modules, back-fed circuits

**D. High-Resistance Faults**
- Excessive resistance at joints, splices, or corroded terminals
- Causes: oxidation (green/white deposits on copper), loose crimps, under-torqued ground bolts
- Symptom: dim lights, slow motors, erroneous sensor readings, voltage drop

**E. Intermittent Faults**
- Fault appears and disappears based on vibration, temperature, or humidity
- Causes: cracked solder joints, loose multi-pin connectors, marginal crimps
- Symptom: erratic behavior, hard-to-reproduce warnings

> [!TIP]
> **Why these faults occur:** The harness is exposed to extreme thermal cycling (−40 °C to +125 °C), engine vibration (5–25 g), chemical attack (battery acid, road salt, hydraulic fluid), and mechanical abrasion. Over a typical 10-year service life, cumulative stress degrades every component.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Fault Signatures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **single most important quick-reference** for the KTU practical exam viva and record writing. Memorize the symptom-to-fault mapping.

| Fault Type | Primary Symptom | Quick Test Method | Expected Healthy Reading | Typical Defective Reading |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open circuit | Component completely dead | Continuity (Ω) with beep | 0–1 Ω (with load) | OL (over-limit) |
| Short to ground | Repeated fuse blowing | Continuity from wire to chassis | OL | 0–5 Ω |
| Short to power | Component ON when it should be OFF | Voltage at load terminal with switch OFF | 0 V | 12 V or 24 V |
| High resistance | Dim lights / weak motor | Voltage drop under load | ≤ 0.5 V drop | > 1.0 V drop |
| Intermittent | Erratic operation | Wiggle test + thermal camera | Stable | Fluctuating |
| Corroded terminal | Intermittent power loss | Visual + milliohm test | ≤ 50 mΩ | > 500 mΩ |
| Insulation failure | Leakage current / phantom drain | Megger at 500 V DC | ≥ 100 MΩ | < 1 MΩ |
| Connector pin damage | Single function failure | Pin-by-pin continuity | All pass | One pin open |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU practical exam, if asked to identify a fault, always quote the **"expected healthy reading" vs the "actual reading observed"** — this is the examiner's checklist for full marks.

### 2.3 Testing Instruments and Their Roles

- **Digital Multimeter (DMM):** Measures voltage (V), current (A), and resistance (Ω). The workhorse instrument.
- **Automotive Test Lamp (12 V / 24 V bulb with probe):** Quick go/no-go check for power and ground.
- **Insulation Tester (Megger / Megohmmeter):** Applies a controlled high DC voltage (typically 500 V) to measure insulation resistance in MΩ.
- **Self-Powered Test Buzzer / Continuity Tester:** Emits audible tone when continuity exists.
- **Automotive Scan Tool (OBD-II):** Reads Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs) that pinpoint harness-related faults.
- **Wiring Diagram / Service Manual:** The roadmap. Without it, fault detection is guesswork.
- **Heat gun / Freeze spray:** Used to induce thermal cycling during intermittent fault tracing.
- **Lab Scope / Oscilloscope:** For CAN-bus and sensor signal integrity verification.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fault detection testing is not merely a workshop exercise — it is a **mandatory quality gate** in the automotive industry. Every OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) performs:

1. **100% End-of-Line (EOL) continuity testing** on every harness produced.
2. **Hi-pot (high potential) dielectric withstand testing** to verify insulation.
3. **Sample-based vibration and thermal cycling tests** for reliability validation.
4. **Field-failure root-cause analysis** using the 5-Why and Fishbone methods.

A trained automotive engineer who can systematically isolate a harness fault saves **billions of rupees annually** in warranty costs across the industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Testing Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Pre-Test Safety and Preparation (Mandatory)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students skip the safety steps and start probing live circuits. This is an automatic deduction of marks and a real-world safety hazard. Always begin the record with a safety preamble.

1. **Disconnect the battery negative terminal** — isolate the vehicle's energy source.
2. Wait **at least 60 seconds** for capacitors in airbag and ECU modules to discharge.
3. Wear **insulated gloves** and **safety glasses**.
4. Verify that the **ignition switch is OFF** and all accessories are switched off.
5. Gather the **vehicle-specific wiring diagram** for the circuit under test.
6. Inspect the **fuse box** for the relevant circuit — replace any blown fuse with one of the **exact rated value** (never use a higher-rated fuse as a "fix").

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Fault Detection Procedure (The KTU Standard Protocol)

The following is the **prescribed sequence** for fault detection on an automotive wiring harness. Each step builds upon the previous one and the logic is non-negotiable.

**Step 1 — Visual and Tactile Inspection**

Begin with a naked-eye and hands-on survey. This catches **40–50%** of harness faults without any instrument.

- Look for **chafed insulation**, **melted sections**, **cracked boots**, and **discoloration** (indicating overheating).
- Feel for **stiffness** in flexible cable sections (indicating insulation aging).
- Gently tug each connector to confirm **positive locking** (audible click).
- Look for **green/white powder** on copper terminals — this is **copper oxidation** or **electrolytic corrosion**.
- Check for **rodent damage** (gnaw marks, nest material inside harness channels).

**Step 2 — Continuity Test (Open Circuit Detection)**

Set the DMM to the **continuity (beep) mode** or **lowest resistance range (200 Ω)**.

- Connect one probe to the **source-side pin** of the harness and the other to the **destination-side pin**.
- A healthy conductor shows a **reading of 0.0 to 1.0 Ω** and the multimeter beeps.
- An open circuit shows **OL (over-limit)** or infinite resistance — no beep.

Mathematical interpretation:

$$R_{measured} = R_{wire} + R_{contact,1} + R_{contact,2}$$

For a healthy 2-meter length of 18 AWG copper wire (cross-section ≈ 0.823 mm²), the theoretical resistance is:

$$R_{wire} = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A} = \frac{1.724 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 2}{0.823 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.0419 \, \Omega$$

> Adding the two contact resistances (each 10–50 mΩ in a healthy crimp), the total expected reading is **0.06 Ω to 0.15 Ω**. A reading above **0.5 Ω** is suspect and indicates a marginal crimp or corroded terminal.

**Step 3 — Short-to-Ground Test**

With the **battery still disconnected** and the **load removed**, set the DMM to **resistance mode (20 kΩ or higher range)**.

- Place one probe on the **suspect conductor terminal** and the other on a known **clean chassis ground** (bare metal, paint-free).
- A healthy insulated conductor reads **OL**.
- A short-to-ground reads **0 Ω to a few ohms** — the lower the resistance, the more direct (and severe) the short.

**Step 4 — Voltage Drop Test (High-Resistance Fault Detection)**

This is the **single most useful test** for loaded circuits. Performed with the circuit **energized and the load operating**.

- Place the DMM in **DC voltage mode**.
- Measure voltage from the **source side of the wire (at the battery/fuse end)** to the **load side of the wire (at the component terminal)**.
- Both probes must contact **the same conductor**, with the circuit drawing normal current.

The voltage drop equation:

$$V_{drop} = I_{load} \times R_{fault}$$

For a headlamp drawing 5 A through a faulty ground strap with 0.5 Ω of corrosion resistance:

$$V_{drop} = 5 \times 0.5 = 2.5 \, V$$

The lamp receives only **12 − 2.5 = 9.5 V** instead of the nominal 12 V, and the headlight runs dim — a classic high-resistance ground fault.

**Acceptable voltage drop limits** (industry standard, KTU may quote these):

$$V_{drop,power} \leq 0.5 \, V \quad \text{(per conductor segment in a power circuit)}$$

$$V_{drop,signal} \leq 0.1 \, V \quad \text{(sensor, reference, and ECU signal lines)}$$

**Step 5 — Insulation Resistance Test (Megger Test)**

Performed using a **500 V DC megohmmeter**. This stresses the insulation far beyond its normal operating voltage to reveal weak spots.

- Disconnect **both ends** of the conductor from the circuit.
- Connect the Megger lead to the conductor and the other lead to chassis ground (or to an adjacent conductor for inter-conductor insulation check).
- Apply the test voltage for **1 minute** and read the resistance.

**Acceptance criterion:**

$$R_{insulation} \geq 100 \, M\Omega$$

A reading below **1 MΩ** indicates severe insulation breakdown and the harness must be replaced or the damaged section repaired with proper adhesive-lined heat-shrink butt connectors.

**Step 6 — Intermittent Fault Tracing (The Wiggle Test)**

- With the circuit energized and the load operating, **physically wiggle** connectors and harness sections while monitoring the DMM.
- A healthy connection produces a **stable reading**.
- An intermittent connection produces **momentary spikes or dropouts** in voltage/current.
- Use **freeze spray** to cool suspect components and a **heat gun** to warm them — the fault often reveals itself under thermal stress.

> [!TIP]
> **For CAN-bus networks specifically**, a faulty termination resistor (should be 60 Ω total — two 120 Ω in parallel at the ends of the bus) is a frequent intermittent fault. Always measure across the CAN-H and CAN-L pins with the bus powered down.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Test for the Symptom

| Observed Symptom | First Test to Perform | Second Test if Inconclusive | Likely Root Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| Component completely dead | Continuity test | Voltage at supply pin with key ON | Open circuit / blown fuse |
| Fuse blows repeatedly | Short-to-ground test | Visual inspection of harness | Chafed wire to chassis |
| Light is dim | Voltage drop test | Ground strap continuity check | High resistance at ground |
| ECU sets CAN-bus DTC | Resistance across CAN-H to CAN-L | Scope waveform inspection | Termination resistor / twisted pair damage |
| Erratic sensor reading | Voltage drop on sensor supply | Wiggle test of connector | Loose pin / corroded contact |
| Battery drains overnight | Insulation resistance (parasitic draw) | Fuse-by-fuse current measurement | Insulation leakage / stuck relay |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Fault Detection Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete decision flow a technician follows during wiring harness fault detection, as prescribed for the KTU lab record and practical examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Symptom Reported] --> B[Step 1 Visual Inspection]
    B --> C{Damage Visible?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Repair or Replace Harness Section]
    C -- No --> E[Step 2 Continuity Test]
    E --> F{Reads OL?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Step 3 Short to Ground Test]
    G --> H{Reads Low Resistance?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Locate and Repair Short]
    H -- No --> J[Step 6 Wiggle Test for Intermittent]
    F -- No --> K[Step 4 Voltage Drop Test]
    K --> L{Drop Exceeds 0.5 V?}
    L -- Yes --> M[High Resistance Fault Identified]
    M --> N[Clean or Replace Terminal]
    L -- No --> O[Step 5 Megger Insulation Test]
    O --> P{Resistance Below 100 MOhm?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Insulation Failure]
    Q --> R[Replace Harness or Splice Repair]
    P -- No --> S[All Tests Pass - Check Load Component]
    D --> Z[End: Document in Service Record]
    I --> Z
    J --> Z
    N --> Z
    R --> Z
    S --> Z
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Typical Automotive Wiring Harness Subsystems

This diagram illustrates the major sub-harnesses that the technician must be familiar with before testing. Each sub-harness has its own characteristic fault profile.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[Battery 12V] --> MAIN[Main Power Cable]
    MAIN --> FUSE[Main Fuse Box]
    FUSE --> ENG[Engine Harness]
    FUSE --> BODY[Body Harness]
    FUSE --> DASH[Dashboard Harness]
    FUSE --> CHAS[Chassis Harness]
    ENG --> ECU[Engine ECU and Sensors]
    ENG --> STARTER[Starter Motor Circuit]
    ENG --> ALT[Alternator Output]
    BODY --> LIGHT[Lighting Circuits]
    BODY --> HORN[Horn and Wiper]
    DASH --> INFOTAIN[Infotainment and Cluster]
    CHAS --> GROUND[Chassis Ground Network]
    GROUND --> BAT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ground network** (bottom of the diagram) is the most frequently overlooked source of faults. In modern vehicles, the battery negative connects to the chassis at one or more points, and the engine block, body panels, and ECU each have their own ground straps. A single loose ground bolt can mimic multiple component failures.

### 4.3 Schematic — Voltage Drop Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BATT_POS[Battery Positive] --> F1[Fuse] --> SW[Switch] --> L1[Load Component]
    L1 --> GND_POINT[Ground Connection Point]
    GND_POINT --> BATT_NEG[Battery Negative]
    V1[DMM Probe 1 at Fuse Output]
    V2[DMM Probe 2 at Load Input]
    V1 -.Voltage Drop Measurement.-> V2
```

**Interpretation:** The DMM probes contact the **same conductor** at two points — one upstream of the load, one at the load terminal. The reading directly indicates the loss in the wire/connector path. In a healthy circuit, this voltage is near zero. Any reading above the KTU acceptance limit (0.5 V for power, 0.1 V for signal) confirms a high-resistance fault in that segment.

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Correct Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Identify Symptom] --> Q1{Is the circuit energized?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2[Use DMM Continuity Mode]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q3{Can the load be operated safely?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4[Use DMM Voltage Drop Mode]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5[De-energize then use DMM Resistance Mode]
    Q2 --> END[Test Recorded]
    Q4 --> END
    Q5 --> END
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
*List any three common types of faults encountered in an automotive wiring harness and state the test instrument used to identify each.*

**Model Answer (for record):**

The three common types of wiring harness faults and the corresponding instruments are:

1. **Open circuit** — identified using a **Digital Multimeter (DMM) in continuity mode** (expected healthy reading: 0–1 Ω; defective: OL).
2. **Short to ground** — identified using a **Digital Multimeter in resistance mode** by measuring between the suspect conductor and chassis (expected healthy: OL; defective: low resistance).
3. **High resistance at a joint** — identified using a **voltage drop test** with the DMM in DC volt mode, performed under load (expected healthy drop: ≤ 0.5 V for power circuits).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Internal Assessment Pattern]**
*What is the acceptable minimum insulation resistance value for an automotive wiring harness, and what instrument is used to measure it?*

**Model Answer (for record):**

The acceptable minimum insulation resistance for an automotive wiring harness is **100 MΩ (mega-ohms)** when measured at **500 V DC**, as per IS 2464 / IEC 60204 standards. The instrument used is a **megohmmeter (Megger)** or insulation tester, which applies a controlled high DC voltage to stress the insulation and reveal weak spots that would not be detected at normal operating voltages.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Mark Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure to perform a **voltage drop test** on an automotive headlamp circuit. Mention the acceptable voltage drop limit and state what conclusion you draw if the measured value exceeds the limit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure** to perform a **continuity test and a short-to-ground test** on a tail-lamp wiring harness using a digital multimeter. Include the expected healthy readings. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Aim:** To measure the voltage drop across the headlamp power feed wire and ground return path to detect high-resistance faults.

**Apparatus Required:** Digital Multimeter (DMM), vehicle with serviceable headlamp circuit, wiring diagram, insulated gloves, safety glasses.

**Theory:**

The voltage drop test is performed **with the circuit energized and the load operating** (headlamps switched ON). The DMM is set to **DC voltage mode (20 V range)**. The two probes are placed on **the same conductor at two different points** — typically one probe at the fuse box output (source end) and the other at the headlamp connector terminal (load end). The reading represents the **electrical loss in the wire and connector joints** between those two points.

**Procedure:**

1. Switch the headlamps ON. Allow them to stabilize for 60 seconds.
2. Set the DMM to DC Volts, 20 V range.
3. Place the **red (positive) probe** on the fuse output terminal feeding the headlamp.
4. Place the **black (negative) probe** on the headlamp bulb socket's power pin.
5. Read and record the voltage displayed on the DMM.
6. Repeat the measurement on the **ground return path** — red probe on the bulb's ground pin, black probe on a clean chassis ground point near the headlamp.
7. Switch OFF the headlamps and record both values in the observation table.

**Acceptance Criteria and Conclusion:**

$$V_{drop,power} \leq 0.5 \, V \quad \text{per conductor segment}$$

$$V_{drop,ground} \leq 0.1 \, V \quad \text{per ground strap}$$

If the measured value exceeds **0.5 V** on the power feed or **0.1 V** on the ground path, the conclusion is that a **high-resistance fault** exists in that segment. The most common causes are **corroded terminal contacts**, **loose crimps**, or an **undersized ground strap**. The corrective action is to **clean the contacts with a contact cleaner spray**, **re-crimp the terminal**, or **replace the ground strap**.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Continuity Test Procedure:**

1. **Disconnect the battery negative terminal** to de-energize the entire vehicle.
2. Remove the tail-lamp bulb to isolate the load.
3. Set the DMM to **continuity (beep) mode** or the **200 Ω resistance range**.
4. Identify the **power feed pin** and the **ground pin** on the tail-lamp connector using the wiring diagram.
5. Place one probe on the **fuse box end** of the power feed wire and the other on the **lamp connector end** of the same wire.
6. **Healthy reading:** 0.0 to 1.0 Ω with an audible beep. This confirms wire continuity and good crimp joints.
7. **Defective reading:** OL (over-limit) with no beep. This indicates an **open circuit** — a broken wire or detached terminal. The corrective action is to **locate the break using the half-split method** (divide the wire into halves and test each half) and **splice with a soldered joint covered by adhesive-lined heat-shrink tubing**.

**Short-to-Ground Test Procedure:**

1. With the battery still disconnected, set the DMM to the **20 kΩ resistance range**.
2. Place one probe on the **tail-lamp power feed pin at the connector**.
3. Place the other probe on a **clean, paint-free chassis ground point**.
4. **Healthy reading:** OL (over-limit). The insulation is intact and no current can leak to ground.
5. **Defective reading:** 0 Ω to a few hundred ohms. This confirms a **short to ground** — the conductor's insulation has been compromised and is contacting the metal body. The corrective action is to **inspect the harness routing**, **identify the chafe point** (often where the harness passes through a metal grommet or near a sharp edge), and **repair with heat-shrink and cable ties** to prevent recurrence.

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **complete fault detection workflow** for an automotive wiring harness. List the tools required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive technician measures a **2.1 V drop** across the ground strap of a car while the headlamps are ON (drawing 5 A). The acceptable limit is **0.1 V**. Calculate the **excess resistance** in the ground path and recommend the corrective action. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Aim:** To describe the systematic fault detection workflow for an automotive wiring harness.

**Tools Required:** Digital Multimeter, automotive test lamp, megohmmeter (Megger), wiring diagram / service manual, scan tool (OBD-II), insulation stripper, crimping tool, heat gun, heat-shrink tubing, contact cleaner spray, safety gloves and glasses.

**Block Diagram Description:** (Refer to the Mermaid functional block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes — the workflow proceeds: **Visual Inspection → Continuity Test → Short-to-Ground Test → Voltage Drop Test → Insulation Resistance Test → Wiggle / Intermittent Test → Document & Repair**).

**Step-by-Step Explanation of the Workflow:**

1. **Receive the complaint / observe the symptom** from the customer or service record.
2. **Visual Inspection (Step 1):** Survey the accessible harness sections for chafed insulation, melted zones, corrosion, rodent damage, and connector damage. This non-invasive step resolves 40–50% of faults.
3. **Continuity Test (Step 2):** De-energize the circuit by disconnecting the battery. Use the DMM in continuity mode to verify that the conductor provides a continuous low-resistance path from source to load. An open circuit is indicated by an OL reading.
4. **Short-to-Ground Test (Step 3):** With the circuit still de-energized, measure resistance from each conductor to chassis. Any reading below OL confirms insulation failure and a short-to-ground fault.
5. **Voltage Drop Test (Step 4):** Reconnect the battery and operate the load. Measure the voltage loss across each conductor segment while current is flowing. Values exceeding 0.5 V (power) or 0.1 V (signal) indicate high-resistance joints.
6. **Insulation Resistance Test (Step 5):** For intermittent or hard-to-find leakage, use a 500 V Megger to stress-test the insulation. Readings below 100 MΩ confirm insulation degradation.
7. **Wiggle / Intermittent Test (Step 6):** Energize the circuit, monitor the DMM, and physically disturb connectors and harness sections. Any momentary reading deviation confirms an intermittent connection.
8. **Document the findings** in the service record, repair the identified fault using industry-standard methods (crimp, solder, heat-shrink), and **re-test** to confirm the repair.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given Data:**

$$V_{drop,measured} = 2.1 \, V, \quad I_{load} = 5 \, A, \quad V_{drop,acceptable} = 0.1 \, V$$

**Step 1 — Calculate the actual total resistance in the ground path:**

Using Ohm's Law:

$$R_{total} = \frac{V_{drop,measured}}{I_{load}} = \frac{2.1}{5} = 0.42 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the acceptable resistance (baseline):**

$$R_{acceptable} = \frac{V_{drop,acceptable}}{I_{load}} = \frac{0.1}{5} = 0.02 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the excess resistance (the fault contribution):**

$$R_{excess} = R_{total} - R_{acceptable} = 0.42 - 0.02 = 0.40 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Verification via the voltage drop equation:**

$$V_{drop,excess} = I_{load} \times R_{excess} = 5 \times 0.40 = 2.0 \, V$$

This matches the observed excess: $2.1 \, V - 0.1 \, V = 2.0 \, V$. Calculation is consistent.

**Step 5 — Interpretation and Corrective Action:**

The ground strap has an **excess resistance of 0.40 Ω**, which is **twenty times** the acceptable value. The root cause is almost certainly **corrosion at the ground bolt-to-chassis interface** or a **frayed ground strap**. The corrective actions are:

1. **Disconnect the battery negative terminal**.
2. **Remove the ground strap bolt**, clean the contact surfaces on both the strap lug and the chassis with a wire brush until bright metal is exposed.
3. Apply **dielectric grease** to prevent future oxidation.
4. **Re-torque the ground bolt** to the manufacturer-specified value (typically 8–12 Nm).
5. If the strap itself is frayed or damaged, **replace it with an OEM-equivalent ground strap** of the correct gauge.
6. **Reconnect the battery and re-test** the voltage drop. The reading should now be ≤ 0.1 V.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PCAUL507 Lab Record / Exam:**
> 1. **Not stating the DMM range used** — always specify the range (e.g., "20 V DC", "200 Ω", "20 kΩ"). The examiner checks this first.
> 2. **Performing a voltage drop test on a de-energized circuit** — this gives a meaningless 0 V reading. Voltage drop **must** be measured under load.
> 3. **Confusing "continuity" with "short-to-ground"** — both use resistance mode but the test setup is opposite. Continuity tests between two ends of the same wire; short-to-ground tests between one wire and chassis.
> 4. **Forgetting to disconnect the battery** before continuity / short-to-ground tests — risk of blowing the DMM fuse and producing false readings.
> 5. **Writing the conclusion as "fault found"** without quoting the **numerical reading** that confirms the fault — the examiner's checklist explicitly requires "expected vs observed" numerical comparison.
> 6. **Skipping the safety preamble** — always begin the record with the four safety steps (battery disconnect, capacitor discharge, PPE, fuse rating check).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A wiring harness fault is any deviation in the electrical continuity, insulation integrity, or connection quality of the harness assembly.
- **Five fault classes:** Open circuit, short to ground, short to power, high resistance, intermittent.
- **Five core tests:** Visual inspection, continuity, short-to-ground, voltage drop, insulation resistance (Megger).
- **Acceptable voltage drop limits:** ≤ **0.5 V** for power circuits, ≤ **0.1 V** for signal circuits and ground straps.
- **Minimum insulation resistance:** **100 MΩ** at 500 V DC.
- **Healthy continuity reading:** **0 to 1 Ω** (with audible beep on a DMM in continuity mode).
- **Key instruments:** DMM, test lamp, megohmmeter, scan tool, wiring diagram.
- **Wire resistance formula:** $R = \rho L / A$ — for copper, $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot m$.
- **Voltage drop formula:** $V_{drop} = I \times R$ — use this for every numerical problem.
- **Always disconnect the battery** before resistance-based tests to avoid DMM damage and false readings.
- **Always test under load** for voltage drop measurements; de-energized voltage drop is meaningless.
- **The half-split method** is the fastest technique to localize an open circuit in a long harness — bisect the wire, test each half, then bisect the faulty half again.
- **Ground strap integrity** is the most overlooked yet most frequent root cause of dim lights, weak cranking, and erratic sensor behavior.
- **Document every reading** in a structured observation table — KTU examiners award marks for tabulated data, not narrative paragraphs.
- **Safety first:** PPE, battery isolation, correct fuse replacement — never bypass a fuse with a higher-rated one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL507 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiments using On Board Diagnostic system

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD)** is a standardized vehicular self-diagnostic and reporting system mandated by regulatory bodies (such as the **California Air Resources Board – CARB** and the **European Union – EU directives 98/69/EC & 2003/76/EC**) that continuously monitors the performance of major engine and emission-control sub-systems via a network of electronic sensors and actuators, and reports any detected malfunction by illuminating the **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)** on the instrument cluster and storing a corresponding **Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC)** in the vehicle's **Engine Control Unit (ECU) / Electronic Control Module (ECM)** non-volatile memory.

The second-generation implementation, universally adopted since **1996 (USA)** and **2001 (EU petrol)** / **2004 (EU diesel)**, is termed **OBD-II** (also written as **OBD2**). It is governed by the **SAE J1962** diagnostic connector standard, **SAE J1979** scan-tool protocol, and **ISO 15031** series for road-vehicle communication.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of OBD as the "black-box + health-monitor" of a modern car.**

Imagine your car is a human body. The car has many "organs" — the engine, transmission, exhaust, brakes, airbags. Just like a hospital patient is hooked up to an ICU monitor that checks heart rate, oxygen level, and temperature in **real time**, an OBD system continuously watches every critical component of the vehicle.

When something goes wrong — say, the air-fuel mixture becomes too lean because of a vacuum leak — the system does three things:
1. **Turns on a warning light** (the MIL / "check engine" light) — like a patient feeling chest pain.
2. **Records a specific code** (e.g., P0171) — like a doctor writing a diagnosis in the chart.
3. **Freezes a snapshot** of engine conditions at the moment of the fault — like freezing a frame in CCTV footage.

A mechanic then plugs in a **scan tool** (the "stethoscope") into the **16-pin OBD-II port** (usually under the dashboard) to read these codes — just like a doctor reading the chart. The car "talks" to the scan tool using a digital language called the **CAN (Controller Area Network) bus** protocol, standardized so that any manufacturer's car can be diagnosed by any compliant tool.

## 1.3 Key OBD-II Technical Standards (KTU-Mandated Terminology)

| Standard | Scope | Governing Body |
|----------|-------|----------------|
| **SAE J1962** | 16-pin diagnostic connector (Type A = 12 V, Type B = 24 V) | SAE International |
| **SAE J1979** | Diagnostic Test Modes (Mode 01–0A) | SAE International |
| **SAE J2012** | Diagnostic Trouble Code definitions | SAE International |
| **ISO 15031-5** | Diagnostic services & emissions-related messages | ISO |
| **ISO 15765-4** | Diagnostics over CAN (CAN-HS / CAN-MS) | ISO |
| **SAE J1939** | Heavy-duty / commercial vehicle diagnostics | SAE International |
| **ISO 14229 (UDS)** | Unified Diagnostic Services (modern) | ISO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The lab experiment specifically targets the **read/clear of DTCs**, **live Parameter ID (PID) data**, **freeze-frame retrieval**, and **O2 sensor / readiness monitor inspection** using a standard OBD-II scan tool.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 OBD-II System Architecture — Block-Level Decomposition

The OBD-II ecosystem has **four logical layers**:

1. **Sensor/Actuator Layer** – Physical devices reporting to the ECU.
2. **ECU / ECM Layer** – Executes the on-board diagnostic executive (OBD-II software).
3. **Communication Layer** – Wired bus (CAN, ISO 9141, KWP2000).
4. **Off-Board Tool Layer** – Scan tool / OBD dongle / HMI laptop.

## 2.2 Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC) Format — `XYYYY`

> [!NOTE]
> Every OBD-II DTC is a **5-character alphanumeric string** with strict positional meaning.

$$\text{DTC} = \underbrace{X}_{\text{System}} \ \underbrace{Y_1 Y_2}_{\text{Category (2 digits)}} \ \underbrace{Y_3 Y_4}_{\text{Fault Index (2 hex digits)}}$$

### Position `X` — System Identifier

| X | System |
|---|--------|
| **P** | **Powertrain** (engine, transmission) |
| **B** | **Body** (airbags, lighting, doors) |
| **C** | **Chassis** (ABS, suspension, steering) |
| **U** | **Network / Communication** (CAN bus, module links) |

### Position `Y₁Y₂` — Code Category

| Code | Type |
|------|------|
| **00** | SAE / Generic (mandatory, manufacturer-independent) |
| **01, 02, 03** | Manufacturer-specific (P1xxx, B1xxx, C1xxx, U1xxx) |
| **10–3F** | Generic (fuel, air, ignition, emissions, transmission) |
| **40–7F** | Reserved / future |
| **80–FF** | Manufacturer-specific (extended) |

### Example: `P0301`
- **P** → Powertrain
- **03** → Ignition system / Misfire
- **01** → Cylinder #1 misfire detected

## 2.3 SAE J1979 Diagnostic Test Modes (KTU High-Yield)

| Mode ID | Mode Name | KTU Lab Use |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| **Mode 01** | **Live data / current Powertrain Diagnostic Data** | Read RPM, coolant temp, MAF, O2 voltages |
| **Mode 02** | **Freeze Frame Data** | Retrieve snapshot of conditions at DTC trigger |
| **Mode 03** | **Read Stored DTCs** | List all confirmed MIL codes |
| **Mode 04** | **Clear DTCs & Freeze Frame** | Reset — turn MIL off, erase memory |
| **Mode 05** | O2 Sensor Monitoring Test Results | Long-term fuel-trim data |
| **Mode 06** | On-Board Monitoring Test Results (non-continuous) | Catalyst, EVAP monitor pass/fail |
| **Mode 07** | Pending DTCs (1-trip fault) | Read codes not yet MIL-illuminating |
| **Mode 08** | Bidirectional Control (EVAP vent, EGR) | Actuator tests |
| **Mode 09** | Vehicle Information (VIN, CVN) | Identification |
| **Mode 0A** | Permanent DTCs (cleared only by ECU after verified pass) | Emissions compliance |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> OBD-II is more of a protocol than a formula-heavy subject. However, the following quantitative relationships are **mandatory for KTU 2024 lab viva & theory:**

| Symbol / Parameter | Relationship / Value | Unit |
|--------------------|----------------------|------|
| **OBD-II supply voltage** | $\mathbf{V_{\text{supply}} = 12 \pm 0.5}$ (Type A) or $\mathbf{24 \pm 1.0}$ (Type B) | V DC |
| **CAN-High recessive level** | $\mathbf{V_{\text{CAN-H,r}} = 2.5}$ | V |
| **CAN-High dominant level** | $\mathbf{V_{\text{CAN-H,d}} = 3.5}$ | V |
| **CAN-Low recessive level** | $\mathbf{V_{\text{CAN-L,r}} = 2.5}$ | V |
| **CAN-Low dominant level** | $\mathbf{V_{\text{CAN-L,d}} = 1.5}$ | V |
| **CAN differential voltage (dominant)** | $\mathbf{V_{\text{diff}} = V_{\text{CAN-H}} - V_{\text{CAN-L}} = 2.0}$ | V |
| **CAN bus speed (OBD-II ISO 15765)** | $\mathbf{500 \ \text{kbit/s}}$ (HS-CAN) | kbps |
| **CAN bus speed (MS-CAN)** | $\mathbf{125 \ \text{kbit/s}}$ | kbps |
| **Engine coolant temp. PID (0x05)** | $\mathbf{T = A - 40}$ (where $A$ = raw byte) | °C |
| **Engine RPM PID (0x0C)** | $\mathbf{N = \dfrac{256 A + B}{4}}$ (where $A, B$ = 2 bytes) | rpm |
| **Vehicle speed PID (0x0D)** | $\mathbf{V = A}$ (single byte) | km/h |
| **Throttle position PID (0x11)** | $\mathbf{\theta_{\%} = 100 \cdot \dfrac{A}{255}}$ | % |
| **O2 sensor voltage PID (0x14)** | $\mathbf{V_{O_2} = \dfrac{A}{200}}$ | V |
| **MIL status (PID 0x01, bit A7)** | $\mathbf{0 = \text{OFF}, \ 1 = \text{ON}}$ | boolean |
| **DTC count (PID 0x01, bits D0–D3 of A)** | $\mathbf{0 \text{ to } 0xF}$ | integer |
| **Number of DTCs cleared (Mode 04 response)** | $\mathbf{N_{\text{cleared}} = 0 \text{ (success)}}$ | count |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All PIDs return raw integer bytes; the scan tool performs the linear conversion (scaling, offset) using the SAE J1979 lookup tables. The values above are the **official SAE linear formulas** for the most-tested PIDs.

## 2.5 Why OBD-II Matters in Engineering

- **Emissions compliance** — Regulatory agencies use OBD data to inspect in-use vehicles.
- **Predictive maintenance** — Fleet operators use telematics to reduce downtime.
- **Insurance & resale** — OBD dongles track driver behaviour (UBI).
- **Forensic analysis** — Crash investigators extract freeze-frame data.
- **Design feedback** — OEMs identify systemic faults via aggregated DTC telemetry.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DTC Decoding — Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A scan tool returns the code **P0420**. Interpret it.

### Step 1 — Identify System Character `X = P`

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus table, `P` denotes the **Powertrain** system — covering engine, transmission, fuel, ignition, emissions, and accessory drives.

### Step 2 — Split the Code into `Y₁Y₂` and `Y₃Y₄`

$$\text{P} \ \underbrace{04}_{\text{Category code}} \ \underbrace{20}_{\text{Fault index}}$$

### Step 3 — Interpret Category `04`

From the SAE J2012 category table:

| `Y₁Y₂` | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| 00 | SAE Generic |
| 01 | Misfire (manufacturer) |
| 02 | Fuel / Air metering |
| 03 | Ignition / Misfire |
| **04** | **Emissions (Auxiliary emissions controls)** |
| 05 | Vehicle speed / idle / engine speed |
| 06 | Computer / Output circuit |
| 07 | Transmission |
| 08–0F | Reserved |

So `04` indicates an **emissions-related** sub-system. (Note: Catalytic-converter efficiency codes also fall in the `04` group, specifically `P0420` and `P0430`.)

### Step 4 — Interpret Fault Index `20`

`20` in hex → `0x20` → decimal `32`. Refer to the SAE J2012 fault dictionary for `P04xx` series:

> **P0420 — Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)**

### Step 5 — Formulate Engineering Conclusion

> The OBD-II system has detected that the **Bank-1 catalytic converter** is no longer storing oxygen as efficiently as expected. Typically, the post-catalyst O2 sensor is mirroring the pre-catalyst sensor (both oscillating at the same rate) because the catalyst has lost its conversion efficiency. Likely root causes:
> 1. Failed / aged catalytic converter.
> 2. Exhaust leak before Bank 1 sensor.
> 3. Faulty downstream O2 sensor.
> 4. Engine running rich or lean for extended period.

**Resulting MIL action:** MIL will be illuminated after **2 consecutive driving cycles** with this fault (one-trip fault = pending; two-trip = confirmed stored).

---

## 3.2 Live-Data PID Conversion — Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A scan tool requests **PID 0x0C (Engine RPM)** and receives the data bytes `A = 0x1A` and `B = 0x6E`. Compute the engine speed.

### Step 1 — Apply the SAE J1979 Linear Formula

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{rpm}} &= \dfrac{256 \, A + B}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Raw Values

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \text{0x1A} = 26_{10} \\
B &= \text{0x6E} = 110_{10}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Numerator

$$\begin{aligned}
256 \cdot A + B &= 256 \cdot 26 + 110 \\
&= 6656 + 110 \\
&= 6766
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Divide by 4

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{rpm}} &= \dfrac{6766}{4} \\
&= 1691.5 \ \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Round to the Resolution of the Sensor

$$\boxed{N_{\text{rpm}} \approx 1692 \ \text{rpm}}$$

This matches a typical **engine idle speed** for a warmed-up 4-cylinder petrol engine.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DTC Families Encountered in the Lab

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is the **minimum set** a KTU 2024 student is expected to recognise in a lab viva.

| DTC | Sub-system | Symptom | Common Cause | Action |
|-----|-----------|---------|--------------|--------|
| **P0011** | Variable Valve Timing (VVT) | Rough idle, low power | Stretched timing chain | Inspect cam phaser |
| **P0101** | MAF Sensor range/performance | Hesitation | Dirty MAF | Clean / replace |
| **P0128** | Coolant temp. below thermostat | Slow warm-up | Stuck-open thermostat | Replace thermostat |
| **P0171** | System too lean (Bank 1) | Misfire, poor FE | Vacuum leak / weak fuel pump | Smoke test |
| **P0300** | Random misfire | Engine shake | Spark / fuel / compression | O2 + compression test |
| **P0420** | Catalyst efficiency Bank 1 | Emissions fail | Aged catalyst | Replace cat |
| **P0506** | Idle Air Control low | Stalling | Carbon in IAC / vacuum leak | Clean throttle body |
| **P0700** | Transmission control system MIL request | Limp mode | TCM sets internal code | Read TCM-side codes |
| **B1352** | Ignition key-in circuit failure | No-start | Faulty ignition switch | Replace switch |
| **C0561** | ABS disabled by other DTCs | ABS light on | Wheel-speed sensor | Diagnose upstream |
| **U0100** | Lost communication with ECM/PCM | Multiple warnings | CAN-H / CAN-L open | Check CAN wiring |

---

## 3.4 DTC Storage & Clearing — Analytical Walkthrough

The MIL is governed by the **OBD-II "trip" logic**:

1. **1st trip with fault** → Fault is logged as **PENDING**, MIL **not** lit.
2. **2nd consecutive trip with the same fault** → Fault is **CONFIRMED**, MIL **lit**, **freeze frame** stored.
3. **Mode 04 (Clear)** is invoked → MIL **extinguished**, pending + confirmed DTCs **erased** from RAM and NVM, freeze frame cleared.
4. **Permanent DTCs (Mode 0A)** can only be cleared by the ECU once a self-diagnosis cycle passes.

The number of *drive cycles* required to clear a permanent code is implementation-specific (typically 40–80 warm-up cycles).

---

## 3.5 OBD-II Communication Protocol Stack

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Application Layer} &\rightarrow \text{SAE J1979 (Modes / PIDs)} \\
\text{Transport Layer} &\rightarrow \text{ISO 15765-2 (ISO-TP for > 7-byte payloads)} \\
\text{Network Layer} &\rightarrow \text{ISO 15765-4 (CAN-based)} \\
\text{Data Link / Physical} &\rightarrow \text{ISO 11898-1/2 (CAN 2.0B, 500 kbps)}
\end{aligned}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCAUL507]**
> **Q1.** What is the function of the SAE J1962 connector in an OBD-II system? Mention the supply voltage pins.

**Model Answer:**
The SAE J1962 connector is the **standardised 16-pin Data Link Connector (DLC)** that provides the physical interface between the vehicle's ECU and any off-board scan tool. It carries the battery supply, chassis ground, signal ground, and multiple protocol signal lines (CAN-H, CAN-L, K-Line, L-Line, J1850 VPW/PWM). The two supply/ground pins are:
- **Pin 4** – Chassis ground
- **Pin 5** – Signal ground
- **Pin 16** – Battery positive (**+12 V, unswitched**)

It ensures that **any** OBD-II-compliant scan tool can communicate with **any** OBD-II-compliant vehicle regardless of manufacturer.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCAUL507]**
> **Q2.** Explain the difference between **Pending** and **Confirmed** DTCs.

**Model Answer:**
A **Pending DTC** is recorded by the OBD-II executive the **first time** a diagnostic test reports a failure (1st trip); the MIL is **not** illuminated, but the code is stored as a "one-trip fault". A **Confirmed DTC** is recorded only after the **same fault is detected on the next consecutive driving cycle** (2nd trip). At that point, the **MIL is illuminated**, the DTC is moved to confirmed status, and a **freeze-frame** snapshot of engine conditions is captured. Confirmed codes are what trigger an emissions-inspection failure; pending codes do not.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Always** quote the standard number (SAE J1979, SAE J2012, ISO 15765-4) when describing a mode — examiners award 2 marks specifically for "standard" identification.
> - **Always** state the **pin number** of the J1962 connector in any wiring question.
> - **Always** show the **PID formula** (e.g., RPM = (256A+B)/4) when computing a parameter — direct numeric answers without derivation lose 1–2 marks.
> - **Never** confuse OBD-I (pre-1996) features with OBD-II — this is the most common KTU trap.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, describe the architecture of an OBD-II system. List the **SAE J1979 diagnostic modes** and explain the function of **Mode 01, Mode 03, and Mode 04** in detail.

> **(b) [7 Marks)** A scan tool reads DTC **P0303** and freeze-frame data showing **PID 0x0C (Engine RPM) = 0x09 0x60** and **PID 0x05 (Coolant Temp.) = 0x5A**. Decode the DTC and compute the engine RPM and coolant temperature.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The OBD-II architecture consists of **four logical layers** (already shown in SECTION 4.1):
1. **Sensor/Actuator Layer** – O2, MAF, TPS, CKP, ECT, knock sensor, injectors, IAC.
2. **ECU/ECM Layer** – Houses the on-board diagnostic executive and the six mandated monitors:
   - Misfire, Fuel System, Catalyst Efficiency, EVAP, O2 Sensor, Comprehensive Components.
3. **Communication Layer** – CAN (ISO 15765-4, 500 kbps) is the modern default; legacy ISO 9141 (K-Line) and SAE J1850 are retained for older vehicles.
4. **Off-Board Tool Layer** – Scan tool, laptop, telematics dongle.

**SAE J1979 Diagnostic Test Modes:**

| Mode | Function |
|------|----------|
| Mode 01 | Read current powertrain live data via PIDs (RPM, speed, coolant, O2, etc.). |
| **Mode 03** | **Read all confirmed DTCs** that have caused the MIL to illuminate. |
| **Mode 04** | **Clear all confirmed and pending DTCs**, freeze-frame data, and MIL status (does not clear permanent DTCs). |
| Mode 02 | Read freeze-frame data associated with a confirmed DTC. |
| Mode 05–0A | Long-term fuel trim, O2 monitor results, pending codes, bidirectional control, vehicle info, permanent codes. |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Decode the DTC `P0303`:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{P} &\rightarrow \text{Powertrain} \\
03 &\rightarrow \text{Ignition / Misfire category} \\
03 &\rightarrow \text{Cylinder \#3 specifically} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, **P0303 = Cylinder 3 Misfire Detected**.

**Step 2 — Compute Engine RPM from raw bytes 0x09 and 0x60:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \text{0x09} = 9_{10}, \quad B = \text{0x60} = 96_{10} \\
N_{\text{rpm}} &= \dfrac{256 \cdot 9 + 96}{4} = \dfrac{2304 + 96}{4} = \dfrac{2400}{4} \\
&= \boxed{600 \ \text{rpm}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Coolant Temperature from raw byte 0x5A:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \text{0x5A} = 90_{10} \\
T_{\text{coolant}} &= A - 40 = 90 - 40 \\
&= \boxed{50 \ ^\circ \text{C}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Diagnostic Conclusion:**

The freeze frame at the moment of the Cylinder-3 misfire was captured at **600 rpm** (idle) with the engine fully warm (**50 °C** coolant). This is characteristic of a **cold-start / low-load misfire** — common causes are:
1. **Failed ignition coil** on Cylinder 3.
2. **Fouled spark plug** on Cylinder 3.
3. **Faulty fuel injector** on Cylinder 3 (electrical or mechanical).
4. **Low compression** in Cylinder 3 (mechanical).

Recommended next lab step: **swap coil from Cyl. 3 to Cyl. 2**, clear code, and re-test to see if the misfire follows the coil.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the pin assignment of the **SAE J1962 diagnostic connector**. Identify the pins used for **CAN bus** communication and the **battery supply**. Why is the CAN bus used as the preferred OBD-II protocol in modern vehicles?

> **(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to the **DTC code format `XYYYY`**, classify the following codes and explain their meaning:
> 1. `B1352`
> 2. `C0561`
> 3. `U0100`
> 4. `P0171`
> 5. `P0420`

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The SAE J1962 connector is a **16-pin trapezoidal** female connector, normally located within **300 mm of the steering wheel** (typically under the dashboard). The pinout is:

| Pin | Function |
|-----|----------|
| **2** | SAE J1850 VPW / PWM + (positive bus) |
| **4** | **Chassis ground** |
| **5** | **Signal ground** |
| **6** | **CAN High (ISO 15765-4)** |
| **7** | K-Line (ISO 9141-2 / ISO 14230-4 KWP2000) |
| **10** | SAE J1850 VPW / PWM – (negative bus) |
| **14** | **CAN Low (ISO 15765-4)** |
| **15** | L-Line (optional, ISO 9141-2 init) |
| **16** | **Battery + 12 V (unswitched)** |

Pins 6 & 14 are the differential **CAN-H** and **CAN-L** lines used for modern high-speed diagnostics at **500 kbit/s**.

**Why CAN is the preferred protocol:**
1. **High bandwidth (500 kbps)** supports fast live-data streaming and complex UDS services.
2. **Multi-master arbitration** — multiple ECUs (ECM, TCM, BCM, ABS) can publish on the same bus without collision.
3. **Robust differential signalling** → excellent noise immunity in the harsh electrical environment of a vehicle.
4. **Standardised by ISO 15765-4** for diagnostics and ISO 11898 for the physical layer, ensuring **manufacturer-independent interoperability**.
5. **Built-in error detection (CRC, ACK, bit-stuffing)** — critical for safety-related ECUs.
6. **Scalability** — the same bus can carry OBD-II messages and high-speed powertrain data simultaneously.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Code | `X` (System) | `Y₁Y₂` (Category) | `Y₃Y₄` (Fault) | Meaning |
|------|--------------|-------------------|----------------|---------|
| **B1352** | B → **Body** | 1 → Manufacturer-specific | 352 → Fault index | Ignition key-in circuit failure (manufacturer-specific) |
| **C0561** | C → **Chassis** | 0 → SAE Generic | 561 → Stability control disabled signal message counter incorrect | ABS / Stability-control module disabled due to internal DTC |
| **U0100** | U → **Network** | 0 → SAE Generic | 100 → Lost communication with ECM/PCM "A" | CAN bus communication lost between a control module and the ECM |
| **P0171** | P → **Powertrain** | 1 → Fuel / Air metering | 71 → System too lean, Bank 1 | Long-term fuel trim indicates lean condition (vacuum leak / weak fuel pressure) |
| **P0420** | P → **Powertrain** | 4 → Auxiliary emissions | 20 → Catalyst efficiency below threshold, Bank 1 | Catalytic converter on Bank 1 not storing oxygen as expected |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfalls in Part (b):**
> - Confusing **B and C codes** with P codes — KTU examiners specifically test that you know `B` is **Body**, `C` is **Chassis**, `U` is **Network/Communication**.
> - Forgetting that **`0` in position 2 means SAE Generic** (the most-tested codes), while `1` means Manufacturer-Specific.
> - Writing "misfire" for P0171 (it is a **lean-condition** code, not a misfire — though a chronic lean can *cause* a misfire).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **MUST-KNOW FACTS for KTU 2024 PCAUL507 Lab Exam:**

- [x] **OBD-II connector** = SAE J1962, **16 pins**, **Pin 16 = +12 V**, **Pin 4 = chassis GND**, **Pin 5 = signal GND**, **Pins 6 & 14 = CAN-H / CAN-L**.
- [x] **CAN bus speed for OBD-II** = **500 kbps** (HS-CAN).
- [x] **DTC format** = **5 characters `XYYYY`**; **P** = Powertrain, **B** = Body, **C** = Chassis, **U** = Network.
- [x] **2nd character = 0** → SAE Generic; **= 1** → Manufacturer-specific.
- [x] **MIL illumination** rule → **2 consecutive trips** for confirmed DTC.
- [x] **Mode 01** = live data; **Mode 02** = freeze frame; **Mode 03** = read DTCs; **Mode 04** = clear DTCs.
- [x] **RPM PID 0x0C formula** → $N = (256A + B)/4$ **rpm**.
- [x] **Coolant PID 0x05 formula** → $T = A - 40$ **°C**.
- [x] **Vehicle Speed PID 0x0D formula** → $V = A$ **km/h** (1 byte).
- [x] **Throttle PID 0x11 formula** → $\theta_{\%} = 100 \cdot A/255$.
- [x] **O2 voltage PID 0x14 formula** → $V_{O_2} = A/200$ **V**.
- [x] **Permanent DTCs (Mode 0A)** can be cleared **only by the ECU** after a verified self-test pass — not by the scan tool.
- [x] **CAN dominant differential** = $V_{CAN-H} - V_{CAN-L} = 3.5 - 1.5 = \mathbf{2.0 \ V}$; recessive = **0 V**.
- [x] **OBD-I era** = pre-1996; **OBD-II mandated** in the **USA from 1996**, **EU petrol 2001**, **EU diesel 2004**.
- [x] **Six mandated OBD-II monitors** = Misfire, Fuel System, Catalyst Efficiency, EVAP, O2 Sensor, Comprehensive Components.
- [x] **Communication protocols** in OBD-II = ISO 15765-4 (CAN), ISO 9141-2, ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000), SAE J1850 VPW, SAE J1850 PWM.
- [x] **Common KTU-lab DTCs** to recognise cold: P0011, P0101, P0128, P0171, P0300–P0308, P0420, P0506, P0700, B1352, C0561, U0100.
- [x] Always record **Freeze Frame (Mode 02)** *before* clearing the code (Mode 04), because freeze-frame data is erased by Mode 04.
- [x] Always **cycle ignition OFF → ON** after Mode 04 to verify MIL extinction.

> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2, PCAUL507, KTU 2024 Scheme.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servicing of electric two-wheeler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL507 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Servicing of electric two-wheeler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Servicing of an Electric Two-Wheeler (E2W)** is defined as the systematic procedure of inspection, diagnostics, preventive maintenance, repair, and recalibration of all electrical and electro-mechanical sub-systems of a battery-electric two-wheeler, including the **Traction Battery Pack (TBS)**, **Battery Management System (BMS)**, **Motor Controller (ECU/MCU)**, **BLDC/PMSM Traction Motor**, **DC-DC Converter**, **On-Board Charger (OBC)**, **Throttle Assembly**, **Wiring Harness**, **Instrument Cluster**, and **Auxiliary Lighting Circuit**, in order to ensure **operational safety (AIS-156 / IEC 62133-2 compliance)**, **range integrity**, and **functional reliability**.

### Subsystem Decomposition Table

| # | Sub-System | Function | Service Criticality |
|---|------------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Traction Battery Pack (TBS) | Stores electrical energy | **High** |
| 2 | Battery Management System (BMS) | Cell balancing, SOC/SOH estimation, protection | **Critical** |
| 3 | Motor Controller (MCU) | Converts DC → 3-φ AC, torque/speed control | **High** |
| 4 | BLDC / PMSM Motor | Converts electrical → mechanical energy | **Medium** |
| 5 | DC-DC Converter | Steps down 48/60 V → 12 V for auxiliaries | **Medium** |
| 6 | On-Board Charger (OBC) | AC mains → DC battery charging | **High** |
| 7 | Throttle / Encoder | Driver input → torque request | **Medium** |
| 8 | Wiring Harness & Connectors | Power and signal distribution | **High** |
| 9 | Instrument Cluster (CAN-bus) | SOC, speed, fault display | **Low** |
| 10 | Braking (Regenerative + Mechanical) | Energy recovery + friction braking | **High** |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Electric Scooter as a Living Organism"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of an electric two-wheeler exactly like a **human body**:
> - The **Battery Pack** is the **stomach/digestive system** — it stores and digests energy (food).
> - The **BMS** is the **brainstem** — it silently monitors, protects, and balances every cell.
> - The **Motor** is the **muscle** — it converts stored energy into movement (torque).
> - The **Controller (MCU)** is the **motor cortex** — it decides *how much* and *how fast* the muscle should contract.
> - The **Throttle** is the **mouth** — the input point where the rider feeds intent into the system.
> - The **Wiring Harness** is the **circulatory/nervous system** — carrying power (blood) and signals (nerve impulses).
> - The **DC-DC Converter** is the **liver** — it conditions the high-energy supply into a form usable by smaller organs (lights, horn, cluster).
> - **Servicing**, therefore, is the equivalent of an **annual health check-up plus physiotherapy** — inspecting vitals, refilling, cleaning, and recalibrating so the organism runs smoothly.

This analogy makes it intuitive why each subsystem needs a *dedicated* diagnostic step during servicing: just as a doctor cannot skip checking the heart during a check-up, a service technician cannot skip the BMS or motor insulation test.

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

The following engineering constants and standard metrics govern E2W servicing under the KTU 2024 scheme:

- **Nominal Battery Voltage (V_bat):** typically **48 V**, **60 V**, or **72 V** (lead-acid replaced by Li-ion)
- **Standard Cell Chemistry:** **NMC (Nickel Manganese Cobalt)**, **LFP (Lithium Iron Phosphate)** — *LFP preferred for Indian tropical climate due to thermal stability*
- **Safety Standard:** **AIS-156 (Phases 1 & 2)** for traction battery safety in India
- **Cell Standard:** **IEC 62133-2** for portable lithium cells
- **IP Rating (Battery enclosure):** **IP67** (minimum mandated)
- **Insulation Resistance Threshold:** **≥ 100 Ω/V** of nominal voltage (as per AIS-156)
- **Charger Standard:** **Bharat AC-001 / Bharat DC-001** for public chargers
- **Motor Efficiency Benchmark:** **≥ 85%** at rated load for BLDC hub motors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 students must remember that *servicing* is **not** just "tightening nuts". Under NEP-2020 outcome-based education, the lab viva will test whether you can **measure, document, and interpret** diagnostic data — not merely list components.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of an Electric Two-Wheeler

An electric two-wheeler operates on a **closed-loop energy conversion chain**. Let us break the chain into structured logic steps:

### Step 1 — Energy Storage
The **traction battery pack** stores DC electrical energy via **Li-ion cells** configured in series-parallel (e.g., **13S10P** = 48 V, ~30 Ah). Each cell has a nominal voltage of **3.6 V (NMC)** or **3.2 V (LFP)**.

### Step 2 — Battery Management
The **BMS** continuously samples **per-cell voltage**, **pack current**, and **temperature** at intervals of **≤ 100 ms**. It performs:
- **Cell balancing** (passive or active)
- **SOC (State of Charge) estimation** via Coulomb counting or Kalman filtering
- **SOH (State of Health) tracking** over cycles
- **Cut-off protection** (over-voltage, under-voltage, over-current, short-circuit, over-temperature)

### Step 3 — Power Conversion
The **Motor Control Unit (MCU)** receives a torque command from the throttle (typically a **Hall-effect encoder** giving 0–5 V analog or PWM signal). The MCU chops the DC bus using **Sinusoidal Pulse-Width Modulation (SPWM)** or **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** to produce a variable-frequency 3-phase AC output for the **BLDC** or **PMSM** motor.

### Step 4 — Mechanical Output
The motor (usually a **hub-mounted BLDC** in 2-wheelers) delivers torque directly to the wheel through a **planetary gear reduction** or **direct-drive** topology. Peak torque is typically **20–35 Nm** for a 1–3 kW scooter class.

### Step 5 — Auxiliary Systems
A **DC-DC buck converter** steps the high-voltage DC bus down to **12 V / 5 A** for the headlamp, indicators, horn, and instrument cluster.

### Step 6 — Charging Path
When plugged in, the **OBC** rectifies and power-factor-corrects the AC mains to DC, feeding the pack through the BMS-controlled contactor. Charging follows a **CC-CV (Constant Current → Constant Voltage)** profile.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Lab Calculations)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important cheat-sheet** for the KTU lab viva. Memorise each equation, its boundary condition, and its unit.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $E_{pack} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ | Pack stored energy | Wh | Capacity verification |
| 2 | $E_{usable} = E_{pack} \times DOD_{max}$ | Usable energy (DOD = Depth of Discharge) | Wh | Range estimation |
| 3 | $P_{motor} = V \times I \times \eta_{motor}$ | Mechanical output power | W | Load testing |
| 4 | $T_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{motor}}{\omega}$ | Torque from power | N·m (with $\omega$ in rad/s) | Dynamometer test |
| 5 | $T_{motor} = k_t \times I_{phase}$ | BLDC torque constant equation | N·m | Phase current measurement |
| 6 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{bat} \times \eta_{mcu} \times \eta_{motor}$ | System efficiency | dimensionless, 0–1 | Energy audit |
| 7 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \dfrac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_0^t I(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Coulomb counting for SOC | % | BMS verification |
| 8 | $SOH = \dfrac{C_{actual}}{C_{rated}} \times 100$ | Battery health | % | Warranty / replacement |
| 9 | $R_{insulation} = \dfrac{V_{test}}{I_{leak}}$ | Insulation resistance (megger test) | MΩ | AIS-156 safety check |
| 10 | $t_{charge} = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \times DOD}{I_{charger} \times \eta_{OBC}}$ | Charging time | hours | Service quote |
| 11 | $V_{throttle} = k_{thr} \times \theta_{twist}$ | Throttle output voltage | V (0–5 V typical) | Throttle calibration |
| 12 | $\omega_{max} = \dfrac{V_{bus}}{k_e}$ | No-load speed limit | rad/s | Field-weakening check |
| 13 | $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \times \eta_{regen}$ | Recovered braking energy | J | Brake-blend calibration |
| 14 | $R_{range} = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{E_{cons/km}}$ | Theoretical range | km | Customer reporting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition Reminder:** All formulas assume **steady-state**, **room temperature (25 °C)**, and **freshly cycled cell**. At elevated temperatures (40–50 °C in Kerala summers), Li-ion capacity drops by **3–5%** per 10 °C rise above nominal — a *guaranteed viva question*.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The knowledge of E2W servicing is critical for:

1. **EV service-startup ventures** (India is targeting **30% EV penetration by 2030**).
2. **Roadside breakdown assistance** — a service engineer must isolate whether fault is in *battery*, *controller*, *motor*, or *throttle* within minutes.
3. **Insurance claim assessment** — post-accident E2W evaluation requires insulation, BMS log, and crash-cutoff verification.
4. **OEM dealership network** — every EV brand (Ola, Ather, Hero Vida, TVS iQube, Bajaj Chetak) requires certified service technicians.
5. **Fleet operators** (Bounce, Yulu, Vogo) — daily SoH tracking across thousands of batteries.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section demonstrates **three derivations** that KTU examiners frequently test in the lab record + viva combination: (a) Range estimation from first principles, (b) Charging-time derivation under CC-CV profile, and (c) Insulation resistance safety derivation. Every algebraic transition is written explicitly. **No step is skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Range of an Electric Two-Wheeler

### Step 1 — Define Usable Energy in the Battery Pack

The battery pack contains a finite amount of usable electrical energy. This is the energy that can be drawn before the BMS enforces the lower cut-off voltage.

$$
E_{usable} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah} \times DOD_{max}
$$

where:
- $V_{nom}$ = nominal pack voltage (V)
- $C_{Ah}$ = rated pack capacity (Ah)
- $DOD_{max}$ = maximum allowed depth of discharge (typically **0.8** for Li-ion to preserve cycle life)

**Conversion logic:** Multiply voltage × charge to obtain energy in watt-hours, then multiply by the depth-of-discharge fraction to obtain *usable* energy (since we never discharge below ~20% SOC in Li-ion service).

### Step 2 — Define Energy Consumed Per Kilometre

The vehicle consumes electrical energy to overcome **rolling resistance**, **aerodynamic drag**, **gradient**, and **internal losses**. Empirically, this is lumped into a single parameter — **energy consumption per km**.

$$
E_{cons/km} = \frac{E_{usable}}{R_{range}}
$$

Rearranging directly gives:

$$
R_{range} = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{E_{cons/km}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Range is simply *how much energy I have* divided by *how much energy I burn per km* — analogous to "how many litres of fuel do I have ÷ km/litre".

### Step 3 — Substitute Usable Energy

$$
R_{range} = \dfrac{V_{nom} \times C_{Ah} \times DOD_{max}}{E_{cons/km}}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation (Worked Example)

Take a typical 60 V, 30 Ah Li-ion e-scooter with $DOD = 0.8$ and $E_{cons/km} = 35$ Wh/km.

$$
E_{usable} = 60 \times 30 \times 0.8 = 1440 \text{ Wh}
$$

$$
R_{range} = \dfrac{1440}{35} = 41.14 \text{ km}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implication:** A 60V/30Ah NMC scooter typically delivers **~40 km realistic range** in mixed city riding. If the customer reports **less than 30 km**, the technician must suspect **battery degradation** ($SOH < 80\%$) or a **regen/aux drain fault**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Charging Time Under CC-CV Profile

### Step 1 — CC (Constant Current) Phase Energy Delivered

During CC, the charger delivers a fixed current $I_{ch}$ until the pack reaches its **absorption voltage** $V_{abs}$ (typically $4.2 \times N_{series}$ for NMC).

$$
t_{CC} = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \times (SOC_{abs} - SOC_{start})}{I_{ch} \times \eta_{OBC}}
$$

### Step 2 — CV (Constant Voltage) Phase Energy Delivered

During CV, the current decays exponentially while voltage is held at $V_{abs}$. The total CV time depends on pack internal resistance and capacity. Empirically, for a healthy pack:

$$
t_{CV} \approx \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{I_{ch}} \times k_{CV}
$$

where $k_{CV} \approx 0.3 - 0.5$ is the *CV absorption factor*.

### Step 3 — Total Charging Time

$$
t_{charge} = t_{CC} + t_{CV}
$$

$$
t_{charge} = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{I_{ch} \times \eta_{OBC}} \left[(SOC_{abs} - SOC_{start}) + k_{CV}\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation

For a 60 V / 30 Ah pack, $SOC_{start} = 0.2$, $SOC_{abs} = 0.9$, $I_{ch} = 10$ A, $\eta_{OBC} = 0.92$, $k_{CV} = 0.4$:

$$
t_{charge} = \dfrac{30}{10 \times 0.92} \left[(0.9 - 0.2) + 0.4\right]
$$

$$
t_{charge} = 3.26 \times 1.1 = 3.59 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Service insight:** If the customer's pack takes **>5 hours** to charge from 20→100%, the OBC may be **under-rated**, the **cooling fan** may be faulty, or one or more cells may be **out-of-balance**, causing premature CV absorption.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Insulation Resistance & Safety Verification (AIS-156)

### Step 1 — Definition

The insulation resistance $R_{ins}$ is the resistance between any live high-voltage conductor and the vehicle chassis (protective earth). It is measured using a **megohmmeter (megger)** at **500 V DC** for a 48 V system.

### Step 2 — Ohm's Law Application

$$
R_{ins} = \dfrac{V_{test}}{I_{leak}}
$$
