**Conversion logic:** We apply a known DC test voltage and measure the resulting leakage current. A high $R_{ins}$ means very little current leaks through the insulation — i.e., the insulation is good.

### Step 3 — AIS-156 Compliance Threshold

$$
R_{ins} \geq 100 \,\Omega/V \times V_{nom}
$$

For a 60 V system:

$$
R_{ins} \geq 100 \times 60 = 6000 \,\Omega = 6 \,k\Omega
$$

Industry practice uses a **stricter 100 kΩ minimum** for service acceptance.

### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation

If $V_{test} = 500$ V and $I_{leak} = 5 \,\mu A$:

$$
R_{ins} = \dfrac{500}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 \times 10^{6} \, \Omega = 100 \, M\Omega
$$

This **passes** AIS-156 by a wide margin.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Subsystem Fault-to-Symptom Mapping

| Symptom (Customer Reported) | Probable Subsystem | Diagnostic Test | Acceptable Value |
|------------------------------|--------------------|----------------|------------------|
| Range drop >30% | Traction Battery | Capacity test at 0.5C discharge | SOH ≥ 80% |
| Sudden power cut-off | BMS protection | Read BMS fault log via OBD/CAN | No active DTC |
| Jerky acceleration | Throttle / Encoder | Output voltage sweep 0→100% | 0.8 V → 4.2 V linear |
| Motor stalling at hill | MCU / Motor phase | Phase-to-phase resistance | $\leq 0.5\,\Omega$ imbalance |
| Lights dimming at start | DC-DC converter | Output voltage at idle | $13.5 \pm 0.5$ V |
| Slow charging (>5 h) | OBC / Cells | OBC output current clamp | $I_{ch} \geq I_{rated}$ |
| Intermittent cluster reset | 12 V supply / CAN bus | Check 12 V rail ripple | < 100 mV pp |
| Brake lever pull hard | Regen blend too high | MCU regen param | Customer-tunable |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture of an Electric Two-Wheeler

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **power flow**, **signal flow**, and **service touchpoints** of a typical electric two-wheeler. This is the *Block-Level Functional Architecture* the KTU examiner expects students to draw in the lab record.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC_M[AC Mains 230V] --> OBC_A[On-Board Charger OBC]
    OBC_A --> BMS_A[Battery Management System BMS]
    BMS_A -->|High Voltage DC Bus| PACK_A[Traction Battery Pack 48-72V]
    PACK_A -->|HV DC| MCU_A[Motor Control Unit MCU]
    MCU_A -->|3-phase AC| MOTOR_A[BLDC PMSM Hub Motor]
    MOTOR_A -->|Mechanical| WHEEL_A[WHEEL]
    PACK_A --> DCDC_A[DC-DC Buck Converter]
    DCDC_A -->|12V DC| AUX_A[Auxiliary Loads Lights Horn Cluster]
    THR_A[Throttle Encoder] -->|0-5V analog| MCU_A
    BMS_A -->|CAN bus| CLUSTER_A[Instrument Cluster]
    MCU_A -->|CAN bus| CLUSTER_A
    BMS_A -->|Thermal sensor| PACK_A
    MOTOR_A -->|Hall sensors| MCU_A
    BRAKE_A[Brake Lever Switch] -->|Regen trigger| MCU_A
    OBC_A -.Service Point.-> SVC_A((Service Touchpoints))
    BMS_A -.Service Point.-> SVC_A
    MCU_A -.Service Point.-> SVC_A
    MOTOR_A -.Service Point.-> SVC_A
    THR_A -.Service Point.-> SVC_A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Arrows show the *direction of energy or signal flow*. Dashed arrows from each subsystem point to the central **Service Touchpoints** node — visually indicating that every subsystem is a *servicing access point*. In the lab record, students must label each arrow with the *type* of energy (AC, HV-DC, LV-DC, CAN-H/L).

---

## 4.2 Service Procedure Flowchart — KTU Lab Experiment Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Customer Complaint Recording] --> S2[Step 2: Visual Inspection of Vehicle]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: PPE and High-Voltage Safety Lockout]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Battery Pack Health Check]
    S4 --> S4a[4a: Measure Pack Voltage]
    S4 --> S4b[4b: Insulation Resistance Megger Test]
    S4 --> S4c[4c: Cell Voltage Imbalance Check]
    S4a --> S5[Step 5: BMS Fault Code Scan via OBD]
    S4b --> S5
    S4c --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: OBC Charging Test]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: DC-DC Converter 12V Output Test]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Throttle Calibration 0 to 5V]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Motor Phase Resistance and Hall Sensor Test]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Road Test and Final Sign-Off]
    S10 --> S11[Service Report Documentation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to *list the steps in servicing*, never start with "open the bonnet" — start with **safety lockout (Step 3)**. Examiners award marks specifically for **PPE usage** (insulated gloves, face shield, HV-insulated tools) and **lockout-tagout (LOTO)** procedure.

---

## 4.3 Subsystem Service Priority Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1((HIGH Voltage Safety)) --- P2((BMS Critical))
    P2 --- P3((Motor and MCU))
    P3 --- P4((OBC Charging))
    P4 --- P5((Aux 12V and Cluster))
    P5 --- P6((Mechanical Brakes Tyres))
```

This priority chain reflects the **risk-severity ordering** taught under AIS-156 service protocols.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q.** List the major sub-systems of an electric two-wheeler and state the function of the BMS.

**Model Answer:**

The major sub-systems are: (i) **Traction Battery Pack** — stores DC energy in Li-ion cells, (ii) **Battery Management System (BMS)** — monitors cell voltage, current, temperature, performs cell balancing, SOC/SOH estimation and protection cut-offs, (iii) **Motor Control Unit (MCU)** — converts DC bus to 3-phase AC for the traction motor, (iv) **BLDC/PMSM Traction Motor** — delivers mechanical torque, (v) **DC-DC Converter** — steps down HV to 12 V aux, (vi) **On-Board Charger** — AC→DC charging, (vii) **Throttle Encoder** — driver input, (viii) **Wiring Harness and CAN bus**.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q.** What is the significance of AIS-156 in E2W servicing?

**Model Answer:**

**AIS-156 (Automotive Industry Standard 156)** is the Indian regulatory standard governing the **safety requirements for traction battery packs** used in electric vehicles. It mandates **thermal propagation prevention**, **IP67 enclosure rating**, **insulation resistance ≥ 100 Ω/V**, **BMS-level cut-offs**, and **crash-disconnect mechanisms**. During servicing, AIS-156 compliance is verified by **megger insulation test**, **isolation monitoring**, and **BMS fault-log audit**. Any service workshop carrying out battery repairs must comply with AIS-156 to be certified under the FAME-II / PM E-Drive scheme.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model | Module Experiment 2 | 14 Marks]**

### Question A (Choice 1)

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the complete architecture of an electric two-wheeler. Label all power-flow arrows and signal-flow arrows distinctly. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The architecture consists of the following interconnected sub-systems:

1. **Traction Battery Pack (48/60/72 V, Li-ion NMC or LFP)** — primary energy reservoir, configured as series-parallel modules (e.g., 13S10P = 48 V / 30 Ah).
2. **BMS** — sits on the high-voltage bus, sampling every cell. It controls the **main contactor** and **pre-charge resistor**.
3. **DC Bus** — carries HV DC from pack to MCU. Typically equipped with **HVIL (High-Voltage Interlock Loop)** connector.
4. **MCU** — receives throttle input, executes FOC algorithm, generates SPWM signals for the 3-phase inverter (typically 6 MOSFETs/IGBTs).
5. **BLDC Motor** — out-runner or in-runner hub motor; produces back-EMF $E = k_e \omega$.
6. **DC-DC Converter** — non-isolated buck topology; output 12 V/5 A regulated.
7. **OBC** — PFC + LLC resonant converter, galvanically isolated, output matched to pack voltage.
8. **Auxiliary Loads** — 12 V cluster, headlamp (LED), horn, indicators.
9. **Throttle Encoder** — Hall-effect, 0.8 V idle to 4.2 V full throttle.
10. **CAN-bus (500 kbps)** — links BMS, MCU, OBC, cluster.

The block diagram (see Section 4.1) distinguishes *power flow* (solid arrows) from *signal flow* (dashed arrows), which is what KTU examiners award marks for.

**(b)** A 60 V / 30 Ah Li-ion electric scooter was reported with reduced range. Service measurements: pack delivers only 22 Ah on a 0.5C discharge test. Calculate the State of Health (SOH) and recommend the next service action. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify formula.**

$$
SOH = \dfrac{C_{actual}}{C_{rated}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
SOH = \dfrac{22}{30} \times 100 = 73.33 \%
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

Since $SOH < 80\%$, the pack has **degraded below the recommended service threshold**. Standard industry practice (and most OEM warranty terms) mandates **battery pack replacement** when SOH falls below **75–80%**.

**Step 4 — Recommended Service Action:**

(i) Generate a written **service quotation** for the customer with SOH data attached.
(ii) Offer a choice: (a) **full pack replacement**, or (b) **module-level replacement** if cell voltages show localised imbalance (one weak module dragging down the whole pack).
(iii) Update the **OEM service portal** with the SOH report for warranty traceability.
(iv) Advise the customer on **usage hygiene** — avoid 100% daily charging, park in shade, avoid deep discharge below 20%.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for **insulation resistance (megger) testing** of an electric two-wheeler battery pack. Mention the standard threshold and what action to take on failure. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Safety Lockout.** Switch off ignition, remove key, disconnect the main HV service plug, and wait **5 minutes** for the DC-link capacitors in the MCU to self-discharge. Wear **Class-0 HV-insulated gloves (1000 V rated)** and use **insulated tools only**.

**Step 2 — Verify Megger.** A **500 V DC megohmmeter** is used. Short the probe leads and confirm zero reading; open-circuit the probes and confirm infinite reading.

**Step 3 — Connect Probes.** Connect the **positive (red) probe** to the **HV positive terminal** of the pack, and the **negative (black) probe** to the **vehicle chassis (earth)**. Repeat the test for the **HV negative terminal** against chassis.

**Step 4 — Apply Test Voltage.** Press the test button for **30 seconds** (some standards require 60 s). Read the stabilized insulation resistance value.

**Step 5 — Compare Against Standard.** As per **AIS-156**, the threshold is:

$$
R_{ins} \geq 100 \,\Omega/V \times V_{nom}
$$

For a 60 V system, $R_{ins} \geq 6 \,k\Omega$. **Industry service practice** insists on **≥ 100 kΩ** for safety sign-off.

**Step 6 — Failure Action.** If $R_{ins} < 100 \,k\Omega$, the pack must be **quarantined**, the source of leakage identified (typically moisture ingress in the BMS housing, cracked HV cable insulation, or corroded connector), the fault rectified, and the test **re-performed** before returning the vehicle.

**(b)** During a charging test, a 60 V / 30 Ah pack took **5.5 hours to charge from 20% to 100%**, whereas the OEM rating is **3.5 hours**. Diagnose the possible causes and describe the verification tests. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagnosis — possible causes ranked by likelihood:**

1. **On-Board Charger (OBC) under-delivering current.** The OBC may have lost a phase in its PFC stage or one of its switching MOSFETs may be open. *Verification:* Measure the OBC output current with a DC clamp meter. Expected: ~10 A. If <7 A, OBC is faulty.
2. **Cell imbalance forcing premature CV absorption.** If one cell reaches 4.2 V early, the BMS enters CV mode prematurely, dragging total charging time. *Verification:* Read individual cell voltages via BMS diagnostic tool during charging. Spread should be ≤ 30 mV.
3. **Battery pack degradation (high internal resistance).** Aged cells have elevated IR, which reduces charge acceptance. *Verification:* Conduct a DC pulse test or capacity test (0.5C discharge to cutoff voltage).
4. **OBC cooling fan failure.** Most OBCs throttle output above 60 °C internal temperature. *Verification:* Feel the OBC enclosure temperature and check fan operation.

**Conclusion / Service Action:** Most likely the OBC is the prime suspect. Replace or repair the OBC, retest charging time, and document in the service report.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the safety lockout step** — examiners specifically look for **PPE, HVIL, and LOTO**. Omitting this is an automatic **−2 marks** deduction.
> 2. **Not labeling arrows in the block diagram** — unlabeled diagrams score zero. Always annotate "HV DC", "LV 12V", "CAN-H", "3-φ AC".
> 3. **Confusing SOC and SOH** — many students mix these up. **SOC = current charge level; SOH = lifetime health.** This is a common viva trap.
> 4. **Unit errors in energy/range calculation** — mixing Wh with Ah is a guaranteed mark deduction.
> 5. **Not citing AIS-156 / IEC standards** — answers without standards are considered "incomplete" by KTU examiners.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2: Servicing of Electric Two-Wheeler**

- 🔋 **Battery Pack** — measure pack voltage, individual cell voltages (imbalance ≤ 30 mV), insulation resistance (≥ 100 kΩ).
- 🛡️ **BMS Functions** — cell balancing, SOC, SOH, over/under voltage cut-off, thermal cut-off, CAN diagnostics.
- ⚡ **Charging Profile** — CC-CV; CC delivers bulk charge to 80–90% SOC, CV tops up to 100%.
- ⚙️ **Motor & MCU** — phase-to-phase resistance ≤ 0.5 Ω imbalance; Hall sensors must be 120° spaced.
- 🔌 **OBC** — output current clamp test; cooling-fan operation; PFC waveform check.
- 🔁 **DC-DC Converter** — output 13.5 ± 0.5 V; ripple < 100 mV pp.
- 🎚️ **Throttle** — 0.8 V idle to 4.2 V WOT; linearity check; return spring tension.
- 📜 **Standards** — **AIS-156 (battery safety)**, **IEC 62133-2 (cells)**, **IP67 (enclosure)**.
- 🧯 **Safety** — **Class-0 HV gloves, face shield, LOTO, HVIL**, wait 5 min for DC-link discharge.
- 📊 **SOH Threshold** — replace pack if SOH < 75–80%.
- 🔢 **Key formulas** — $E = V \times C_{Ah}$, $R_{range} = E_{usable}/E_{cons}$, $SOH = C_{act}/C_{rated}$, $R_{ins} \geq 100\,\Omega/V$.
- 📝 **Documentation** — every lab session must end with a signed **service report** including measured values, fault codes, and customer advisory.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servicing and testing of charging systems for electric vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL507 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Servicing and testing of charging systems for electric vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> The **Electric Vehicle (EV) Charging System** is a coordinated electromechanical and electronic infrastructure comprising the **Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE)**, the **On-Board Charger (OBC)** or **Off-Board DC Fast Charger**, the **Battery Management System (BMS)**, high-voltage cabling, connectors, and communication modules, whose collective function is to safely transfer, condition, and regulate electrical energy from the AC mains grid (or DC fast-charge station) into the **High-Voltage Traction Battery Pack** of an electric vehicle while continuously monitoring insulation, temperature, current, voltage, and handshake protocols.

For KTU 2024 Scheme purposes, the experiment targets three measurable competencies:

1. **Identify** the components of an EV charging system (per AIS-138 / IEC 61851).
2. **Service** the system by inspecting connectors, cables, cooling, and OBC filters.
3. **Test** electrical parameters (insulation resistance, ground continuity, control pilot signal, voltage, current, and SOC-based charging termination) using certified lab instruments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Fuel Station with a Conversation"

Imagine a petrol fuel station where you insert a nozzle into the car's tank. The station must speak to the car before fuel flows — the car must be ready, the nozzle must be the right shape, and the pump must shut off automatically when the tank is full. An **EV charging system works identically**, except:

- **The "fuel" is electricity** (AC or DC).
- **The "pump" is the EVSE (Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment)**.
- **The "nozzle" is the charging connector** (Type 2, CCS2, CHAdeMO, or Bharat standards).
- **The "conversation" is the handshake protocol** (Control Pilot + Proximity Pilot signalling defined by **IEC 61851-1**).

Just as a petrol nozzle has a trigger lock, the EV charging cable carries two low-voltage signal wires that perform the handshake — confirming cable integrity, vehicle readiness, ventilation needs, and maximum allowable current — *before* the high-voltage contactor closes.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standards to Memorise

The following values are **high-yield KTU board-exam constants** and must be known verbatim:

- **AC Grid Frequency:** **50 Hz** (India), **60 Hz** (US/Japan).
- **Standard AC Charging Voltages:** **230 V single-phase** (India Level 1), **415 V three-phase** (India Level 2).
- **DC Fast Charging Voltages:** **200 V – 1000 V DC** (per CCS2 standard).
- **Insulation Resistance Threshold (Safety):** **≥ 100 Ω/V** or **≥ 500 kΩ** (per IEC 61851-1 / AIS-138).
- **Control Pilot (CP) Signal:** **±12 V DC PWM** at **1 kHz** with duty cycle representing current capacity (10%–96%).
- **Proximity Pilot (PP) Resistance Values:** **1.5 kΩ** (13 A), **680 Ω** (20 A), **220 Ω** (32 A), **100 Ω** (63 A).
- **Bharat AC001 Connector Current:** **≤ 15 A**, Bharat DC001: **≤ 200 A** at **≤ 100 V DC** (per **AIS-138 Part 1 & 2**).
- **Communication Protocols:** **CAN 2.0B** (CHAdeMO, Bharat DC), **PLC (Power Line Communication)** (ISO 15118, CCS2).
- **Ambient Operating Temperature for Charging:** **−20 °C to +55 °C** (per AIS-138).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Any lab record mentioning "EV charging" must reference **AIS-138** (India-specific) and **IEC 61851-1** (international). These are the two pillars of all board questions on this experiment.

### 1.4 Lab Equipment Typically Used in KTU-Affiliated Workshops

> [!NOTE]
> The following instruments form the standard inventory for this experiment at KTU-affiliated colleges under the **2024 Scheme Automotive Electrical Lab**:

- **Digital Clamp Meter** (TRMS, ≥ 600 V DC capable)
- **Megohmmeter / Insulation Resistance Tester** (500 V DC / 1000 V DC, ≥ 1 GΩ range)
- **EV Charging System Analyzer** (e.g., **Charger Analyzer CHAdeMO/CCS/AC**, like the **Dewesoft SIRIUS** or **Omicron DANEO 400**)
- **Oscilloscope** (≥ 100 MHz, with isolated differential probes for high-voltage DC bus)
- **EV Simulator / Charging Station Tester** (e.g., **Metrel EurotestXC** or **Fluke FEV300**)
- **Thermal Imaging Camera** (for hotspot detection during charging)
- **Battery Simulator / Dummy Load** (programmable DC load bank)
- **CAN Bus Analyser** (for CCS/CHAdeMO/BMS diagnostics)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of EV Charging Systems

EV charging systems are classified along **three independent axes** — power level, current type, and connection location.

#### Axis 1: By Power Level (per **SAE J1772** and **IEC 61851-1**)

| Level | Type | Voltage | Power | Charging Time (for 30 kWh battery, approx.) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1 (Slow AC)** | AC single-phase | **230 V** | **≤ 3.3 kW** (≈ 15 A) | **9–11 hours** | Home / garage, Bharat AC001 |
| **Level 2 (Fast AC)** | AC three-phase | **415 V** | **7.4 – 22 kW** (16–32 A) | **2–5 hours** | Workplace / public AC stations |
| **Level 3 (DCFC)** | DC | **200–1000 V** | **50 – 350 kW** | **15–30 minutes (to 80%)** | Highway CCS2 stations |

#### Axis 2: By Current Type

- **AC Charging** — Uses the **vehicle's On-Board Charger (OBC)** to convert AC to DC internally. Power limited by OBC rating (typically 3.3 kW, 6.6 kW, 11 kW, or 22 kW).
- **DC Charging** — Bypasses the OBC; the EVSE itself contains the AC-DC rectifier and feeds DC directly to the traction battery. Power can be much higher (50–350 kW).

#### Axis 3: By Connection Method

- **Case A** — Cable attached to EV (e.g., portable Level 1 charger).
- **Case B** — Cable attached to EVSE (most public AC stations).
- **Case C** — Cable permanently attached to EVSE (DC fast charging).

### 2.2 Functional Block Architecture of an EV Charging System

The system has **five interacting subsystems** that the lab student must physically identify:

1. **EVSE (Supply Side)** — Contactor, RCD (Residual Current Device), Control Pilot oscillator, Proximity Pilot resistor network, protection relays.
2. **Vehicle Entry Port** — Inlet socket, locking actuator, PP/CP pin contacts.
3. **On-Board Charger (OBC)** — EMI filter → PFC boost rectifier → HV DC-DC converter → output filter → HV bus to battery.
4. **Battery Management System (BMS)** — Master + slave modules; monitors cell voltage, temperature, SOC (State of Charge), SOH (State of Health); issues contactor close/open commands.
5. **Traction Battery Pack** — Lithium-ion modules in series-parallel; thermal management (liquid or air-cooled).

### 2.3 The Charging Handshake — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **handshake is the single most-tested concept** in KTU viva and theory questions on this experiment. Memorise the **six states (A through F)** defined in **IEC 61851-1**:

| State | Vehicle Status | CP Voltage (typical) | Contactors | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **State A** | Standby (not connected) | **+12 V DC** | Open | Idle, no EV present |
| **State B** | Connected, not ready | **+9 V DC** | Open | Plug inserted, EV not requesting charge |
| **State C** | Connected, charging (ventilation not required) | **+6 V DC** | Closed | Active AC/DC charging |
| **State D** | Connected, charging (ventilation required) | **+3 V DC** | Closed | For lead-acid batteries only — not used in modern EVs |
| **State E** | Error — Short circuit on CP | **−12 V DC** | Open | EVSE disables output |
| **State F** | Error — No EVSE oscillator detected | **+12 V but no PWM** | Open | Cable integrity fault |

The **PWM duty cycle** carried on the CP line directly tells the vehicle the **maximum available current**, calculated as:

$$I_{\max} = \frac{\text{Duty Cycle (\%)}}{100} \times A_{\max,\text{cable}}$$

where $A_{\max,\text{cable}}$ is the **rated ampacity** of the charging cable.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** you may need to solve numerical KTU board questions on EV charging systems. Use `$\vert$` instead of `|` for any absolute-value bars.

| S.No | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = V \times I \times \cos\phi$ | $P$ = power (W), $V$ = voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $\cos\phi$ = power factor | Real AC charging power (OBC PF usually **≥ 0.99**) |
| 2 | $P_{DC} = V_{DC} \times I_{DC}$ | DC fast charging power | DCFC station output |
| 3 | $E_{batt} = \int P(t)\,dt$ | Energy stored (kWh) | Battery capacity rating |
| 4 | $t_{charge} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{\eta \times P_{charger}}$ | $t_{charge}$ = charging time, $\eta$ = round-trip efficiency | Computing charging duration |
| 5 | $C\text{-rate} = \dfrac{I_{charge}}{Q_{batt}}$ | $Q_{batt}$ = battery capacity (Ah) | Charging aggressiveness classification (1C, 2C, 3C…) |
| 6 | $I_{\max} = \dfrac{\text{Duty Cycle (\%)}}{100} \times I_{cable,\text{rated}}$ | From CP PWM duty cycle | Decoding EVSE current advertisement |
| 7 | $R_{insulation} = \dfrac{V_{test}}{I_{leak}}$ | Insulation resistance (Ω) | Safety testing per IEC 61851-1 |
| 8 | $R_{insulation,\min} = 100 \;\Omega/\text{V}$ | Minimum permissible insulation | Pass/fail criterion — must be **≥ 100 Ω/V of working voltage** |
| 9 | $\eta_{system} = \dfrac{E_{delivered}}{E_{drawn}} \times 100\%$ | System efficiency | EVSE + OBC + battery combined |
| 10 | $V_{cell,\max} = 4.2 \text{ V (Li-ion NMC)}, \; 4.35 \text{ V (NCA)}$ | Cell cutoff voltage | BMS voltage threshold |
| 11 | $V_{pack} = N_s \times V_{cell,\text{nominal}}$ | $N_s$ = number of cells in series | Pack voltage design |
| 12 | $Q_{heat} = I^2 \times R_{cable} \times t$ | Heat loss in charging cable | Cable sizing & thermal testing |
| 13 | $P_{cool,\text{required}} = \dfrac{Q_{heat}}{t}$ | Cooling system duty | OBC liquid cooling design |
| 14 | $\text{SOC}_{\text{new}} = \text{SOC}_{\text{old}} + \dfrac{I \times t \times 100}{Q_{batt} \times 3600}$ | State of Charge update (%) | BMS Coulomb counting |
| 15 | $E_{\text{vehicle range}} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{\text{kWh/km consumption}}$ | Driving range (km) | Indirect — for energy-balance problems |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles covered here are **not academic abstractions**. They are used by:

- **Tata Motors, Mahindra Electric, Ather Energy, Ola Electric** — for factory diagnostics on Tiago EV, Nexon EV, XUV400, e-scooter charging systems.
- **BPCL, IOCL, Tata Power, Statiq, Ather Grid** — for public EVSE commissioning and AIS-138 certification.
- **Automotive service technicians** — to interpret OBD-II DTCs (Diagnostic Trouble Codes) like **P0D07** (battery charger performance), **P0D08** (charging system voltage low), **U0100** (lost communication with BMS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Tip:** When working in an EV service bay, always wear **Class 0 insulated gloves (rated to 1000 V AC / 1500 V DC)** and use an **isolated oscilloscope** when probing the HV DC bus. A regular scope probe tied to mains earth will create a **ground loop that can destroy the OBC IGBTs** during a fault condition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Charging Time as a Function of Battery Capacity and Charger Power

This is the **most common numerical problem** in KTU board papers on this topic. We derive the closed-form expression with **every algebraic step explicitly written out**.

**Given:**
- Battery pack nominal energy: $E_{batt}$ in **kWh**
- Charger rated output power: $P_{ch}$ in **kW**
- System (EVSE + OBC + battery) round-trip efficiency: $\eta$ (dimensionless, typically **0.85 to 0.92**)

**Step 1 — Energy Balance.**
The energy that must be drawn from the grid, $E_{grid}$, must equal the energy delivered to the battery divided by the system efficiency (because losses occur in EVSE contactors, OBC PFC stage, OBC DC-DC stage, cable heating, and BMS auxiliary loads).

$$E_{grid} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{\eta}$$

**Step 2 — Time-Energy Equivalence.**
Energy equals power multiplied by time, so the charging time $t_{charge}$ (in hours) is simply:

$$t_{charge} = \dfrac{E_{grid}}{P_{ch}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2.**

$$t_{charge} = \dfrac{E_{batt}/\eta}{P_{ch}} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{\eta \cdot P_{ch}}$$

This is the **master charging time equation**. Box it.

**Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example.**

A **Tata Nexon EV** has a **30.2 kWh** battery. It is charged by a **7.2 kW AC Level-2 EVSE** through its **on-board charger (efficiency 92%)**. Find the time to charge from **0% to 100% SOC**.

- $E_{batt} = 30.2 \text{ kWh}$
- $P_{ch} = 7.2 \text{ kW}$
- $\eta = 0.92$

$$t_{charge} = \dfrac{30.2}{0.92 \times 7.2}$$

$$= \dfrac{30.2}{6.624} \approx 4.56 \text{ hours}$$

**Answer:** Approximately **4 hours 34 minutes**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Decoding the Control Pilot PWM Duty Cycle

The CP line is a **1 kHz square wave** whose **duty cycle D** (in %) communicates the maximum permissible current to the EV.

**Step 1 — IEC 61851-1 Current Encoding Rule.**
The maximum available current $I_{\max}$ is given by a piecewise linear function of the duty cycle:

$$
I_{\max} = \begin{cases}
\text{No charging permitted} & \text{if } D < 3\% \\[4pt]
\dfrac{D \times 0.6}{100} \;\text{(A)} & \text{if } 3\% \leq D \leq 7\% \\[8pt]
\dfrac{(D - 7) \times 0.6}{100} + 0.6 \;\text{(A)} & \text{if } 7\% < D \leq 80\% \\[8pt]
\text{No charging permitted} & \text{if } 85\% < D \leq 100\%
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplified Linear Approximation for Quick KTU Calculations.**
For exam purposes, KTU uses the **simpler formula**:

$$I_{\max} \;\text{(A)} \approx \dfrac{D}{100} \times I_{cable,\text{rated}}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example.**

A **CCS2 cable** is rated at **32 A**. The oscilloscope on the CP pin shows a **PWM duty cycle of 50%**. What is the maximum current the EV will draw?

$$I_{\max} = \dfrac{50}{100} \times 32 = 16 \text{ A}$$

**Answer:** The EV will draw at most **16 A** (≈ 6.6 kW on a 415 V three-phase supply, or ≈ 3.7 kW on 230 V single-phase).

### 3.3 Derivation: Insulation Resistance Pass/Fail Criterion

Per **IEC 61851-1, Clause 6.1**, the insulation resistance of the HV system must be tested using a **500 V DC megohmmeter** (or 1000 V DC for > 500 V systems).

**Step 1 — Test Setup.**
Disconnect the HV battery contactors. Apply test voltage between:
- HV+ bus and chassis ground
- HV− bus and chassis ground
- HV+ and HV− together (inter-pole resistance)

**Step 2 — Pass/Fail Threshold.**
The minimum acceptable insulation resistance is:

$$R_{insulation,\min} = 100 \;\Omega/\text{V} \times V_{working,\max}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example.**
A 400 V EV system is tested.

$$R_{insulation,\min} = 100 \times 400 = 40{,}000 \;\Omega = 40 \text{ k}\Omega$$

The megohmmeter must read **at least 40 kΩ** for the system to pass. (Note: in practice, a healthy EV reads **≥ 1 MΩ to 10 GΩ**, so 40 kΩ is a very conservative regulatory floor.)

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting the Correct Test for a Given Fault

The following matrix guides a KTU lab student in selecting the appropriate test when a fault symptom is given:

| Symptom Observed | Most Likely Fault | First Test to Run | Instrument | Pass Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| EV does not respond to plug-in | CP line short / open | CP voltage & duty cycle test | Oscilloscope / Multimeter | 1 kHz PWM, ±12 V |
| Charging stops after 2 minutes | Overtemperature | Thermal scan of OBC & connectors | Thermal camera | Connector ≤ **90 °C**, OBC ≤ **85 °C** |
| Slow charging (only 1 kW instead of 7 kW) | EVSE derating | CP duty cycle measurement | Oscilloscope | Duty cycle should match EVSE rating |
| EVSE trips ground fault (RCD) | Insulation breakdown | Megger test | 500 V megohmmeter | ≥ **100 Ω/V** |
| "Charger not compatible" error | PP resistor mismatch | PP resistance measurement | Multimeter | 1.5 kΩ / 680 Ω / 220 Ω / 100 Ω |
| Battery SOC stuck at 80% | BMS taper charging | CAN bus read of BMS | CAN analyser | SOC climbs with time |
| Intermittent charging | Loose contactor / arcing | Voltage drop test at connector | Clamp meter with mV mode | Drop ≤ **500 mV** at rated current |
| BMS reports isolation fault | Coolant leak on HV cables | Visual + insulation test | Megger + borescope | ≥ 100 Ω/V |

### 3.5 Derivation: Coulomb-Counting SOC Update (BMS Algorithm)

The **coulomb-counting method** is the industry-standard way BMS estimates SOC. The derivation:

**Step 1 — Definition of SOC.**

$$\text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}(t_0) + \dfrac{\int_{t_0}^{t} I_{batt}(\tau)\,d\tau}{Q_{batt,\text{rated}}}$$

**Step 2 — For Constant Charging Current I.**
If $I$ is approximately constant over a time interval $\Delta t$:

$$\Delta \text{SOC} = \dfrac{I \times \Delta t}{Q_{batt}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.**
A **60 Ah** battery is charged at a **constant 30 A** for **1 hour**.

$$\Delta \text{SOC} = \dfrac{30 \times 1}{60} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**Answer:** The SOC rises by **50 percentage points** (e.g., from 20% to 70%).

This is why EVs are commonly rated with the **C-rate** notation: this battery is being charged at **0.5C** (i.e., 30 A = 0.5 × 60 A), and 1C charging would mean 60 A, completing a full charge in **1 hour**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Full EV Charging System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[AC Mains Grid 230V / 415V 50Hz] --> B[EVSE Supply Equipment]
    B --> B1[Residual Current Device RCD 30mA Type B]
    B1 --> B2[Main Contactor K1]
    B2 --> B3[Control Pilot Oscillator 1kHz PWM]
    B2 --> B4[Proximity Pilot Resistor Network]
    B3 --> C[Charging Cable CCS2 or Type 2]
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Vehicle Inlet Socket]
    D --> E[On-Board Charger OBC]
    E --> E1[EMI Input Filter]
    E1 --> E2[PFC Boost Rectifier]
    E2 --> E3[Isolated HV DC-DC Converter]
    E3 --> F[Battery Management System BMS]
    F --> F1[Cell Voltage Monitor]
    F --> F2[Cell Temperature Monitor]
    F --> F3[SOC and SOH Estimator]
    F --> F4[Contactor Control Unit]
    F --> G[HV Traction Battery Pack]
    G --> H[Thermal Management Loop]
    H --> H1[Coolant Pump]
    H --> H2[Chiller and Radiator]
    B3 -.CP Signal.-> F
    F -.CAN or PLC.-> E
    F -.HVIL Command.-> B2
```

**Diagram Description (for lab record):** The AC mains enters the EVSE where an RCD provides ground-fault protection, the contactor K1 closes only after the CP handshake is valid, and the **Control Pilot (CP)** and **Proximity Pilot (PP)** lines traverse the cable to the vehicle inlet. The vehicle's OBC converts AC to DC, the BMS supervises every cell, and only then is the battery contactor closed to allow current flow.

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Charging Handshake Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant GRID as AC Mains
    participant EVSE as EVSE Station
    participant CP as Control Pilot Line
    participant OBC as On-Board Charger
    participant BMS as Battery Management System
    participant BAT as HV Battery Pack
    GRID->>EVSE: Energize (idle)
    EVSE->>CP: Generate 1 kHz PWM, +12V State A
    Note over EVSE: Plug inserted by user
    EVSE->>CP: PP resistor detected, voltage drops to +9V State B
    CP->>OBC: OBC reads PWM, decodes I_max
    OBC->>BMS: Request permission (CAN: CHRG_START_REQ)
    BMS->>BMS: Self-check cells, isolation, temperature
    BMS-->>OBC: Permission granted (CAN: CHRG_START_ACK)
    OBC->>EVSE: Pull CP to +6V via diode (State C)
    EVSE->>GRID: Close contactor K1, energize cable
    EVSE->>OBC: AC mains available
    OBC->>BAT: Convert AC-DC, ramp current up
    BMS->>BMS: Monitor cell voltage, temp, SOC
    BAT->>BMS: Cell data (CAN every 100 ms)
    Note over BAT: SOC reaches 100 percent
    BMS->>OBC: Terminate charge (CAN: CHRG_STOP)
    OBC->>EVSE: Reduce CP current, voltage rises to +9V
    EVSE->>GRID: Open contactor K1
    Note over EVSE: User unplugs cable, system returns to State A
```

**Diagram Description:** This sequence diagram traces every message exchanged during a typical charging session, matching the **IEC 61851-1 / ISO 15118** state machine. Students must annotate this in their lab record with timing values (handshake typically completes in **< 5 seconds**).

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Lab Testing Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start - Receive EV for Service] --> S1[Visual Inspection of Connectors and Cables]
    S1 --> S2{Damage or Burn Marks?}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Replace Connector and Retest]
    S2 -->|No| S4[Insulation Resistance Test 500V DC Megger]
    S4 --> S5{R greater than 100 ohm per V?}
    S5 -->|No| S6[Locate Insulation Fault using Sub-sectioning]
    S5 -->|Yes| S7[Ground Continuity Test between PE pin and Chassis]
    S7 --> S8{Resistance less than 0.1 ohm?}
    S8 -->|No| S9[Repair Protective Earth Wiring]
    S8 -->|Yes| S10[Connect EVSE Analyzer to Inlet]
    S10 --> S11[Verify CP Signal 1 kHz PWM and Duty Cycle]
    S11 --> S12{Duty Cycle within 10 to 96 percent?}
    S12 -->|No| S13[Replace EVSE or Repair CP Wire]
    S12 -->|Yes| S14[Verify PP Resistance matches Cable Rating]
    S14 --> S15{PP within plus or minus 5 percent of Nominal?}
    S15 -->|No| S16[Replace Cable Assembly]
    S15 -->|Yes| S17[Initiate Charging Session]
    S17 --> S18[Monitor OBC Temperature with Thermal Camera]
    S18 --> S19{Temperature below 85 deg C?}
    S19 -->|No| S20[Service OBC Cooling System]
    S19 -->|Yes| S21[Read SOC and Charging Current]
    S21 --> S22{Current matches EVSE Rated Output?}
    S22 -->|No| S23[Diagnose BMS Derating or OBC Fault]
    S22 -->|Yes| S24[All Tests Pass - Sign off Lab Record]
```

**Diagram Description:** This flowchart represents the **complete KTU lab testing protocol** for an EV charging system. Every decision diamond corresponds to a **measurable pass/fail criterion**, and every rectangle represents a **physical test action** the student must perform and record observations against.

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Charging Connector Pinout Reference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CCS2_Combo [CCS2 Combo 2 Connector]
        P1[Pin 1 CP - Control Pilot]
        P2[Pin 2 PP - Proximity Pilot]
        P3[Pin 3 PE - Protective Earth]
        P4[Pin 4 N - Neutral AC]
        P5[Pin 5 L1 - Line 1 AC]
        P6[Pin 6 L2 - Line 2 AC]
        P7[Pin 7 L3 - Line 3 AC]
        P8[DC+ - High Voltage DC Positive]
        P9[DC- - High Voltage DC Negative]
    end
    P1 --> CP_Logic[CP Logic reads PWM duty cycle]
    P2 --> PP_Logic[PP Logic reads cable current rating]
    P3 --> Earth_Bond[Chassis Earth Bond]
    P4 --> AC_Mains[AC Mains for OBC Input]
    P5 --> AC_Mains
    P6 --> AC_Mains
    P7 --> AC_Mains
    P8 --> HV_Bus[High Voltage DC Bus to Battery]
    P9 --> HV_Bus
```

**Diagram Description:** The **CCS2 (Combined Charging System 2)** is the dominant Indian and European standard. It integrates AC Level 1/2 pins (upper portion) and two large DC pins (lower portion) in a single physical connector. Lab students must **identify each pin by number** when servicing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** Define the term **Control Pilot (CP)** signal as per **IEC 61851-1**. State its frequency and how the duty cycle is interpreted by the vehicle.

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

The **Control Pilot (CP)** is a low-voltage communication line between the **EVSE (charging station)** and the **electric vehicle**, used to safely coordinate the start, control, and termination of a charging session.

- **Frequency:** The CP signal is a **±12 V DC square wave at 1 kHz (1000 Hz)**.
- **Duty Cycle Meaning:** The PWM **duty cycle**, ranging from **10 % to 96 %**, communicates the **maximum permissible charging current** the EV may draw from the EVSE.
- **States:** The average voltage level (12 V → 9 V → 6 V → 3 V) indicates the **vehicle state** — Standby (A), Connected (B), Charging (C), and Ventilation-required (D).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for frequency/PWM, 1 mark for duty cycle meaning.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** Differentiate between **AC Level 1**, **AC Level 2**, and **DC Fast Charging** with respect to voltage, power, and typical charging time.

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Parameter | AC Level 1 | AC Level 2 | DC Fast Charging |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage** | 230 V AC, 1-phase | 415 V AC, 3-phase | 200 – 1000 V DC |
| **Power** | ≤ 3.3 kW | 7.4 – 22 kW | 50 – 350 kW |
| **Charging Location** | On-board charger | On-board charger | Off-board rectifier |
| **Time for 30 kWh** | 9 – 11 hours | 2 – 5 hours | 15 – 30 min (to 80 %) |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per row of comparison.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of an EV charging system**. Describe the role of the **OBC**, **EVSE**, and **BMS** in detail, and explain the **charging handshake protocol** as per IEC 61851-1.

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Component Roles (7 marks)**

The EV charging system consists of the following major blocks:

1. **EVSE (Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment):** The supply-side device that delivers AC or DC power, houses the **RCD**, main **contactor**, **Control Pilot oscillator**, and **Proximity Pilot** detection circuit. It performs the safety handshake before energising the cable.

2. **Charging Cable & Connector:** Carries AC or DC power along with CP, PP, and PE conductors. The connector pinout is dictated by the standard — Type 2, CCS2, CHAdeMO, or Bharat AC001.

3. **Vehicle Inlet & Locking Actuator:** Receives the connector and locks it during charging.

4. **On-Board Charger (OBC):** For AC charging, the OBC converts AC mains to DC via an **EMI filter → PFC boost rectifier → galvanically isolated HV DC-DC converter → output filter**. Modern OBCs achieve **≥ 92 % efficiency** and PF ≥ 0.99.

5. **Battery Management System (BMS):** The brain of the battery pack. Performs **cell voltage monitoring (typically every 10 ms), cell temperature monitoring, SOC/SOH estimation, balancing (passive or active), and contactor control**. The BMS will not allow charging if any cell is outside the safe window (**2.5 V – 4.2 V** for NMC Li-ion).

6. **Traction Battery Pack & Thermal Management:** Stores energy (e.g., 30.2 kWh in Nexon EV). Cooled by **liquid glycol loop** or air, with strict temperature limits (typically **15 °C – 35 °C** optimal operating window).

*(7 marks: 2 marks for block diagram, 5 marks for component explanation.)*

**Part (b) — Charging Handshake Protocol (7 marks)**

The handshake per **IEC 61851-1** proceeds as follows:

1. **State A — Standby:** No vehicle connected. CP pin reads **+12 V DC**, no PWM present.
2. **Plug-In Detection:** User inserts the connector. The PP line resistor (e.g., **220 Ω** for 32 A cable) is detected by the EVSE.
3. **State B — Connected:** The vehicle pulls the CP voltage down to **+9 V** via a sensing circuit, indicating "connected but not ready."
4. **EV Readiness:** The BMS performs self-checks (isolation, cell voltages, temperatures) and signals readiness.
5. **State C — Charging:** The vehicle pulls the CP voltage down further to **+6 V DC** via a **diode bridge**, signalling "ready to charge." The EVSE closes the main contactor and energises the cable.
6. **PWM Communication:** The **1 kHz PWM duty cycle** carries the cable's current rating. The vehicle's OBC ramps the current up to this limit.
7. **State D — Ventilation Required (legacy):** +3 V state, applicable only to lead-acid charging.
8. **Termination:** When SOC = 100 % or a fault occurs, the BMS signals "stop charging," the OBC ramps current down, the CP voltage returns to +9 V, and the contactor opens.

The six IEC states (A, B, C, D, E, F) provide **fail-safe operation** — any detected anomaly forces the system back to State A.

*(7 marks: 6 × 1 mark for state descriptions, 1 mark for safety summary.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** Describe the **complete testing procedure** for an EV charging system in a service bay. Include tests for **insulation resistance, ground continuity, control pilot signal, charging current, and OBC temperature**. Mention the instruments used and the **pass/fail criteria** per AIS-138 / IEC 61851-1.

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**Part (a) — Safety and Insulation Tests (7 marks)**

The complete testing procedure follows the **AIS-138 / IEC 61851-1** standard:

1. **Visual Inspection (Pre-Test):**
   - Inspect connector pins for burn marks, deformation, ingress of water (IP rating verification).
   - Check cable jacket for cuts, abrasions.
   - Verify locking actuator function.

2. **Insulation Resistance Test:**
   - Instrument: **500 V DC Megohmmeter (Megger)** for 400 V systems; **1000 V DC** for higher-voltage packs.
   - Connect megger leads between **HV+ bus and chassis**, then **HV− bus and chassis**, then **HV+ and HV−**.
   - **Pass Criterion:** $R_{insulation} \geq 100 \;\Omega/\text{V} \times V_{working}$
   - For a 400 V system: **≥ 40 kΩ** minimum (practical healthy reading: **≥ 1 MΩ**).

3. **Ground Continuity Test (PE Bond):**
   - Instrument: **Low-resistance ohmmeter** or **Kelvin bridge**.
   - Measure between the **EVSE PE pin** and **vehicle chassis ground**.
   - **Pass Criterion:** $R_{PE} \leq 0.1 \;\Omega$.

4. **Control Pilot (CP) Signal Test:**
   - Instrument: **Isolated oscilloscope** or **EVSE analyser**.
   - Measure frequency (should be **1 kHz ± 5 %**), amplitude (**±12 V DC**), and **duty cycle (10 – 96 %)**.
   - Decode the duty cycle into maximum permitted current using:
   $$I_{\max} = \dfrac{D}{100} \times I_{cable,\text{rated}}$$

5. **Proximity Pilot (PP) Test:**
   - Measure the PP-pin resistance to ground.
   - **Expected values:** **1.5 kΩ (13 A), 680 Ω (20 A), 220 Ω (32 A), 100 Ω (63 A)**.
   - Pass Criterion: within **±5 %** of nominal.

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per test × 4 tests, 1 mark for visual inspection.)*

**Part (b) — Performance and Thermal Tests (7 marks)**

6. **Charging Current & Voltage Measurement:**
   - Instrument: **TRMS Clamp meter** for AC current; **Hall-effect DC clamp** for DC bus current.
   - Connect a **battery simulator / DC load bank** if performing off-vehicle test.
   - **Pass Criterion:** Measured current within **±10 %** of EVSE-rated output at steady state.

7. **OBC Temperature Monitoring:**
   - Instrument: **Thermal imaging camera** or **K-type thermocouple logger**.
   - Monitor OBC heatsink, output inductor, and DC bus capacitors during a **30-minute continuous charge**.
   - **Pass Criterion:** Surface temperature **≤ 85 °C** on OBC; connector pins **≤ 90 °C**.

8. **BMS Communication Test:**
   - Instrument: **CAN bus analyser** (e.g., PEAK PCAN-USB, Vector VN1610).
   - Monitor CAN frames at **500 kbps** for CHAdeMO or **125 kbps – 1 Mbps** for CCS.
   - Verify that BMS reports **CHRG_START_ACK**, **cell voltage values**, **temperature values**, and **CHRG_STOP** at full SOC.

9. **Fault Insertion Test (Optional, advanced labs):**
   - Simulate CP short circuit (verify EVSE opens contactor within **< 100 ms**).
   - Simulate over-temperature (verify OBC derates current).

10. **Documentation:**
    - Record all measurements in the **lab record** with date, instrument serial numbers, ambient temperature, and **technician signature**.
    - Cross-reference against AIS-138 / IEC 61851-1.

*(7 marks: 2 marks each for current/voltage, OBC temp, BMS comm; 1 mark for documentation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing OBC and EVSE roles** — Students often incorrectly say the "charger is in the car." For **AC charging, the OBC is in the car**; for **DC fast charging, the rectifier is in the EVSE**. Examiners specifically deduct for this.
> 2. **Wrong CP voltage values** — Memorising **+12 V, +9 V, +6 V, +3 V** for states A–D is mandatory; mixing these up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 3. **Forgetting the insulation resistance formula** — Students often quote just "1 MΩ" without showing $100 \;\Omega/\text{V} \times V_{working}$.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** — Every 14-mark question on this topic **must have a labelled block diagram**; missing one typically costs 2 marks.
> 5. **No mention of standards** — Always cite **AIS-138** (Indian) and **IEC 61851-1** (international); examiners reward this explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Block — Memorise Before Exam**

**Concepts & Definitions**
- ☐ EVSE = Supply-side equipment; OBC = Vehicle-side AC-DC converter (for AC charging only).
- ☐ Three charging levels by power: Level 1 (≤ 3.3 kW), Level 2 (≤ 22 kW), Level 3 DCFC (50 – 350 kW).
- ☐ Six IEC 61851-1 states: A (12 V), B (9 V), C (6 V), D (3 V), E (−12 V), F (no PWM).
- ☐ CP signal: 1 kHz PWM, ±12 V, duty cycle encodes current.
- ☐ PP resistors: 1.5 kΩ (13 A), 680 Ω (20 A), 220 Ω (32 A), 100 Ω (63 A).

**Key Formulas**
- ☐ $t_{charge} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{\eta \cdot P_{ch}}$
- ☐ $I_{\max} = \dfrac{D}{100} \times I_{cable,\text{rated}}$
- ☐ $R_{insulation,\min} = 100 \;\Omega/\text{V} \times V_{working}$
- ☐ $\Delta \text{SOC} = \dfrac{I \cdot \Delta t}{Q_{batt}} \times 100\%$
- ☐ $P_{DC} = V_{DC} \times I_{DC}$, $P_{AC} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$

**Testing & Servicing**
- ☐ Always use **Class 0 insulated gloves** and **isolated oscilloscope probes** for HV work.
- ☐ **Insulation test** uses 500 V DC megger; pass ≥ 100 Ω/V.
- ☐ **Ground continuity** must be ≤ 0.1 Ω.
- ☐ **Connector pin temp** ≤ 90 °C; OBC heatsink ≤ 85 °C.
- ☐ **CAN bus** is the diagnostic backbone (500 kbps for CHAdeMO).

**Standards to Cite in Answers**
- ☐ **AIS-138 Part 1** — AC charging (Bharat AC001).
- ☐ **AIS-138 Part 2** — DC charging (Bharat DC001).
- ☐ **IEC 61851-1** — International conductive charging system.
- ☐ **ISO 15118** — PLC-based high-level communication (used by CCS2).
- ☐ **SAE J1772** — North American AC/DC connector standard.
- ☐ **GB/T 27930** — Chinese DC charging communication protocol.

**Lab Viva One-Liners**
- ☐ "What is the maximum DC fast charging voltage under CCS2?" → **1000 V DC**.
- ☐ "What is the typical PF of an EV OBC?" → **≥ 0.99**.
- ☐ "What is the OBC efficiency?" → **92 – 95 %**.
- ☐ "What is the typical end-of-charge cell voltage?" → **4.2 V (NMC)**.
- ☐ "How long does the CP handshake take?" → **< 5 seconds**.
- ☐ "Name the Indian connector for low-power DC." → **Bharat DC001** (15 A, 100 V, 1.5 kW max).

**Common Indian EV Service Centre Tools**
- ☐ **Charger Analyzer (Dewesoft / Omicron / Metrel)**
- ☐ **Megohmmeter 1 kV**
- ☐ **TRMS Clamp Meter (Fluke 376 FC or equivalent)**
- ☐ **Thermal Camera (FLIR E5 / Testo 875)**
- ☐ **CAN Analyser (PEAK PCAN-USB / Vector VN1610)**
- ☐ **Insulated Gloves (Class 0, 1000 V rated)**
- ☐ **HV Warning Signs & Lockout-Tagout Kit**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on this topic, structure your answer in **two clean parts** (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**, include **at least one diagram**, cite the **AIS-138 or IEC 61851-1 standard**, and **write units explicitly** with every numerical value. Examiners reward methodological clarity far more than memorised text.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamometer testing for electric two-wheelers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUL507 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Dynamometer testing for electric two-wheelers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Dynamometer testing** of an electric two-wheeler is a standardized laboratory procedure in which the vehicle is operated on a chassis dynamometer (a stationary "rolling road" or treadmill for vehicles) to measure its real-world performance parameters under simulated road-load conditions. The dynamometer applies a controlled, programmable load to the drive wheel that mathematically replicates the aerodynamic drag, rolling resistance, and inertial forces the vehicle would experience during actual on-road operation. Critical parameters measured include the **maximum motor torque**, **peak wheel power**, **acceleration profile**, **top speed**, **gradeability**, **energy consumption (Wh/km)**, and **all-electric range (AER)** as stipulated under AIS-040 (Rev. 1) and ISO 13064 for L-category electric powertrains. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment falls under the practical verification of *Performance Evaluation of Electric Vehicles* and is directly mapped to **CO2 (Analyze)** and **CO3 (Evaluate)** of the PCAUL507 course outcomes. Students must be able to identify, connect, and interpret outputs from a chassis dynamometer rig applied to an EV two-wheeler. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine pushing a child on a swing — to keep the swing moving at a constant arc, you must push with exactly the right amount of force to overcome air resistance and friction. A **dynamometer does the exact same job**, except the "child" is the electric two-wheeler's driven wheel: - **The swing** = The vehicle's drive wheel - **The air resistance + chain friction** = The dynamometer's braking load (absorber) - **You, pushing** = The electric motor pushing the wheel forward - **The force you feel at your palms** = The **torque** the dynamometer measures - **The speed of the swing** = The **rotational speed** of the roller In essence, the dynamometer is a controllable mechanical "brake" that lets engineers *quantify* how hard the motor is working without ever leaving the laboratory floor. Instead of driving the bike through city traffic, the bike simply drives a giant steel drum indoors while the drum pushes back with a precisely known force. ## 1.3 Classification of Dynamometers Used in Two-Wheeler EV Labs | Type of Dynamometer | Operating Principle | Suitability for EV 2W | Key Advantage | |---|---|---|---| | **Eddy Current Dynamometer (ECD)** | Magnetic field induces eddy currents in a rotating disc, creating opposing torque | **Most commonly used** in KTU-affiliated labs | Smooth, stepless load control | | **Regenerative (AC) Dynamometer** | Motor-generator acts as load, feeds energy back to grid | High-end automotive R&D | Energy efficient, very high accuracy | | **Hydraulic Dynamometer** | Fluid shear between rotor and stator | Heavy commercial EVs | Very high absorptive capacity | | **Powder Brake Dynamometer** | Magnetic powder locks against rotor | Cost-effective, small labs | Simple, low cost | > [!NOTE] > For the KTU PCAUL507 lab, the **single-roller chassis dynamometer with eddy current absorber** is the standard rig. The two-wheeler's rear wheel rests on a calibrated steel drum of known moment of inertia, and a torque arm measures the reaction force. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Chassis Dynamometer The system operates on a closed-loop **force-velocity equilibrium** principle. The five sequential stages of operation are: 1. **Mechanical Coupling:** The rear wheel of the two-wheeler is positioned on the rotating drum and a tie-down harness secures the front wheel, preventing translational motion. 2. **Sensor Initialization:** A rotary encoder (on the drum shaft) measures wheel speed; a load cell (on the torque arm) measures reaction force; current and voltage transducers measure pack output; an RPM sensor reads motor speed. 3. **Road-Load Simulation:** The dynamometer controller applies a programmed load that emulates **three resistive forces**: - **Rolling Resistance:** $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ - **Aerodynamic Drag:** $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ - **Grade Resistance:** $F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ 4. **Data Acquisition:** A DAQ (Data Acquisition) system samples signals at **≥ 10 Hz** per channel to capture transient events like acceleration bursts. 5. **Performance Output:** Torque, power, energy, and range curves are plotted against speed and time. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Load Component - **Rolling resistance** exists because rubber deforms as it rolls, dissipating energy as heat. It is **linear with mass and speed-independent** to a first approximation. - **Aerodynamic drag** scales with the **square of velocity**, which is why high-speed cruising drains the battery disproportionately fast. This is the dominant loss above ~40 km/h. - **Grade resistance** simulates hill climbing without needing an actual hill. On a 10% grade ($\theta = 5.73°$), this force equals roughly 10% of the vehicle's weight. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Typical Range (E2W) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Drum surface speed | $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N / 60$ | m/s | 0 – 25 m/s | | 2 | Vehicle speed from roller | $V = (60 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot N) / (1000 \cdot G)$ | km/h | 0 – 90 km/h | | 3 | Wheel torque (from force) | $T = F \cdot L$ | N·m | 5 – 80 N·m | | 4 | Wheel power | $P = (2\pi \cdot N \cdot T) / 60$ | W (or kW) | 0.25 – 5 kW | | 5 | Rolling resistance | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | N | 15 – 40 N | | 6 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | N | 0 – 35 N | | 7 | Total road load | $F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{grade}$ | N | 15 – 75 N | | 8 | Equivalent test mass inertia | $I_{eq} = m_{eq} \cdot (r_d)^2$ | kg·m² | 8 – 25 kg·m² | | 9 | Specific energy consumption | $E_{sp} = (V_{bat} \cdot I \cdot t) / d$ | Wh/km | 25 – 60 Wh/km | | 10 | All-Electric Range | $R = (C_{bat} \cdot V_{nom}) / (E_{sp} \cdot 1000)$ | km | 60 – 150 km | | 11 | Acceleration force | $F_{acc} = m \cdot a$ | N | 50 – 250 N | | 12 | Gradeability | $\theta = \arcsin(F_{tract} / (m \cdot g))$ | degrees | 7° – 18° | > [!NOTE] > **Where:** $D$ = drum diameter, $N$ = drum RPM, $G$ = gear ratio, $L$ = torque arm length, $r_d$ = dynamic rolling radius, $\rho$ = air density (**1.225 kg/m³**), $C_d$ = drag coefficient (≈ 0.6 for a scooter), $A$ = frontal area, $C_{bat}$ = battery capacity (Ah), $V_{nom}$ = nominal voltage (V). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Dynamometer testing is the **backbone of EV homologation** before a vehicle is sold. Every manufacturer — Ola Electric, Ather, TVS, Hero Electric — runs hundreds of hours of dyno testing to certify the AIS-040 (Rev. 1) range and energy consumption numbers printed in their brochures. In a KTU lab context, this experiment bridges the gap between *theoretical motor equations* (e.g., back-EMF $E = K_e \cdot \omega$) and the *real-world losses* (windage, tire slip, drivetrain friction) that no classroom equation can capture. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Conversion of Drum RPM to Vehicle Speed The relationship between the rotational speed of the dynamometer drum and the linear speed of the vehicle is foundational. Every KTU numerical on this topic starts here. **Given:** Drum diameter $D$ in meters, drum speed $N$ in RPM. **Step 1 — Circumferential distance per revolution:** Each rotation of the drum advances the contact patch by one circumference. $$d_{rev} = \pi \cdot D \quad \text{[meters per revolution]}$$ **Step 2 — Linear velocity at the drum surface:** Multiply the per-revolution distance by the revolutions per second. $$v = d_{rev} \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$ $$\boxed{v = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{[m/s]}}$$ **Step 3 — Convert m/s to km/h:** Multiply by 3.6. $$V = v \cdot 3.6 = \frac{3.6 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{16.67}$$ $$\boxed{V = \frac{60 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{[km/h]}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wheel Torque from Force Arm Reading The dynamometer measures force $F$ on a horizontal lever arm of length $L$ attached to the drum's stationary reaction plate. Torque is the moment of this force. **Step 1 — Definition of torque:** $$T = F \cdot L \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$ For a horizontal arm loaded vertically, $\alpha = 90°$, so $\sin(\alpha) = 1$. $$\boxed{T = F \cdot L \quad \text{[N·m]}}$$ **Step 2 — Practical units check:** If $F$ is in kgf (kilogram-force), convert: $F_N = F_{kgf} \times 9.81$. This is a common trap in KTU labs. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Wheel Power Output Power is the rate of doing work. In a rotating system: $$P = \tau \cdot \omega$$ where $\omega$ is the angular velocity in rad/s. **Step 1 — Express angular velocity from drum RPM:** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $$P = T \cdot \frac{2\pi \cdot N}{60}$$ $$\boxed{P = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}}$$ **Step 3 — Convenient form for dyno displays:** $$P_{kW} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60000}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Specific Energy Consumption This is the single most important metric for an EV customer. **Step 1 — DC energy delivered by the battery pack:** The pack delivers average voltage $V_{bat}$ and average current $I$ over a time interval $t$. $$E_{dc} = V_{bat} \cdot I \cdot t \quad \text{[Watt-hours]}$$ **Step 2 — Distance covered on the drum:** $$d = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot t}{1000} \quad \text{[km]}$$ **Step 3 — Specific energy consumption:** $$\boxed{E_{sp} = \frac{V_{bat} \cdot I \cdot t}{d} \quad \text{[Wh/km]}}$$ **Step 4 — All-Electric Range:** Given a battery of capacity $C$ Ah at nominal voltage $V_{nom}$, total stored energy is $E_{tot} = C \cdot V_{nom}$ Wh. $$\boxed{R = \frac{C \cdot V_{nom}}{E_{sp}} \quad \text{[km]}}$$ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) > **Problem:** A 110 kg electric scooter is tested on a chassis dynamometer. The drum diameter is **0.4 m**, the torque arm length is **0.5 m**, and the load cell reads **120 N** at a steady drum speed of **480 RPM**. Compute: (a) the vehicle speed in km/h, (b) the wheel torque in N·m, and (c) the wheel power in kW. **Solution — Part (a): Vehicle Speed** $$V = \frac{60 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$$ $$V = \frac{60 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.4 \cdot 480}{1000}$$ Let's expand this step by step. $$ \begin{aligned} V &= \frac{60 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.4 \cdot 480}{1000} \\ &= \frac{60 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 480 \cdot \pi}{1000} \\ &= \frac{11520 \cdot \pi}{1000} \\ &= \frac{36191.15}{1000} \\ &= 36.19 \text{ km/h} \end{aligned} $$ **Solution — Part (b): Wheel Torque** $$T = F \cdot L = 120 \cdot 0.5 = 60 \text{ N·m}$$ **Solution — Part (c): Wheel Power** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60000} \quad \text{[kW]} \\ &= \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot 480 \cdot 60}{60000} \\ &= \frac{180955.74}{60000} \\ &= 3.016 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Summary of Results:** | Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Vehicle speed | $V$ | 36.19 | km/h | | Wheel torque | $T$ | 60 | N·m | | Wheel power | $P$ | 3.02 | kW | ## 3.6 Derivation 5: Equivalent Inertia Correction A two-wheeler on a single roller does not have the same "felt inertia" as on the road. The dynamometer must add an equivalent inertia $I_{eq}$ to faithfully replicate road behavior. $$ \begin{aligned} I_{eq} &= m_{eq} \cdot (r_{dynamic})^2 \\ \text{where } m_{eq} &= m_{vehicle} + m_{rider} + m_{rotational-equivalent} \end{aligned} $$ The total effective mass includes the vehicle, the test rider (typically a 75 kg dummy on a KTU rig), and an "equivalent rotational mass factor" (usually 3–5% of the total) added to account for spinning wheels and motor rotors. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Tip:** KTU numerical problems frequently use a combined **kerb mass + 75 kg rider**. If unstated, always assume the rider mass is **75 kg**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Chassis Dynamometer Test Setup for Electric Two-Wheeler ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Battery Pack
V and I Sensors] --> B[Motor Controller
Throttle Input] B --> C[BLDC / PMSM
Motor] C --> D[Transmission
and Chain Drive] D --> E[Rear Wheel
of Two-Wheeler] E --> F[Dynamometer
Drum Roller] F --> G[Torque Arm
Load Cell] F --> H[Rotary Encoder
Speed Sensor] G --> I[DAQ System
Data Acquisition] H --> I A --> I I --> J[PC Display
Torque Power Curves] I --> K[Report Generator
Energy and Range] L[Cooling Fan
for Drum] --> F ``` ## 4.2 Flowchart: Standard Test Sequence as per AIS-040 (Rev. 1) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Preconditioning
Battery SoC to 100 percent] --> S2[Step 2: Vehicle Strapping
on Chassis Dyno] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Sensor Calibration
Torque Speed Current] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Road Load
Coefficient Setting] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Idle Test
No Throttle for 60s] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Acceleration Test
Wide Open Throttle] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Constant Speed Cruise
at 45 km per hour] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Gradeability Test
Simulated Slope] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Range Test
ECE R40 or IDC Cycle] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Energy Consumption
and SoC Logging] S10 --> S11[Step 11: Report Generation
and Validation] ``` ## 4.3 Block Architecture: Measurement and Control Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Sensors SE1[Load Cell
Force in N] SE2[Encoder
Speed in RPM] SE3[Voltage Divider
Pack Voltage] SE4[Hall Effect Sensor
Pack Current] SE5[Thermocouple
Motor Temperature] end subgraph Signal_Conditioning SC1[Amplifier] SC2[Filter and Isolator] SC3[ADC Converter] end subgraph Controller CT1[Microcontroller
or Industrial PC] CT2[Dyno Controller
ECU] end subgraph Output OP1[Torque vs Speed Curve] OP2[Power vs Speed Curve] OP3[Energy and Range Report] OP4[Real Time Dashboard] end SE1 --> SC1 --> SC3 SE2 --> SC1 SE3 --> SC2 --> SC3 SE4 --> SC2 --> SC3 SE5 --> SC2 --> SC3 SC3 --> CT1 CT1 --> CT2 CT1 --> OP4 CT2 --> OP1 CT2 --> OP2 CT2 --> OP3 ``` ## 4.4 Component Layout of a Single-Roller Dyno Rig ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Two-Wheeler
Front Tie-Down] --> B[Center of
Drum Roller] C[Rear Wheel
Drive Wheel] --> B B --> D[Dynamometer
Eddy Current Absorber] D --> E[Torque Arm
with Load Cell] B --> F[Encoder
on Drum Shaft] D --> G[Cooling Fan
and Heat Sink] H[Bench Control
Station] --> D H --> I[DAQ and
Display Unit] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **Q1. Define chassis dynamometer. Why is it preferred over on-road testing for electric two-wheelers?** **Model Answer:** A chassis dynamometer is a stationary test apparatus in which a vehicle's drive wheel runs on a calibrated rotating roller, while a controllable absorber (eddy current, regenerative, or hydraulic) applies a known load to simulate road resistance. *Preferred over on-road testing because:* 1. **Repeatability** — climatic, traffic, and gradient variations are eliminated. 2. **Safety** — high-speed, full-throttle, and failure-mode tests are conducted in a controlled environment. 3. **Precision** — torque, speed, current, and voltage are measured directly with high-accuracy transducers. 4. **Regulatory compliance** — AIS-040 (Rev. 1) and ISO 13064 procedures require laboratory-grade repeatability for certification. **(3 Marks: Definition 1, three reasons 2)** --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q2. List the three resistive forces that a chassis dynamometer must simulate for an electric two-wheeler and write the expression for each.** **Model Answer:** The three resistive forces are: 1. **Rolling resistance:** $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ 2. **Aerodynamic drag:** $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ 3. **Grade resistance:** $F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ Total road load: $F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{grade}$ **(3 Marks: 1 mark per correct expression with definition)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Choice-Based Long Answer (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a single-roller chassis dynamometer used for testing electric two-wheelers. List the major components and their functions. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer — Part (a):** A single-roller chassis dynamometer consists of a heavy rotating steel drum of known diameter on which the rear drive wheel of the two-wheeler is placed. The front wheel is tied down to a fixture to prevent longitudinal motion. The drum is coupled to an **eddy current absorber** that applies a controlled braking torque. *Major components and functions:* - **Roller drum** — provides a moving surface of known diameter for speed measurement. - **Eddy current absorber (ECA)** — applies programmable load to simulate road resistance. - **Torque arm with load cell** — measures reaction force, which is multiplied by arm length to give torque. - **Rotary encoder** — measures drum RPM, which is converted to vehicle speed. - **Tie-down fixture** — restrains the front of the vehicle. - **Cooling fan** — removes heat from the eddy current absorber. - **DAQ and PC** — logs all parameters and generates curves. The dynamometer controller is programmed with the **road-load coefficients** ($F_0$, $F_1$, $F_2$) corresponding to the target vehicle mass, frontal area, and drag coefficient. During a test, the controller adjusts the ECA excitation current in real time so that the drum's load curve matches the calculated road-load curve. **(7 Marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for components, 2 for working principle)** --- **b)** An electric scooter of kerb mass 105 kg is tested on a chassis dynamometer with a drum diameter of 0.35 m. At a steady speed corresponding to a drum RPM of 510, the load cell reads 95 N on a torque arm of length 0.5 m. Calculate (i) the vehicle speed in km/h, (ii) the wheel torque, and (iii) the wheel power in kW. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer — Part (b):** **(i) Vehicle speed:** $$V = \frac{60 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{60 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.35 \cdot 510}{1000} = \frac{33.64}{1} \approx 33.64 \text{ km/h}$$ Wait, recalculating accurately: $$ \begin{aligned} V &= \frac{60 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.35 \cdot 510}{1000} \\ &= \frac{60 \cdot 0.35 \cdot 510 \cdot \pi}{1000} \\ &= \frac{10710 \cdot \pi}{1000} \\ &= \frac{33645.0}{1000} \\ &\approx 33.65 \text{ km/h} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Wheel torque:** $$T = F \cdot L = 95 \cdot 0.5 = 47.5 \text{ N·m}$$ **(iii) Wheel power:** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60000} \\ &= \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot 510 \cdot 47.5}{60000} \\ &= \frac{152213.7}{60000} \\ &\approx 2.54 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Summary Table:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Vehicle speed | 33.65 km/h | | Wheel torque | 47.5 N·m | | Wheel power | 2.54 kW | **(7 Marks: 2 for speed, 2 for torque, 3 for power with full working)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **a)** Explain with diagrams the difference between an **eddy current dynamometer** and a **regenerative dynamometer**. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each in the context of EV two-wheeler testing. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer — Part (a):** | Feature | Eddy Current Dynamometer | Regenerative Dynamometer | |---|---|---| | Principle | A rotating steel disc passes through a magnetic field; eddy currents induced in the disc produce an opposing torque. | A motor-generator acts as a load; the absorbed energy is converted back to electricity and returned to the grid. | | Energy dissipation | All absorbed energy is lost as **heat** in the disc. | Absorbed energy is **recovered as electrical energy**. | | Control | Smooth, continuous, stepless control via field current. | Control via torque-mode inverter reference. | | Accuracy | Moderate (±1–2%). | Very high (±0.1–0.5%). | | Cost | Low to moderate. | High. | *Eddy current — advantage:* Simple, rugged, low cost, ideal for routine KTU lab use. *Disadvantage:* Energy wasted as heat; long-duration tests need large cooling fans. *Regenerative — advantage:* Energy is fed back to the grid, ideal for long endurance and range tests. *Disadvantage:* High capital cost and complex inverter control. **(7 Marks: 3 for principles with diagrams, 2 for ECA pros/cons, 2 for regen pros/cons)** --- **b)** During a 60-minute constant-speed test at 40 km/h on a chassis dynamometer, the battery pack of an electric two-wheeler delivered an average current of 18 A at an average voltage of 57.6 V. The drum diameter was 0.40 m and the test speed was 40 km/h. Determine (i) the total DC energy consumed in Wh, (ii) the distance simulated in km, and (iii) the specific energy consumption in Wh/km. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer — Part (b):** **(i) Total DC energy consumed:** $$E_{dc} = V_{bat} \cdot I \cdot t = 57.6 \cdot 18 \cdot 1 = 1036.8 \text{ Wh}$$ **(ii) Distance simulated:** At a constant 40 km/h for 1 hour: $$d = V \cdot t = 40 \cdot 1 = 40 \text{ km}$$ **(iii) Specific energy consumption:** $$E_{sp} = \frac{E_{dc}}{d} = \frac{1036.8}{40} = 25.92 \text{ Wh/km}$$ **Summary:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Total DC energy | 1036.8 Wh | | Distance | 40 km | | Specific energy consumption | 25.92 Wh/km | *Interpretation:* A typical 2.5 kWh battery pack would give a range of about: $$R = \frac{2500}{25.92} \approx 96.5 \text{ km}$$ **(7 Marks: 2 for energy, 2 for distance, 3 for specific energy with all steps shown)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Marks Are Lost:** > 1. **Unit conversions** — Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s, or m/s to km/h. Always show the conversion step explicitly. > 2. **Confusing wheel power and motor power** — Dyno measures *wheel* power, which is *less* than motor power due to drivetrain losses (typically 8–15%). > 3. **Using wrong formula for vehicle speed** — Many students forget the factor of 60/1000 (or 16.67) when converting drum RPM to km/h. > 4. **Neglecting the rider mass** — KTU convention is to add **75 kg** for the test rider unless stated otherwise. > 5. **Force vs torque** — The load cell reads *force* in N or kgf; multiplying by arm length gives *torque* in N·m. Skipping the multiplication costs 2 marks. > 6. **Wrong drum radius** — Use the **drum diameter** $D$, not the radius, in the $V = \pi D N / 60$ formula. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### **High-Density Revision Checklist** - **Chassis dynamometer** = stationary roller + controllable absorber + sensors. - **Primary output of a dyno test:** torque–speed curve, power–speed curve, energy consumption, range. - **Three simulated road-load forces:** rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, grade resistance. - **Master formulas (must be memorized):** - $V = \dfrac{60 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ km/h - $T = F \cdot L$ N·m - $P = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60}$ W - $E_{sp} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{d}$ Wh/km - $R = \dfrac{C \cdot V_{nom}}{E_{sp}}$ km - **Standards to cite in answers:** AIS-040 (Rev. 1), ISO 13064, ECE R40. - **Key constants:** $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², default rider mass = 75 kg. - **Eddy current dyno** = dissipative, common, low cost. - **Regenerative dyno** = energy recovering, high accuracy, high cost. - **Drum diameter is the master geometric parameter** — every velocity calculation begins with it. - **For acceleration tests**, the dynamometer must replicate the inertial force $F = m \cdot a$; this requires flywheel or active inertia simulation. - **Always state assumptions** — ambient temperature, SoC level, road-load coefficients. - **Plot the curves** — KTU answer scripts that include a sketch of the torque–power–speed curve consistently score higher. - **Efficiency check** — Wheel power is always less than electrical input power; the ratio defines the **drivetrain efficiency** $\eta = P_{wheel} / P_{electrical}$. - **Range test discipline** — Run the test until the battery management system (BMS) cuts off; do *not* extrapolate or interpolate without justification. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "C-R-A-G-P":** **C**hassis, **R**olling resistance, **A**ero drag, **G**rade force, **P**ower. Remember the order of simulated loads and the formula sequence: $V \rightarrow F \rightarrow T \rightarrow P \rightarrow E_{sp} \rightarrow R$.

topics.title: Dynamometer testing for IC engine two-wheelers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL507 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamometer testing for IC engine two-wheelers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **dynamometer** (often abbreviated as "dyno") is a precision instrument used to measure the **rotational force (torque)**, **rotational speed (RPM)**, and consequently the **power output** of an internal combustion (IC) engine or a complete vehicle. In the context of two-wheelers, dynamometer testing is the standardised laboratory procedure in which a motorcycle or scooter engine is subjected to a controlled, quantifiable load at progressively varied speeds so that its performance map (torque curve, power curve, fuel consumption map, and emission profile) can be plotted.

For KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory record purposes, the experiment is officially titled **"Dynamometer testing for IC engine two-wheelers"** and falls under the broader module of **Performance Evaluation of Automotive Powertrains**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Dynamometer testing is the controlled loading of a two-wheeler engine (or its rear wheel) on a calibrated absorbing/measuring device to determine brake power, brake specific fuel consumption, air-fuel ratio, volumetric efficiency, and exhaust emissions at different throttle positions and engine speeds.

### 1.2 Classification of Dynamometers Used for Two-Wheelers

Two-wheeler dynamometers are broadly classified based on **where** the load is applied:

1. **Engine Dynamometer (Motor Stand Dyno)**
   - The engine is removed from the chassis and bolted to a test bed.
   - The engine's output shaft is coupled directly to the dynamometer rotor.
   - Provides the most accurate measurement because parasitic losses of the drivetrain (chain, sprocket, gearbox) are excluded.
   - Used in OEM R&D and ARAI-certified test cells.

2. **Chassis Dynamometer (Rolling Road / Roller Dyno)**
   - The complete two-wheeler is placed with its **rear (drive) wheel** resting on a rotating steel drum.
   - The front wheel is strapped to a fixture to keep the vehicle vertical.
   - The drum is coupled to an absorbing unit (eddy current, hydraulic, or AC electrical).
   - Simulates road-load conditions including wind and rolling resistance.
   - Most common in service workshops and college laboratories.

3. **Inertia Dynamometer**
   - Uses a known flywheel mass to absorb the kinetic energy of the engine as it accelerates.
   - Power is calculated from the rate of change of angular velocity.

### 1.3 Classification Based on Loading Principle

- **Absorption (Passive) Dynamometer:** Absorbs and dissipates the mechanical energy as heat. Examples — **Eddy current dynamometer**, **Hydraulic dynamometer (water brake)**.
- **Power Absorption (Active) Dynamometer:** Absorbs and converts the mechanical energy to electrical energy. Examples — **DC dynamometer**, **AC dynamometer (regenerative)**.

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU 2024 Scheme PCAUL507 laboratory, the **Eddy Current Dynamometer** mounted on a **Chassis Test Rig** is the most commonly instrumented setup because it offers a stepless, smooth, electrically controllable load, and is safe for student operation.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Treadmill for a Motorcycle"

Imagine strapping a stationary motorcycle to a giant treadmill roller. The rider twists the throttle, and instead of moving forward, the engine's effort is met with a controlled, measurable resistance. The harder the engine pushes, the more the roller resists — exactly like pedalling a bicycle on a stationary trainer, except the trainer dial can be turned up or down electronically to simulate going uphill, cruising on a flat road, or accelerating from rest.

In this analogy:
- The **rider's legs** = Engine pistons delivering torque.
- The **treadmill belt resistance** = Eddy current or hydraulic brake.
- The **bicycle speedometer** = RPM sensor / optical pickup.
