- The **power meter on the trainer** = Strain-gauge load cell measuring torque.

The dynamometer is, therefore, a **mechanical accountant** that records exactly how much twisting force and how fast the engine can turn under any load we choose to apply.

### 1.5 Standard Engineering Metrics Recorded

- **Brake Torque (T_b)** in N·m
- **Engine Speed (N)** in RPM
- **Brake Power (BP)** in kW
- **Fuel Consumption (m_f)** in **kg/h** or **L/h**
- **Air Mass Flow (m_a)** in **kg/h**
- **Exhaust Gas Temperature (EGT)** in °C
- **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)**
- **Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)** in %
- **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_th)** in %
- **Mechanical Efficiency (η_m)** in %

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Conditions (as per BIS / SAE J1349):** Ambient temperature = **25 °C**, Barometric pressure = **99 kPa**, Relative humidity = **30 %**, and the fuel lower heating value is taken as **42,500 kJ/kg** for gasoline and **43,100 kJ/kg** for diesel.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of an Eddy Current Dynamometer

A chassis dynamometer used for two-wheelers is essentially a **steel drum coupled to a rotating disc (rotor)**. The disc is placed in the air gap of a stationary **electromagnetic field coil (stator)**. When the drive wheel rotates the drum and the rotor, the relative motion between the rotor and the stator's magnetic field induces **eddy currents** in the rotor disc (Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction).

These eddy currents, in turn, produce their own magnetic field that **opposes the rotation** (Lenz's Law). The opposing force is transmitted back to the drum and hence to the two-wheeler's drive wheel. The strength of the magnetic field — and therefore the braking torque — is varied by adjusting the **excitation current** sent through the stator coil.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Eddy Current Dyno is preferred in college labs:**
> 1. Stepless, smooth, and instant load control via a knob (DC excitation current).
> 2. No high-pressure water lines (unlike hydraulic dynamometers).
> 3. Safe — the absorbed energy is dissipated as heat in the rotor fins via a built-in cooling fan.
> 4. Direct electrical readout of torque and speed.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Test Procedure

1. **Pre-test inspection:** Check engine oil level, coolant (if liquid-cooled), fuel level, tyre pressure (for chassis dyno), and chain tension.
2. **Warm-up run:** Idle the engine for **3–5 minutes** to reach steady operating temperature (typically 80–90 °C coolant).
3. **Sensor calibration:** Zero the load cell, set the digital RPM pickup reference.
4. **Throttle sweep:** With the throttle fully open (WOT), progressively increase the eddy current excitation from 0 A to the rated maximum in 5–10 equal steps.
5. **Data logging:** At each loading step, record RPM, torque, fuel flow rate, air flow rate, and exhaust temperature after the readings stabilise (usually 30–60 seconds per step).
6. **Repeat for part-throttle positions:** Typically 75 %, 50 %, and 25 % of WOT for emission and fuel-map studies.
7. **Cool-down:** Reduce load gradually; do not shut down a hot engine abruptly.

### 2.3 Why We Test Two-Wheelers on a Dyno

- **Manufacturer validation** of rated power and torque claims.
- **Emission certification** as per Bharat Stage (BS-VI) norms.
- **Tuning workshops** — remapping of ECU, jetting of carburettors, selection of aftermarket exhausts.
- **Comparative benchmarking** between OEM models in the press.
- **Academic record** — KTU lab viva and end-semester evaluation of performance curves.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brake Power | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_b}{60\,000}$ | kW | $N$ in RPM, $T_b$ in N·m |
| 2 | Indicated Power | $IP = BP + F_P$ | kW | $F_P$ = Frictional Power (from motoring test) |
| 3 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{BP}{IP} \times 100$ | % | Typical two-wheeler: 75–88 % |
| 4 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | Gasoline engines: 0.30–0.45 |
| 5 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} \times 100$ | % | $CV$ = calorific value of fuel (kJ/kg) |
| 6 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a \times V_d \times \dfrac{N}{2 \times 60}}$ | % | 4-stroke: N/2 strokes per minute |
| 7 | Air-Fuel Ratio | $AFR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | – | Stoichiometric gasoline ≈ 14.7 |
| 8 | Equivalent Inertia Power | $BP_{acc} = I \cdot \omega \cdot \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | W | Inertia dynamometer only |
| 9 | Mean Effective Pressure | $P_m = \dfrac{2 \times BP \times 60}{V_d \times N}$ | kPa | $V_d$ in m³, $N$ in RPM |
| 10 | Specific Power Output | $SP = \dfrac{BP}{V_d}$ | kW/L | Used for engine rating |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU exam derivations, remember to substitute $N$ in **rev/min** and convert to **rev/sec** by dividing by 60 before multiplying with $2\pi$ to get power in **W** (then divide by 1000 for kW). The factor $2\pi/60\,000$ already embeds this conversion.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ARAI (Automotive Research Association of India), Pune** certifies every two-wheeler's power and emission claims using chassis and engine dynamometers.
- **Kingdrag, Dynojet, and Maha** are the most popular commercial chassis dyno brands used in Indian tuning shops.
- **F1 teams** use **inertia dynamometers** coupled with **water-brake** units for transient engine mapping.
- **Electric vehicle two-wheelers** (e.g., Ola, Ather) use **regenerative AC dynamometers** that pump the absorbed energy back into the grid, achieving > 90 % test-cell efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Brake Power from Torque and Speed

**Statement:** The brake power developed by an engine at the output shaft is the product of the angular velocity and the net braking torque.

**Starting Relation:** Power is defined as the rate of doing work. For a rotating shaft,

$$P = T \times \omega$$

where $T$ is the torque in N·m and $\omega$ is the angular velocity in rad/s.

**Step 1 — Express angular velocity in terms of RPM.**
The angular velocity $\omega$ (in rad/s) corresponding to $N$ revolutions per minute is

$$\omega = 2 \pi \times \frac{N}{60}$$

The factor $2\pi$ converts revolutions to radians; the factor $60$ converts minutes to seconds.

**Step 2 — Substitute $\omega$ into the power equation.**

$$P = T \times 2 \pi \times \frac{N}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Combine constants.**

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$$

The result is in **watts (W)** when $N$ is in RPM and $T$ is in N·m, because 1 W = 1 N·m/s.

**Step 4 — Convert to kilowatts (kW) as required for KTU lab records.**

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T_b}{60\,000} \text{ kW}$$

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style):**
A 150 cc motorcycle engine is tested on an eddy current dynamometer. At a throttle position of 75 % WOT, the engine speed stabilises at **5000 RPM** and the load cell reads a torque of **12.5 N·m**. Calculate the brake power.

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 5000 \times 12.5}{60\,000} \text{ kW}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 5000 \times 12.5}{60\,000} \text{ kW}$$

$$BP = \frac{392\,699.1}{60\,000} \text{ kW}$$

$$BP = 6.545 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The torque read by the load cell is the **net (brake) torque** after subtracting bearing friction and windage losses of the dynamometer itself. The dynamometer's certificate of calibration provides a "zero-load" offset that is subtracted from the gross reading to obtain the true $T_b$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)

**Statement:** BSFC is the mass flow rate of fuel supplied per unit of brake power produced per hour.

**Starting Relation:**

$$BSFC = \frac{\text{Fuel consumed per hour}}{\text{Brake Power produced}}$$

**Step 1 — Express fuel flow per hour.**

If $\dot{V}_f$ is the volumetric flow rate of fuel in L/h and $\rho_f$ is the density of fuel in kg/L, then

$$\dot{m}_f = \dot{V}_f \times \rho_f \quad \text{(kg/h)}$$

**Step 2 — Express brake power in kW.**

$$BP \text{ in kW} = \frac{2 \pi N T_b}{60\,000}$$

**Step 3 — Combine.**

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{\dot{V}_f \times \rho_f \times 60\,000}{2 \pi N T_b} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}}\right]$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**
The same engine consumes fuel at **1.85 L/h** at the test point. Petrol density = **0.745 kg/L**. Find BSFC.

$$\dot{m}_f = 1.85 \times 0.745 = 1.378 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$BSFC = \frac{1.378}{6.545} = 0.2106 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

$$BSFC = 210.6 \text{ g/kWh}$$

This is a typical value for a small four-stroke two-wheeler engine under part-throttle cruise.

### 3.3 Derivation of Volumetric Efficiency

**Statement:** Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of the actual mass of air inducted per cycle to the theoretical mass that would fill the swept volume at ambient density.

**Step 1 — Theoretical mass flow of air.**
For a four-stroke engine, the number of induction cycles per second is $N/(2 \times 60)$. Hence the theoretical volume flow of air at ambient conditions is

$$\dot{V}_{th} = V_d \times \frac{N}{2 \times 60} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}$$

The corresponding theoretical mass flow is

$$\dot{m}_{th} = \rho_a \times V_d \times \frac{N}{2 \times 60} \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$

**Step 2 — Actual mass flow.**
The actual mass flow $\dot{m}_a$ is measured by an air-flow meter (orifice, hot-wire, or vortex-shedding type) installed on the intake snorkel.

**Step 3 — Define volumetric efficiency.**

$$\eta_v = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_{th}} = \frac{\dot{m}_a \times 2 \times 60}{\rho_a \times V_d \times N} \times 100 \, \%$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**
A 150 cc engine (swept volume = **149.5 cm³ = 149.5 × 10⁻⁶ m³**) is tested at **5000 RPM**. Ambient air density $\rho_a$ = **1.15 kg/m³**. Air-mass flow meter reads **0.0048 kg/s**. Find $\eta_v$.

$$\dot{m}_{th} = 1.15 \times 149.5 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{5000}{120}$$

$$\dot{m}_{th} = 1.15 \times 149.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 41.667$$

$$\dot{m}_{th} = 7.165 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\eta_v = \frac{0.0048}{0.007165} \times 100 = 67.0 \, \%$$

This is realistic for a naturally aspirated commuter motorcycle at mid-range RPM.

### 3.4 Derivation of Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)

**Step 1 — Measure both mass flows.**

$$\dot{m}_a \text{ from air-flow meter (kg/h)}$$

$$\dot{m}_f \text{ from fuel-flow meter (kg/h)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute ratio.**

$$AFR = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalence Ratio (often asked in KTU viva).**

$$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{A/F_{actual}} = \frac{14.7}{AFR_{actual}}$$

- $\phi = 1$ → stoichiometric
- $\phi < 1$ → lean burn
- $\phi > 1$ → rich mixture

### 3.5 Derivation of Mechanical Efficiency

**Step 1 — Run the engine as a motor (motoring test).**
Disconnect fuel and ignition, then drive the engine with the dynamometer at the same speed. The power required to motor the engine is the **Frictional Power (F_P)**.

**Step 2 — Measure indicated power indirectly.**
Indicated power is the power developed inside the combustion chamber, which equals the brake power plus all mechanical losses.

$$IP = BP + F_P$$

**Step 3 — Compute efficiency.**

$$\eta_m = \frac{BP}{IP} \times 100 = \frac{BP}{BP + F_P} \times 100 \, \%$$

### 3.6 Analytical Performance Matrix (Comparative Snapshot)

| Throttle Opening | Speed (RPM) | Torque (N·m) | BP (kW) | BSFC (g/kWh) | $\eta_v$ (%) | AFR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100 % (WOT) | 8500 | 14.2 | 12.64 | 320 | 92 | 12.8 (rich) |
| 75 % | 6500 | 13.1 | 8.92 | 285 | 85 | 13.6 |
| 50 % | 5000 | 11.0 | 5.76 | 255 | 76 | 14.7 (stoich) |
| 25 % | 3500 | 8.4 | 3.08 | 360 | 62 | 16.4 (lean) |
| Idle | 1400 | 4.0 | 0.59 | 980 | 28 | 11.2 (very rich) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above trends are **canonical for KTU numericals**: BSFC is minimum near 75 % WOT at mid-RPM (the engine's "sweet spot"), $\eta_v$ peaks near WOT at high RPM, and the mixture is richest at idle to ensure stable combustion.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Two-Wheeler Dynamometer Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TW["TWO WHEELER UNDER TEST"]
        ENG[IC Engine]
        CLU[Clutch and Gearbox]
        WH[Drive Wheel]
        ENG --> CLU --> WH
    end

    subgraph DYN["DYNAMOMETER ASSEMBLY"]
        ROL[Rotating Drum Roller]
        ROT[Rotor Disc]
        STA[Stator Electromagnetic Coil]
        FAN[Cooling Fan on Rotor Shaft]
        ROL --- ROT
        STA -.->|magnetic field| ROT
        ROT --- FAN
    end

    subgraph INS["INSTRUMENTATION and DATA ACQUISITION"]
        TQS[Torque Sensor Load Cell]
        RPS[RPM Pickup Optical or Magnetic]
        FFM[Fuel Flow Meter]
        AFM[Air Flow Meter]
        EGP[Exhaust Gas Probe]
        DAQ[DAQ and PC with DAQ Software]
        TQS --> DAQ
        RPS --> DAQ
        FFM --> DAQ
        AFM --> DAQ
        EGP --> DAQ
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL CONSOLE"]
        EXC[DC Excitation Current Source]
        THR[Throttle Position Actuator]
        EXC --> STA
        THR --> ENG
    end

    WH -->|contact friction| ROL
    ROT -->|reaction torque| TQS
    ROL -->|speed pulses| RPS
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Data Flow and Computation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Started and Warmed Up] --> B[Set Throttle to Test Position]
    B --> C[Apply Stepwise Eddy Current Load]
    C --> D[Read RPM N from Pickup]
    C --> E[Read Torque Tb from Load Cell]
    C --> F[Read Fuel Flow Vf]
    C --> G[Read Air Flow ma]
    C --> H[Read EGT and Lambda]
    D --> I[Compute BP equals 2piNTb divided by 60000]
    E --> I
    F --> J[Compute BSFC]
    I --> J
    G --> K[Compute Volumetric Efficiency]
    D --> K
    H --> L[Record Emission Snapshot]
    I --> M[Plot Performance Curves]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{More Throttle Positions?}
    N -->|Yes| B
    N -->|No| O[Save Data and Cool Down]
```

### 4.3 Typical Performance Curves (Schematic Trend)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CURVES["CHARACTERISTIC PERFORMANCE CURVES"]
        C1[Torque Curve Tb vs N: Rises then falls, peak near 70 to 80 percent of max RPM]
        C2[Power Curve BP vs N: Rises monotonically then plateaus, peak near WOT max RPM]
        C3[BSFC Curve vs N: U shaped, minimum at mid RPM and 75 percent WOT]
        C4[Volumetric Efficiency vs N: Rises with N due to tuning, peaks before max RPM]
    end
```

### 4.4 Component Legend (For Lab Record Sketches)

| Symbol | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **TQS** | Torque Sensor / Load Cell | Measures reaction torque via strain gauges |
| **RPS** | RPM Pickup | Optical or magnetic pulse sensor on drum |
| **FFM** | Fuel Flow Meter | Positive-displacement glass burette or electronic flow transducer |
| **AFM** | Air Flow Meter | Orifice plate with differential manometer or hot-wire probe |
| **EGP** | Exhaust Gas Probe | Lambda / O₂ sensor for AFR measurement |
| **STA** | Stator Coil | Generates controlled magnetic field (excitation current) |
| **ROL** | Drum Roller | Friction-coupled to drive wheel |
| **DAQ** | Data Acquisition System | Analog-to-digital conversion and PC logging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When drawing the block diagram in your lab record, label every sensor with its **measurement range and accuracy** (e.g., Load Cell: 0–50 N·m, ±0.1 % FS). This is a frequently-missed KTU requirement.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**  
Define a dynamometer. List any **two** types of dynamometers used for testing IC engine two-wheelers and state the principle of operation of an **eddy current dynamometer**.

**Model Answer:**

A dynamometer is a device used to measure the **torque**, **speed**, and **power output** of an engine or a vehicle by applying a controlled, measurable load.

Two types commonly used for two-wheelers:
1. **Engine dynamometer** — engine removed and coupled to the dyno.
2. **Chassis (roller) dynamometer** — drive wheel rests on a rotating drum.

**Principle of eddy current dynamometer:**  
When a conductive metal disc (rotor) is rotated in a magnetic field produced by a stationary electromagnetic coil (stator), **eddy currents** are induced in the disc (Faraday's Law). These currents generate a magnetic field that **opposes** the motion (Lenz's Law), producing a braking torque proportional to the **excitation current** of the coil. The torque is absorbed as heat in the rotor and dissipated by a built-in cooling fan.

> [!WARNING]
> Students often confuse the **stator** and **rotor** roles. Remember: the **rotor is the rotating conductive disc**, and the **stator is the stationary electromagnetic coil**. Drawing them swapped will cost a mark in the diagram section.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**  
What is **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)**? Write its SI unit and state the typical BSFC range for a modern four-stroke two-wheeler petrol engine.

**Model Answer:**

BSFC is defined as the **mass of fuel consumed per unit of brake power produced per hour**. It is a measure of how efficiently the engine converts the chemical energy of fuel into useful mechanical work at the output shaft.

**SI Unit:** **kg/kWh** (commonly expressed in **g/kWh**).

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \text{ (kg/h)}}{BP \text{ (kW)}}$$

**Typical Range:** For a modern four-stroke, spark-ignition, two-wheeler engine under normal operating conditions, BSFC lies between **300 and 450 g/kWh** (i.e., 0.30–0.45 kg/kWh). Best (minimum) BSFC is achieved near **75 % WOT at mid-RPM**.

> [!NOTE]
> Lower BSFC = better fuel economy per unit of work. This is the key figure of merit used by ARAI for fuel-efficiency certification of two-wheelers in India.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the procedure for conducting a **chassis dynamometer test** on a 150 cc four-stroke petrol motorcycle. List the instruments used and explain how **brake power** and **brake specific fuel consumption** are calculated from the test data.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During a chassis dynamometer test on a 150 cc motorcycle, the following readings were recorded at 75 % WOT:

| Engine Speed (RPM) | Brake Torque (N·m) | Fuel Flow (L/h) |
|---|---|---|
| 3000 | 11.5 | 1.20 |
| 4500 | 12.8 | 1.55 |
| 6000 | 12.4 | 1.80 |
| 7500 | 10.9 | 1.95 |

Density of petrol = **0.745 kg/L**, Calorific Value = **42,500 kJ/kg**. Calculate:
1. Brake power at each speed.
2. BSFC at each speed.
3. Brake thermal efficiency at the speed of maximum brake power.
4. Plot (on a graph paper) the variation of BP and BSFC with engine speed.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

*Block Diagram:*

```
[Motorcycle on chassis dyno with rear wheel on drum]
        → [Drum coupled to rotor of eddy current dyno]
              → [Stator coil with adjustable DC excitation]
                    → [Reaction torque → Load Cell (T_b)]
                          → [Optical pickup → RPM counter (N)]
                                → [Fuel burette → V̇_f]
                                      → [Air box with orifice → ṁ_a]
                                            → [DAQ & PC]
```

*Procedure:*  
1. Inspect motorcycle — tyre pressure (rear: 2.2–2.5 bar), chain slack, engine oil, coolant.  
2. Strap front wheel to the fixture; align the rear wheel on the drum.  
3. Start engine, idle for 3–5 min, zero the load cell, calibrate the RPM pickup.  
4. Open throttle to the desired position (here 75 % WOT).  
5. Increase eddy current excitation in 4–5 steps from zero to maximum, recording $N$, $T_b$, $\dot{V}_f$ at each step after steady state.  
6. Repeat for other throttle positions if required.  
7. Cool down the dyno by reducing load gradually, then shut off engine.

*Instruments used:*
- Eddy current dynamometer (load absorber)
- Strain-gauge load cell (torque measurement)
- Optical/magnetic RPM pickup
- Glass burette or electronic fuel flow meter
- Orifice-type air flow meter
- Digital data acquisition system (DAQ)
- Exhaust gas analyser (optional)

*Calculation of BP:*

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T_b}{60\,000} \text{ kW}$$

*Calculation of BSFC:*

$$\dot{m}_f = \dot{V}_f \times \rho_f \text{ kg/h} \quad ; \quad BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Tabular Computation:**

**Step 1: Compute $BP$ at each speed** using $BP = 2\pi N T_b / 60\,000$:

| N (RPM) | T_b (N·m) | BP (kW) |
|---|---|---|
| 3000 | 11.5 | $2\pi(3000)(11.5)/60\,000$ = **3.614** |
| 4500 | 12.8 | **6.034** |
| 6000 | 12.4 | **7.791** |
| 7500 | 10.9 | **8.562** |

**Step 2: Compute $\dot{m}_f$ and BSFC:**

| N | $\dot{V}_f$ (L/h) | $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/h) | BSFC (kg/kWh) | BSFC (g/kWh) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3000 | 1.20 | 0.894 | 0.247 | 247.4 |
| 4500 | 1.55 | 1.155 | 0.191 | 191.4 |
| 6000 | 1.80 | 1.341 | 0.172 | 172.1 |
| 7500 | 1.95 | 1.453 | 0.170 | 169.7 |

**Step 3: Brake Thermal Efficiency at maximum BP (7500 RPM, BP = 8.562 kW, $\dot{m}_f$ = 1.453 kg/h):**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{8.562 \times 3600}{1.453 \times 42\,500} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{30\,823.2}{61\,752.5} \times 100 = 49.91 \, \%$$

Wait — let me re-check. The units of BP must be in kJ/h, not kW, when multiplied by 3600 s/h. Since $1 \text{ kW} = 1 \text{ kJ/s} = 3600 \text{ kJ/h}$, we have:

$$BP \text{ in kJ/h} = 8.562 \times 3600 = 30\,823.2 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{30\,823.2}{1.453 \times 42\,500} \times 100 = \frac{30\,823.2}{61\,752.5} \times 100 \approx 49.9 \, \%$$

This is unusually high for a petrol engine — it indicates either the test data is approximate or the engine is highly loaded. In KTU exams, $\eta_{th}$ for petrol two-wheelers typically lies between **20 % and 32 %**. The high computed value is a teaching opportunity: the student should check the consistency of test data.

**Step 4: Graphs:** (To be drawn on graph sheet)
- X-axis: Engine Speed N in RPM (0 to 8000)
- Y₁-axis (left): BP in kW (0 to 10)
- Y₂-axis (right): BSFC in g/kWh (0 to 300)
- BP curve: rises from 3.6 kW at 3000 RPM to a maximum of 8.56 kW at 7500 RPM (a near-monotonic rising trend).
- BSFC curve: falls from 247 g/kWh at 3000 RPM to a minimum of 170 g/kWh at 7500 RPM (a decreasing trend in this narrow speed range).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **working principle of a hydraulic dynamometer (water brake)**. Compare it with the **eddy current dynamometer** on the basis of **loading control, energy dissipation, and typical applications** in two-wheeler testing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 110 cc two-wheeler engine is tested on an eddy current dynamometer. At 5500 RPM, the indicated power is **6.20 kW** and the brake torque is **9.85 N·m**. Calculate:
1. Brake power.
2. Frictional power.
3. Mechanical efficiency.
4. Brake specific fuel consumption, if the fuel consumption is **2.4 L/h** and the fuel density is **0.74 kg/L**.
5. Air-fuel ratio, if the air flow rate is **30.6 kg/h**.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

*Sketch of Hydraulic Dynamometer:*

```
                    [Engine Shaft]
                          |
                    [Input Rotor]
                          |
                [Water inside housing]
                          |
        [Stator (stationary) on trunnion bearings]
                          |
                [Reaction torque arm → Load cell]
                          |
                [Water inlet + outlet for cooling]
```

*Working Principle:*  
A hydraulic dynamometer consists of a **rotor** coupled to the engine shaft and a **stationary casing (stator)** filled with water. The rotor is provided with pockets or slots. As the rotor spins, water is thrown outward by centrifugal force and recirculated through the stator pockets, creating a shearing action on the water. This hydrodynamic drag transmits a reaction torque to the stator, which is measured by a force arm resting on a load cell. The load is varied by adjusting the **water level** inside the casing (more water = more drag).

*Comparison Table:*

| Parameter | Eddy Current Dynamometer | Hydraulic Dynamometer |
|---|---|---|
| Loading Control | By DC excitation current (stepless, instant) | By water level (slower, less precise) |
| Energy Dissipation | Heat in rotor, air-cooled by fan | Heat absorbed by circulating water |
| Torque Range | Low to medium (≤ 200 N·m typically) | Medium to very high (up to 5000 N·m) |
| Maintenance | Low (no water lines) | High (water seals, pumps) |
| Suitability for Two-Wheelers | Excellent (chassis dyno) | Limited (large truck/bus engines) |
| Cost | Moderate | High (requires water cooling rig) |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Numerical:**

**Given:** N = 5500 RPM, IP = 6.20 kW, $T_b$ = 9.85 N·m, $\dot{V}_f$ = 2.4 L/h, $\rho_f$ = 0.74 kg/L, $\dot{m}_a$ = 30.6 kg/h.

**Step 1: Brake Power.**

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 5500 \times 9.85}{60\,000}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 5500 \times 9.85}{60\,000} = \frac{340\,378}{60\,000} = 5.673 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Frictional Power.**

$$F_P = IP - BP = 6.20 - 5.673 = 0.527 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Mechanical Efficiency.**

$$\eta_m = \frac{BP}{IP} \times 100 = \frac{5.673}{6.20} \times 100 = 91.5 \, \%$$

This is on the higher side for a 110 cc engine (typical 80–88 %), indicating the engine is well-designed and at peak operating speed.

**Step 4: BSFC.**

$$\dot{m}_f = \dot{V}_f \times \rho_f = 2.4 \times 0.74 = 1.776 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{1.776}{5.673} = 0.313 \text{ kg/kWh} = 313 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Step 5: Air-Fuel Ratio.**

$$AFR = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{30.6}{1.776} = 17.23$$

This is **leaner than stoichiometric** (14.7), indicating the engine is operating in lean-burn cruise mode (typical at 5500 RPM, 75 % WOT, part-throttle fuel-map region for a 110 cc commuter motorcycle).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert $\dot{V}_f$ in L/h to $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/h before computing BSFC. Always state the conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Radian vs. RPM confusion:** The formula $BP = 2\pi N T / 60\,000$ assumes $N$ is in **revolutions per minute**, not per second. Dividing by 60 once is correct; dividing again is a common error.
> 3. **Sign of friction power:** $F_P$ is always **positive** and is computed as $|IP - BP|$. Writing it negative loses one mark.
> 4. **AFR vs. equivalence ratio:** AFR > 14.7 means **lean**; AFR < 14.7 means **rich**. Mixing up these two conditions in viva is a frequent mark-loss.
> 5. **BSFC minimum:** Students often forget to state at which throttle/speed BSFC is minimum. The examiner expects: *"BSFC is minimum near 75 % WOT at mid-RPM, indicating the engine's most efficient operating point."*
> 6. **Stator vs. Rotor:** In an eddy current dyno diagram, the **stator is stationary** and the **rotor rotates**. Reversing these labels loses the diagram mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Recall Points for KTU 2024 PCAUL507:**

- **Dynamometer = device to apply controlled load + measure torque + measure speed** → derive power.
- **Two types of mounting:** Engine dyno (engine removed) vs. Chassis dyno (drive wheel on roller). For college labs, **chassis dyno with eddy current absorber** is the most common.
- **Eddy current dyno principle:** Faraday's induction + Lenz's opposing force. **Stator coil excited by DC**; rotor disc spins; reaction torque is the brake torque.
- **Hydraulic (water-brake) dyno principle:** Hydrodynamic shear on water between rotor and stator; load varied by water level.
- **Brake Power formula (must memorize):**

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T_b}{60\,000} \text{ kW}$$

- **BSFC formula (must memorize):** $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/h) divided by BP (kW) → unit kg/kWh.
- **Mechanical Efficiency:** $\eta_m = BP / IP$. Typical two-wheeler: **75–88 %**.
- **Volumetric Efficiency:** $\eta_v = \dot{m}_a / (\rho_a \times V_d \times N / 120)$ for 4-stroke.
- **AFR stoichiometric for petrol ≈ 14.7**, for diesel ≈ 14.5, for CNG ≈ 17.2.
- **BSFC minimum = best fuel economy**; occurs at **mid-RPM, 75 % WOT** (the "sweet spot").
- **Power peaks at the highest RPM** at WOT; **torque peaks at 70–80 % of max RPM** at WOT.
- **Standard test conditions:** 25 °C, 99 kPa, 30 % RH (per SAE J1349 / IS 14599).
- **Exhaust gas temperature (EGT)** rises with load; max safe EGT for petrol two-wheeler is **~700 °C**; for diesel **~600 °C**.
- **Frictional power** is found by **motoring the engine** (no fuel, no ignition) at the same speed and reading the dynamometer load.
- **Cooling:** Never shut down a hot dyno rotor or a hot engine abruptly — let it idle for 2–3 minutes to prevent thermal shock.
- **Safety in lab:** Always wear safety glasses, keep loose clothing away from the rotating drum, and never attempt to reach into the dyno enclosure while the drum is spinning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** Out of the four common performance curves (torque, power, BSFC, $\eta_v$), the ones most frequently asked in 14-mark Part B questions are **Power vs. Speed** and **BSFC vs. Speed**. Practise plotting these with at least 5 data points, marking peak values, and identifying the "sweet spot."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance testing of hybrid vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL507 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance testing of hybrid vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Testing of Hybrid Vehicles** is the systematic, standardized experimental procedure used to quantitatively evaluate the dynamic, energetic, and environmental behavior of a hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) under controlled laboratory or on-road conditions. The test quantifies parameters such as **fuel economy (km/l or mpg)**, **energy consumption (kWh/100 km)**, **state-of-charge (SOC) dynamics**, **all-electric range (AER)**, **total system efficiency**, **exhaust emissions (CO, HC, NOx, CO₂, PM)**, and **gradeability/acceleration performance** over standardized drive cycles.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCAUL507 – Automotive Electrical Lab**, this experiment falls under **Module Experiment 2**, where students are expected to demonstrate hands-on understanding of the dual powertrain (Internal Combustion Engine + Electric Machine) by subjecting a hybrid vehicle (or a hybrid test rig) to a defined duty cycle, recording telemetry, and computing performance indices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid Vehicle Classification (as per SAE J1715 / ISO 23274):**
> - **HEV (Hybrid Electric Vehicle):** Combines ICE + Electric machine, no external charging.
> - **PHEV (Plug-in Hybrid):** Externally rechargeable, larger battery.
> - **FCEV (Fuel Cell Hybrid):** Fuel cell + battery + electric motor.
> - **MHEV (Mild Hybrid):** 48V system, start/stop + torque assist only.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are testing **two cooks working together in one kitchen** — one uses a gas stove (Internal Combustion Engine) and the other uses an electric induction stove (Electric Motor with Battery). Performance testing is like running a timed cooking competition:

- **Speed test (Acceleration):** How fast can they finish a 5-course meal together?
- **Efficiency test (Fuel Economy):** Who uses less energy (gas + electricity) to cook the same meal?
- **Endurance test (Range):** How many meals can they cook before the gas cylinder runs out OR the induction stove's battery dies?
- **Emissions test (Pollution):** How much smoke and smell does the kitchen produce per meal?

The genius of the hybrid is that the two cooks **share the workload intelligently** — the gas stove handles highway cruising, the induction stove handles city traffic with regenerative braking (recovering energy from stirring motion), and they cooperate during hill climbs. **Performance testing measures how intelligently this cooperation actually happens.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Performance Metrics (Standardized Units):**
> - **Fuel Consumption:** litres/100 km or km/litre
> - **Energy Consumption:** kWh/100 km
> - **CO₂ Emissions:** g/km
> - **All-Electric Range (AER):** km
> - **Combined System Efficiency:** % (Mechanical Output ÷ Energy Input)
> - **SOC Window:** % (e.g., 30% – 80%)

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

The Indian automotive sector is rapidly transitioning to **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI) Phase 2** norms, with **FAME-II subsidies** aggressively promoting hybrid and electric vehicles. The KTU 2024 curriculum mandates that every B.Tech Automobile Engineering graduate must be able to:
1. Select appropriate test cycles (e.g., **MIDC – Modified Indian Drive Cycle**).
2. Operate chassis dynamometers and OBD-II data loggers.
3. Compute combined fuel + electric energy consumption (the **utility factor weighted** metric).
4. Critically analyze SOC trajectories to validate hybrid control strategy effectiveness.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Hybrid Powertrain Under Test

A typical **parallel or series-parallel (power-split) hybrid** powertrain being tested contains:

- **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE):** Petrol/Diesel, output measured at flywheel.
- **Electric Machine (EM):** Either a Motor-Generator (MG1) or Traction Motor (MG2).
- **Traction Battery Pack:** Li-ion / NiMH, nominal voltage (e.g., **201.6 V** for Toyota THS-II).
- **Power Control Unit (PCU):** Contains inverter and DC-DC converter.
- **Planetary Gear Set (for Toyota-style THS):** Splits power between ICE, MG1, MG2.
- **Final Drive & Wheels:** Where output torque/speed is measured.

### 2.2 Types of Performance Tests Conducted

The KTU lab manual groups these tests into **six canonical categories**:

| Test Type | What it Measures | Drive Cycle Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel Economy Test** | Litres of fuel per 100 km | MIDC, NEDC, WLTP |
| **Energy Consumption Test** | kWh from grid + fuel energy | WLTP Class 3 |
| **Emission Test** | g/km of CO, HC, NOx, PM | MIDC (chassis dyno + CVS) |
| **All-Electric Range (AER) Test** | Distance covered in pure EV mode | UDDS or LA92 |
| **Acceleration & Gradeability** | 0–100 km/h time, max grade (%) | Constant acceleration profile |
| **SOC Balancing Test** | Battery SOC swing over cycle | Repeat cycle until SOC stable |

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Test Step

**Step 1: Preconditioning the Vehicle**
- *Why?* Battery temperature and SOC affect efficiency (cold battery loses 15–20% capacity).
- *How?* Soak vehicle at 20–30°C for 12–24 hours, then drive one complete warm-up cycle.

**Step 2: Mounting on Chassis Dynamometer**
- *Why?* Reproducibility — on-road testing has wind/gradient variability.
- *How?* Vehicle wheels rest on rollers; inertia flywheels (e.g., 1500 kg) simulate vehicle mass; an eddy-current or AC dynamometer absorbs road load.

**Step 3: Running the Standardized Drive Cycle**
- *Why?* Allows global comparability of results.
- *How?* A robotic driver follows a speed-vs-time trace (e.g., MIDC: 6 minutes 30 seconds, max speed 90 km/h).

**Step 4: Data Acquisition**
- *Why?* Compute derived metrics.
- *How?* OBD-II port, CAN bus logger, exhaust gas analyzer (Horiba OBS-ONE), and PEMS (Portable Emissions Measurement System).

**Step 5: Post-Processing**
- *Why?* Convert raw signals to engineering quantities.
- *How?* Apply **carbon balance method** to compute fuel consumption from CO₂ emissions when direct gravimetric measurement is unavailable.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains all high-yield formulas tested in KTU ESE & continuous evaluation. Memorize units carefully.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fuel Consumption (Volumetric)** | $FC = \dfrac{V_{fuel}}{D} \times 100$ | L/100 km | $V_{fuel}$ = fuel volume, $D$ = distance |
| 2 | **Fuel Consumption (Mass)** | $m_f = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{0.8643}$ for petrol | g/km | Carbon balance method (UNECE R101) |
| 3 | **Energy Density of Petrol** | $E_{fuel} = m_f \times 44{,}000$ | kJ | Lower Heating Value (LHV) ≈ 44 MJ/kg |
| 4 | **Electrical Energy Used** | $E_{elec} = \dfrac{\int V_{bat} \cdot I_{bat} \, dt}{3600}$ | kWh | Integrate over full cycle |
| 5 | **Combined Energy Consumption (PHEV)** | $EC_{comb} = UF \cdot EC_{ev} + (1-UF) \cdot EC_{cs}$ | kWh/100 km | $UF$ = Utility Factor |
| 6 | **Utility Factor (SAE J2841)** | $UF = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot AER}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda$ = decay constant |
| 7 | **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption** | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{brake}}$ | g/kWh | $P_{brake}$ in kW |
| 8 | **Hybrid System Efficiency** | $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{P_{fuel} + P_{elec,in}}$ | % | Wall-to-wheel basis |
| 9 | **Wheel Power** | $P_w = F_t \cdot v = (m \cdot g \cdot f + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3) + m \cdot a \cdot v$ | W | $F_t$ = tractive force |
| 10 | **Tractive Force** | $F_t = m \cdot g \cdot (f \cos\theta + \sin\theta) + \dfrac{\rho C_d A v^2}{2} + m \cdot a$ | N | $\theta$ = grade angle |
| 11 | **Rolling Resistance** | $F_r = m \cdot g \cdot f \cdot \cos\theta$ | N | $f$ ≈ 0.012 for car tyres |
| 12 | **Aerodynamic Drag** | $F_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | N | $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³ air |
| 13 | **SOC Change** | $\Delta SOC = \dfrac{\int I_{bat} \, dt}{Q_{bat}} \times 100$ | % | $Q_{bat}$ in Ah |
| 14 | **Equivalent Electric Range (PHEV)** | $AER_{eq} = \dfrac{E_{bat,usable}}{EC_{comb}}$ | km | Useful for consumer comparison |
| 15 | **CO₂ from Fuel** | $CO_2 = m_f \times 3.16$ (petrol) | g | Stoichiometric ratio |
| 16 | **Regen Braking Efficiency** | $\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{kinetic}}$ | % | Typically 30–60% |
| 17 | **Vehicle Inertia (Dyno Simulation)** | $I_{eq} = m_{vehicle} \cdot r_{wheel}^2$ | kg·m² | Set on flywheels |

> **Standard constants to memorize (bold for quick recall):**
> - Air density: **$\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
> - Petrol LHV: **$44 \text{ MJ/kg}$**
> - Carbon fraction in petrol: **$0.8643$**
> - Petrol density: **$0.745 \text{ kg/L}$**
> - CO₂ per kg petrol: **$3.16 \text{ kg}$**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These tests are mandated by:
- **ARAI (Automotive Research Association of India)** for type approval under CMVR.
- **MoRTH** for BS-VI compliance.
- **International:** UNECE R101 (CO₂/fuel), EPA Tier 3 (USA), Euro 6d (EU).

Performance testing directly informs:
- **Consumer labelling** (e.g., the yellow fuel-consumption sticker on new cars).
- **Fleet management decisions** (B2B sales).
- **Hybrid control strategy calibration** (when to start ICE, when to use EV mode).
- **Subsidy eligibility** under FAME-II (PHEVs must meet minimum AER of 25 km).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Carbon Balance Method for Fuel Consumption

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **most frequently asked Part A question** in KTU ESE for automotive labs. Understand every line.

**Starting Principle (Stoichiometry of Petrol Combustion):**
Petrol is approximated as **iso-octane (C₈H₁₈)**. Complete combustion reaction:

$$C_8H_{18} + 12.5 O_2 \rightarrow 8 CO_2 + 9 H_2O$$

**Step 1 — Mass fractions from molecular weights:**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{C_8H_{18}} &= 8(12) + 18(1) = 114 \text{ g/mol} \\
M_{CO_2} \text{ produced} &= 8 \times 44 = 352 \text{ g} \\
M_{H_2O} \text{ produced} &= 9 \times 18 = 162 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Carbon mass in fuel:**

$$\text{Carbon in fuel} = 8 \times 12 = 96 \text{ g} \quad (\text{out of } 114 \text{ g fuel})$$

$$\text{Carbon fraction } (f_C) = \dfrac{96}{114} = 0.8421$$

**Step 3 — Mass of CO₂ per kg of fuel:**

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ per kg fuel} = f_C \times \dfrac{M_{CO_2}}{M_C} = 0.8421 \times \dfrac{44}{12} = 0.8421 \times 3.667$$

$$= 3.088 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ per kg fuel (theoretical, pure iso-octane)}$$

**Step 4 — Correction for real petrol (C/H ratio varies):**

Real petrol is not pure C₈H₁₈. Empirically, **0.8643** is the carbon fraction of reference petrol (UNECE R101).

$$m_{CO_2} \text{ (g/km)} = m_f \text{ (g/km)} \times 0.8643 \times \dfrac{44}{12}$$

$$\boxed{m_f = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{3.1681} \quad \text{(g of fuel per km)}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert g/km to L/100 km:**

$$\text{FC (L/100 km)} = \dfrac{m_f / \rho_{fuel}}{D} \times 100 = \dfrac{m_f}{D \cdot \rho_{fuel}} \times 100$$

With $m_f$ in g/km, $D = 1$ km, $\rho_{fuel} = 745$ g/L:

$$\text{FC} = \dfrac{m_f}{745} \times 100 \quad \text{L/100 km}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Utility Factor Weighted Energy Consumption (PHEV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PHEVs run partly on grid electricity and partly on fuel. The combined metric is mandated by SAE J2841 and UNECE R101.

**Step 1 — Define the Utility Factor (UF):**

The UF is the **fraction of daily trips** that can be completed within the All-Electric Range.

**Step 2 — SAE J2841 exponential model:**

$$UF = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot d}$$

Where:
- $d$ = daily distance (km)
- $\lambda$ = 0.0156 km⁻¹ (statistical fit to NREL driving data)

**Step 3 — Combined formula:**

$$\boxed{EC_{combined} = UF \cdot EC_{EV} + (1 - UF) \cdot EC_{CS}}$$

Where:
- $EC_{EV}$ = energy consumption in pure electric mode (kWh/100 km)
- $EC_{CS}$ = energy consumption in charge-sustaining mode (kWh/100 km equivalent)

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (typical PHEV):**

Given: $EC_{EV} = 14$ kWh/100 km, $EC_{CS} = 6$ L/100 km petrol, daily distance = 50 km, petrol LHV = 8.76 kWh/L.

$$UF = 1 - e^{-0.0156 \times 50} = 1 - e^{-0.78} = 1 - 0.458 = 0.542$$

Convert fuel to energy: $EC_{CS} = 6 \times 8.76 = 52.56$ kWh/100 km (energy equivalent).

$$EC_{combined} = 0.542 \times 14 + 0.458 \times 52.56$$

$$= 7.588 + 24.07 = 31.66 \text{ kWh/100 km equivalent}$$

This shows **PHEVs are highly energy-thirsty when operated as normal hybrids** but very efficient on short trips — a critical insight for KTU valuation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Wheel Power and Tractive Force Equation

**Step 1 — Energy balance at the wheel:**

The ICE + EM combined output must overcome four resistive forces:

$$P_{wheel} = P_{rolling} + P_{aero} + P_{grade} + P_{acceleration}$$

**Step 2 — Express each as force × velocity:**

$$F_t \cdot v = F_r \cdot v + F_d \cdot v + F_g \cdot v + m \cdot a \cdot v$$

Dividing through by $v$:

$$F_t = F_r + F_d + F_g + m \cdot a$$

**Step 3 — Substitute standard expressions:**

$$\boxed{F_t = m \cdot g \cdot f \cdot \cos\theta + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot a}$$

Where:
- $m$ = vehicle mass (kg)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²
- $f$ = rolling resistance coefficient
- $\rho$ = air density (1.225 kg/m³)
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient
- $A$ = frontal area (m²)
- $v$ = vehicle speed (m/s)
- $\theta$ = road gradient (radians)
- $a$ = acceleration (m/s²)

**Step 4 — Compute wheel power:**

$$P_w = F_t \cdot v$$

For a typical car cruising at 100 km/h (27.78 m/s) on level road:
- $m$ = 1500 kg
- $f$ = 0.012
- $C_d$ = 0.30
- $A$ = 2.2 m²
- $\theta$ = 0, $a$ = 0

$$\begin{aligned}
F_r &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 176.6 \text{ N} \\
F_d &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (27.78)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.66 \times 771.6 \\
&= 311.9 \text{ N} \\
F_t &= 176.6 + 311.9 = 488.5 \text{ N} \\
P_w &= 488.5 \times 27.78 = 13{,}571 \text{ W} = 13.57 \text{ kW} \approx 18.2 \text{ hp}
\end{aligned}$$

This explains why hybrids use **engine-off coasting** at cruise — the 13.57 kW is met partly by battery alone for short periods.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Test Procedure → Output Mapping

| Test Stage | Instrument | Raw Output | Derived Output | Validation Criteria |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vehicle soak | Climate chamber | T = 25°C | Stabilized thermal state | ΔT < 1°C/hour |
| Dyno setup | Inertia flywheels | Inertia set to 1500 kg | Simulated mass | ±10 kg |
| Cycle execution | Robot driver | Speed trace | Deviation from profile | ±2 km/h tolerance |
| Fuel measurement | Gravimetric meter | Δm = 2.4 kg over 100 km | FC = 4.0 L/100 km | Repeat within 1% |
| Battery data | CAN bus log | $V_{bat}(t)$, $I_{bat}(t)$ | E_electric in kWh | Coulomb counting |
| Emission sampling | CVS + analyzer | $m_{CO_2}$, $m_{NO_x}$ | g/km | ARAI limit 113 g/km (BS-VI) |
| SOC analysis | BMS data | SOC start, SOC end | ΔSOC ≈ 0 (charge-sustaining) | ±2% drift acceptable |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Hybrid Vehicle Performance Test Setup

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete signal flow** during a hybrid vehicle performance test, from driver input to data acquisition.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Driver / Robot Driver Pedal Input] --> B[Hybrid Control Unit ECU]
    B --> C{Energy Management Strategy}
    C -- EV mode --> D1[Power Control Unit Inverter]
    C -- Engine mode --> D2[Internal Combustion Engine]
    C -- Hybrid mode --> D3[Both ICE and EM]
    D1 --> E[Traction Motor MG2]
    D2 --> F[Planetary Gear Set Power Split]
    D3 --> F
    F --> G[Final Drive and Wheels]
    G --> H[Chassis Dynamometer Rollers]
    H --> I[Eddy Current Dynamometer Absorber]
    H --> J[Inertia Flywheels]
    H --> K[OBD II CAN Bus Data Logger]
    D1 --> L[Battery Management System BMS]
    L --> M[Traction Battery Pack Li-ion]
    I --> N[Data Acquisition System DAQ]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    O[Exhaust Gas Sampling Probe] --> P[Constant Volume Sampler CVS]
    P --> Q[Emission Analyzer CO HC NOx CO2]
    Q --> N
    N --> R[PC with Test Software AVL Cruise MATLAB]
    R --> S[Performance Report FC EC AER Emissions]
```

### 4.2 Hybrid Energy Flow Diagram During Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P[Powertrain]
        ICE[Internal Combustion Engine]
        MG1[MG1 Generator]
        MG2[MG2 Traction Motor]
        BAT[Battery Pack]
    end
    subgraph W[Drivetrain]
        PG[Planetary Gearset]
        FD[Final Drive]
        WH[Wheels and Tires]
    end
    subgraph E[Environment]
        RD[Rolling Resistance]
        AD[Aerodynamic Drag]
        GR[Road Gradient]
    end
    ICE --> PG
    PG --> MG1
    PG --> MG2
    MG2 --> FD
    FD --> WH
    MG1 --> BAT
    BAT --> MG2
    WH --> RD
    WH --> AD
    WH --> GR
    MG2 -->|Regen| BAT
```

### 4.3 Drive Cycle Test Sequence (MIDC Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1: Vehicle Preconditioning 25 deg C soak] --> S2[Step 2: Mount on Chassis Dynamometer]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Set Inertia and Road Load]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Reset Fuel Meter and BMS]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Run MIDC Phase 1 Urban 197 sec]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Run MIDC Phase 2 Extra Urban 400 sec]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Record End SOC and Fuel Mass]
    S7 --> S8{Did SOC drift more than 2 percent}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Run Additional Cycle for SOC Stabilization]
    S8 -- No --> S10[Compute Final FC and Emissions]
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Generate ARAI Format Test Report]
```

### 4.4 Hybrid Mode Decision Logic (Reference for Lab Viva)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Demands Torque Td] --> B{SOC greater than 30 percent}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is Vehicle Speed less than 50 km/h}
    B -- No --> H[Start ICE Immediately]
    C -- Yes --> D[Pure EV Mode MG2 only]
    C -- No --> E{Is Torque Demand less than 70 Nm}
    D --> F[SOC drops by 0.5 percent per km]
    E -- Yes --> G[EV Mode Continue]
    E -- No --> H
    G --> I[SOC below 30 percent triggers ICE]
    H --> J[ICE Active MG1 Charges Battery]
    J --> K[Decide MG2 Assist if Torque demand high]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define "All-Electric Range (AER)" of a Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle. State the standard test procedure for measuring AER as per UNECE R101.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **All-Electric Range (AER)** is the maximum distance a plug-in hybrid electric vehicle (PHEV) or battery electric vehicle (BEV) can travel in **pure electric mode** (with the ICE off) starting from a fully charged battery, under a prescribed drive cycle on a chassis dynamometer.

**Test Procedure (UNECE R101 / SAE J1634):**
1. Fully charge the traction battery using a standard AC charger (Mode 2 or Mode 3).
2. Soak vehicle at 20–30°C for 12 hours.
3. Mount on chassis dynamometer with road-load coefficients set to declared values.
4. Run the **WLTP Class 3 cycle** continuously.
5. Record the moment vehicle speed drops below cycle requirement OR ICE starts automatically.
6. **AER = cumulative distance** from start to ICE-on moment.
7. Repeat 3 times; report average.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for procedure)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Explain the "Carbon Balance Method" used to compute fuel consumption of a hybrid vehicle during a chassis dynamometer test.**

**Model Answer:**

> The carbon balance method computes fuel consumption indirectly by measuring the **mass of CO₂ in exhaust gases**, avoiding the need for gravimetric fuel measurement which is impractical on a dynamometer.

**Principle:** Since combustion converts all carbon in fuel to CO₂, knowing the CO₂ mass allows back-calculation of fuel mass.

**Formula (as per UNECE R101):**

$$m_f = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{0.8643 \times 3.667} = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{3.1681}$$

The **0.8643** is the carbon fraction of reference petrol; **3.667 = 44/12** is the molar mass ratio of CO₂ to C.

**Steps:**
1. Exhaust gas is collected by **Constant Volume Sampler (CVS)**.
2. CO₂ concentration is measured by **NDIR analyzer** (typically 0–20% range).
3. Total CO₂ mass = (concentration × CVS flow × time).
4. Convert g/km to L/100 km using fuel density 0.745 kg/L.

*(3 marks: 1 for principle, 1 for formula, 1 for procedure)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Mark Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Set A]**

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **complete test setup** for measuring fuel economy and emissions of a hybrid electric vehicle on a chassis dynamometer. List the instruments used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hybrid vehicle of mass **1500 kg** has frontal area **2.2 m²** and drag coefficient **0.30**. The rolling resistance coefficient is **0.012**. Calculate the **tractive force and wheel power** required to cruise at a constant **100 km/h on a 5% gradient**. Take air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Test Setup (7 marks):**

The complete test setup for a hybrid vehicle performance test consists of:

```
[Vehicle] → [Chassis Dyno Rollers] → [Inertia Flywheels + Eddy Current Absorber]
            ↓
        [Exhaust] → [CVS Unit] → [Emission Analyzer (CO, HC, NOx, CO₂)]
            ↓
        [OBD-II / CAN Bus] → [DAQ System] → [PC with AVL/MATLAB]
            ↓
        [Driver Robot / Human] follows [WLTP / MIDC Speed Trace]
```

**Key Instruments:**
1. **Chassis dynamometer** (single-roller or twin-roller) with road-load simulation.
2. **Inertia flywheels** (e.g., 1500 kg equivalent).
3. **Exhaust gas analyzer** (Horiba OBS-ONE series).
4. **Constant Volume Sampler (CVS)** for bag collection.
5. **OBD-II scanner** for SOC, motor RPM, battery current/voltage.
6. **Fuel flow meter** (gravimetric, AVL 735S).
7. **Data acquisition system** with ≥10 Hz sampling.
8. **Climate chamber** for preconditioning (20–30°C).
9. **Robot driver** for cycle-following.
10. **Power analyzer** for AC mains (during charging).

*(Allocation: 2 marks for block diagram, 2 marks for instruments, 2 marks for procedure, 1 mark for standards cited)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $m = 1500$ kg
- $A = 2.2$ m²
- $C_d = 0.30$
- $f = 0.012$
- $v = 100$ km/h = $\dfrac{100}{3.6} = 27.78$ m/s
- $\theta = \arctan(0.05) = 2.862°$  →  $\sin\theta = 0.0499$, $\cos\theta = 0.9988$
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Rolling resistance force**

$$F_r = m \cdot g \cdot f \cdot \cos\theta = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 0.9988$$

$$F_r = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Aerodynamic drag force**

$$F_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (27.78)^2$$

$$F_d = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.66 \times 771.6 = 311.9 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Grade climbing force**

$$F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.0499 = 734.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Total tractive force** (constant speed, $a = 0$)

$$F_t = F_r + F_d + F_g = 176.58 + 311.9 + 734.3 = 1222.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Wheel power**

$$P_w = F_t \times v = 1222.8 \times 27.78 = 33{,}969 \text{ W} = 33.97 \text{ kW} \approx 45.5 \text{ hp}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Examiner's Pitfall List:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** — KTU awards 0 marks if unit error propagates.
> 2. **Using tan(5°) instead of sin(5°)** — for small angles $\tan\theta \approx \sin\theta$, but examiners expect the **exact** formula $\sin\theta$.
> 3. **Assuming $\cos\theta \approx 1$** without stating the approximation — always substitute 0.9988.
> 4. **Forgetting the $a = 0$ condition** — if question says "constant speed", no inertia term.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the **Utility Factor (UF)** concept used for combined energy consumption reporting of Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles. A PHEV has an **AER of 50 km**, and its energy consumption in pure EV mode is **15 kWh/100 km** and in charge-sustaining mode is **5 L/100 km petrol** (LHV = 8.76 kWh/L). The average daily driving distance is **30 km**. Calculate the **weighted combined energy consumption** in kWh/100 km. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the procedure to measure **State-of-Charge (SOC) variation** of a hybrid vehicle battery over a complete MIDC test cycle, and explain how SOC balance validates the hybrid control strategy. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Utility Factor Calculation (7 marks):**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**

The Utility Factor (UF) represents the **statistical probability** that a given daily trip can be completed entirely on electric energy, based on the ratio of trip distance to AER. SAE J2841 defines:

$$UF = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot d}$$

with $\lambda = 0.0156$ km⁻¹ (this is a globally averaged value derived from NREL's "Trip Energy" study of real-world driving).

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

Given:
- $AER = 50$ km
- $d = 30$ km (daily distance)
- $EC_{EV} = 15$ kWh/100 km
- $EC_{CS} = 5$ L/100 km = $5 \times 8.76 = 43.8$ kWh/100 km (energy equivalent)

**Step 1: Compute UF**

$$UF = 1 - e^{-0.0156 \times 30} = 1 - e^{-0.468}$$

$$e^{-0.468} = 0.626$$

$$UF = 1 - 0.626 = 0.374$$

**Step 2: Apply weighted formula**

$$EC_{combined} = UF \cdot EC_{EV} + (1-UF) \cdot EC_{CS}$$

$$= 0.374 \times 15 + 0.626 \times 43.8$$

$$= 5.61 + 27.42 = 33.03 \text{ kWh/100 km}$$

**Interpretation:** The PHEV uses **33 kWh/100 km** of combined (well-to-wheel equivalent) energy — surprisingly high because $d < AER$ is not the case (30 < 50), but the trip still often exceeds pure EV range due to speed/load variations. Real-world AER is often 30–40% lower than the certified value.

---

**Part (b) — SOC Variation Measurement (7 marks):**

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Fully charge** the traction battery (100% SOC) and let it rest for 4 hours.
2. Connect **CAN bus logger** to read BMS messages (typically message ID 0x6B2 for SOC, every 100 ms).
3. Run the MIDC cycle (or repeat it 4–5 times consecutively).
4. Log $V_{bat}(t)$, $I_{bat}(t)$, $T_{bat}(t)$, and $SOC(t)$ at 10 Hz.
5. After each cycle, allow a **5-minute rest** to capture SOC rebound.
6. Plot SOC vs. time.

**Validation of Control Strategy (3 marks):**

> A **well-designed hybrid control strategy** should:
> 1. **Maintain SOC within a "swing window"** (typically 30% – 80%) to ensure long battery life.
> 2. Show a **net-zero SOC change** at the end of the complete cycle (charge-sustaining).
> 3. Use **engine-off coasting** during low-load phases, visible as flat SOC periods.
> 4. Engage **regenerative braking** at decelerations — visible as **sharp SOC upward spikes** (e.g., +0.5% in 2 seconds).
> 5. Defer engine start to **above 30% SOC** to avoid deep discharge damage.

**Example SOC Trajectory Interpretation:**
- 0–60s: SOC drops from 70% → 68% (EV launch)
- 60–90s: SOC constant (engine cruise, no draw)
- 90–110s: SOC jumps to 70% (regen braking)
- Conclusion: **CD-CS mode validated**, control strategy is correct.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfalls for Question B:**
> 1. **Wrong λ value** — KTU uses **0.0156** strictly; using 0.012 or 0.02 → 0 marks.
> 2. **Not converting L/100 km to kWh/100 km** — common error worth losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing UF with SOC** — UF is statistical, SOC is instantaneous.
> 4. **Not mentioning battery rest period** before logging — required by UNECE R101.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE (Module 2, Experiment 2):**

- ✅ **Hybrid Vehicle Types:** HEV, PHEV, FCEV, MHEV — know definitions cold.
- ✅ **Standard Drive Cycles:** MIDC (India), NEDC, WLTP (global) — durations and max speeds.
- ✅ **Key Instruments:** Chassis dyno, CVS, OBD-II, gravimetric fuel meter, Horiba analyzer.
- ✅ **Carbon Balance Formula:** $m_f = m_{CO_2} / 3.1681$ — must be memorized.
- ✅ **Tractive Force Equation:** Sum of rolling + drag + grade + inertia — must derive from scratch.
- ✅ **Utility Factor:** $UF = 1 - e^{-0.0156 d}$ — exact constant required.
- ✅ **SOC Window:** 30% – 80% for charge-sustaining HEVs.
- ✅ **AER Test Standard:** UNECE R101, start with 100% SOC, log until ICE starts.
- ✅ **Regen Braking Efficiency:** typically 30–60%; cannot exceed 100% (lost to heat).
- ✅ **LHV of petrol:** 44 MJ/kg = 8.76 kWh/L — used in energy calculations.
- ✅ **Air density:** 1.225 kg/m³ at sea level.
- ✅ **Difference between BSFC and BSEC** — BSFC for ICE, BSEC for electric motor.
- ✅ **Standards to cite:** UNECE R101, SAE J1715, SAE J1634, SAE J2841, ISO 23274, ARAI MoRTH CMVR.
- ✅ **Examiner's pet question:** "Why use chassis dynamometer instead of on-road?" → Reproducibility + weather + gradient control.
- ✅ **Examiner's pet question:** "Why does a hybrid consume less fuel in city than highway?" → Regen + engine-off coasting only useful in stop-go traffic.

> **Final KTU Pro-Tip:** When answering any hybrid performance question, **always state the applicable standard (UNECE / SAE / ARAI)** at the top. Examiners award 0.5–1 bonus mark for this habit.

---

# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: SERVICING AND MAINTENANCE OF POWER WINDOW SYSTEM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Window System**: An automotive electrical subsystem that uses **electrically operated reversible DC motors** coupled to mechanical regulators (scissor-type, cable-type, or single-arm type) to raise and lower door glasses (window panes) at the command of the driver or passengers via a switch interface.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Automobile Engineering curriculum, a *Power Window System* is classified as a **Body Electronics / Comfort System** load under the **Low-Voltage Automotive Electrical Architecture (12 V / 24 V DC)**. The system is governed by a **parallel circuit topology** with each door functioning as an independent sub-circuit, all of which are typically protected by a **centralized fuse and relay module** inside the cabin fuse box.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Think of a power window system as a "**Robotic Arm for your Window**." Imagine you are sitting inside a car and the glass is heavy (each glass weighs roughly **4 to 8 kg**). Lifting it manually every time is tiring. The power window is essentially a small electric motor (like the motor in a child's battery-powered toy car) connected to a mechanical "**scissor lift**" or "**pulleys and cables**" mechanism (similar to an elevator). When you press a switch, the motor spins, which moves the mechanism, which pushes the glass up or pulls it down — all within **3 to 5 seconds**.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics & Standards

| Parameter | Standard Specification |
|---|---|
| Operating Voltage | **12 V DC** (Passenger cars) / **24 V DC** (Commercial vehicles) |
| Motor Type | **Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) Reversible Motor** |
| Rated Power | **60 W to 150 W** per motor |
| Stall Current | **25 A to 30 A** (thermal fuse trips at this point) |
| Operating Current | **5 A to 8 A** (normal glass movement) |
| Glass Travel Time | **3 to 5 seconds** (full open to full close) |
| Stall Force | **8 kgf to 12 kgf** minimum |
| Endurance Rating | **20,000 to 30,000 cycles** (industry standard) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: For the KTU lab viva, the examiner frequently asks: *"What is the typical operating voltage and motor type of a power window?"* — The answer must be **12 V DC PMDC reversible motor**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture & Working Principle

The power window system operates on the principle of **electromechanical energy conversion**. When the driver presses the window switch:

1. **Switch Actuation**: A momentary DPDT (Double Pole Double Throw) switch closes one of two circuits (UP or DOWN), routing battery current to the motor field windings in a specific polarity.
2. **Motor Operation**: The PMDC motor receives current and rotates either **clockwise or counter-clockwise** depending on polarity.
3. **Mechanical Coupling**: The motor's rotational motion is transferred to the **window regulator** (scissor, cable, or tape drive).
4. **Linear Conversion**: The regulator converts rotary motion into linear motion, which slides the glass along the door channel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PMDC Motors?**: Permanent Magnet DC motors are preferred over field-wound motors because they have a **high starting torque** (essential for lifting glass from rest), **reversible direction capability** (just reverse polarity), and **compact size** (fits inside door cavities).

## 2.2 Power Window Circuit — High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Law | Expression | Application Context | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ohm's Law | $V = I \times R$ | Calculating voltage drop across switch contacts | V, A, Ω |
| Power Dissipation | $P = V \times I$ | Motor power consumption calculation | Watts (W) |
| Motor Speed (Back-EMF) | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | Determining motor speed under load | Volts (V) |
| Torque Equation | $T = K_t \times \Phi \times I_a$ | Calculating starting torque for glass lift | N·m |
| Stall Current | $I_{stall} = V / R_a$ | Critical for fuse/breaker sizing | Amperes (A) |
| Energy per Cycle | $E = P \times t$ | Battery drain per window operation | Joules (J) |
| Voltage Drop (Line Loss) | $V_{drop} = I \times R_{wire} \times L$ | Sizing wire gauge for door harness | Volts (V) |
| Heat Generation | $H = I^2 \times R \times t$ | Thermal fuse trip time calculation | Joules (J) |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility**: The stall current formula is used by automotive designers to size the **thermal circuit breaker** (typically **25 A to 30 A**) that protects the wiring harness. If the glass jams (e.g., frozen to rubber seal), the motor stalls, current surges to stall value, and the breaker trips within **3 to 8 seconds** to prevent fire.

## 2.3 Components of a Modern Power Window System

1. **Master Control Switch (Driver-side)** — Contains 4 switches (FL, FR, RL, RR) plus a **lock-out switch** that disables rear window switches (child safety).
2. **Individual Door Switches** — Single DPDT switch at each passenger door.
3. **PMDC Reversible Motor** — One per door, typically rated **12 V, 80 W**.
4. **Window Regulator** — Mechanical linkage: **X-ARM (scissor)**, **CABLE DRIVE**, or **FLEXIBLE TAPE DRIVE**.
5. **Thermal Circuit Breaker (Auto-Reset)** — Protects each motor from stall/overload.
6. **Wiring Harness** — Typically **18 AWG to 16 AWG** copper stranded wire.
7. **Fuse Box** — Main fuse rated **30 A** feeds the master switch.
8. **Ground Path** — Returns through door hinge ground straps (often a failure point).

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Press UP switch** → Current flows: **Battery (+) → Fuse → UP contact → Motor (+ terminal) → Motor (− terminal) → DOWN contact → Ground**.
- **Press DOWN switch** → Current flows in **reverse polarity** through the motor → motor rotates the opposite direction → glass descends.
- **Release switch** → DPDT returns to neutral (center OFF) → motor stops → glass holds position due to **worm gear self-locking** in regulator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Self-Locking Principle**: Most power window regulators use a **worm and wheel gear** at the motor output. The worm gear's geometry inherently prevents back-driving, meaning the glass **cannot slide down on its own** when the motor is off — this is a critical safety feature.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stall Current and Fuse Sizing

When a power window motor stalls (e.g., glass reaches top, frozen seal, or obstruction), the back-EMF $E_b$ becomes zero. We can derive the stall current.

**Starting Equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around motor loop):**

$$V = E_b + I_a \times R_a$$

**Step 1: Identify the stall condition**

At stall, the rotor is not rotating, therefore no back-EMF is generated.

$$E_b = 0$$

**Step 2: Substitute the stall condition into the KVL equation**

$$V = 0 + I_{stall} \times R_a$$

$$V = I_{stall} \times R_a$$

**Step 3: Solve for the stall current**

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V}{R_a}$$

**Step 4: Apply numerical values (typical PMDC motor)**

Assume a 12 V motor with armature resistance of $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$ (typical for an 80 W motor).

$$I_{stall} = \frac{12\ \text{V}}{0.5\ \Omega}$$

$$I_{stall} = 24\ \text{A}$$

**Step 5: Determine the fuse rating**

Industry practice is to select a fuse rated at **125% to 150%** of the stall current to allow brief inrush but trip on sustained stall.

$$I_{fuse} = 1.25 \times 24\ \text{A} = 30\ \text{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: This is why KTU lab manuals specify a **30 A fuse** in the power window circuit — the calculation above justifies the design choice.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common Faults, Causes, and Servicing Actions

| S.No. | Observed Symptom | Probable Root Cause | Diagnostic Method | Servicing / Repair Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Window does not move in either direction | Blown fuse, broken wire, faulty switch | Check fuse with multimeter; test switch continuity | Replace fuse; repair broken conductor; replace switch |
| 2 | Window moves slowly / labored | Worn motor brushes, dry regulator slides, binding glass channel | Measure motor current under load; visually inspect channel | Replace motor; lubricate regulator pivot points; clean and lubricate rubber channel |
| 3 | Window moves only in one direction | Failed switch contact, open motor brush lead, broken regulator arm | Swap switch with known good unit; check motor terminals with ohmmeter | Replace switch; repair motor; replace regulator |
| 4 | Window operates then suddenly stops | Thermal breaker tripped, motor overheating, binding mechanism | Wait 60 seconds and retry; check for binding | Replace thermal breaker; replace motor; free up mechanism |
| 5 | Glass falls inside door (drops down) | Broken regulator cable, snapped scissor arm, detached glass clamps | Remove door panel and visually inspect | Replace complete regulator assembly |
| 6 | Switch feels loose / no click | Broken switch detent, worn plunger | Remove switch and inspect | Replace switch assembly |
| 7 | Master switch works, individual door switch doesn't | Failed individual switch, broken wire in door boot | Test switch with multimeter in continuity mode | Replace switch; repair door jamb wires |
| 8 | Noise (grinding / clicking) during operation | Worn regulator gear, dry bearings, loose glass | Remove door panel and listen with stethoscope | Lubricate; replace regulator |
| 9 | Window reverses direction (auto-up anti-pinch feature active) | Obstruction in window channel, faulty pinch sensor | Check channel for debris; reset ECU (if equipped) | Clean channel; recalibrate anti-pinch module |
| 10 | Current draw excessive (>15 A) | Motor bearing seizure, regulator jammed | Clamp ammeter around motor wire | Replace motor or regulator |

## 3.3 Servicing Procedure — Standardized KTU Lab Sequence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)** for Power Window Servicing:

**Step 1: Pre-Service Inspection**
- Verify the reported fault with the vehicle owner.
- Operate all four windows through full travel to confirm the issue.
- Check the **battery voltage** (must be > 12.4 V) and **ground strap integrity**.

**Step 2: Visual Inspection**
- Remove the door panel using a **trim removal tool** (avoid scratching paint).
- Inspect the **regulator mechanism** for visible damage, broken cables, or snapped arms.
- Check **wiring harness** for chafing, especially at the door hinge (rubber boot) area.

**Step 3: Electrical Testing**
- Set multimeter to **DC Volts (20 V range)**.
- Probe the **motor connector** while pressing the switch:
  - **UP command**: Expect **+12 V** at one terminal, **0 V** (ground) at the other.
  - **DOWN command**: Expect **+12 V** at the other terminal, **0 V** at the first.
- If voltage is present but motor doesn't run → **Motor is faulty**.
- If voltage is absent → **Switch or wiring fault** is upstream.

**Step 4: Mechanical Servicing**
- Lubricate regulator pivot points with **white lithium grease** or **silicone spray**.
- Clean glass run channels with a **mild soap solution**; apply **silicone dressing** to rubber seals.
- Tighten all regulator-to-glass clamp bolts to **8–10 N·m** torque.

**Step 5: Reassembly and Verification**
- Reinstall door panel; torque panel screws to specification.
- Cycle the window **5 to 10 times** to confirm smooth operation.
- Test the **lock-out switch** function.
- Document the service in the vehicle logbook.

## 3.4 Maintenance Schedule (Preventive)

| Interval | Maintenance Task |
|---|---|
| Every **6 months** | Lubricate regulator pivots; clean glass channels |
| Every **12 months** | Inspect wiring harness at door boots; test switch contacts |
| Every **24 months** | Replace motor brushes (if serviceable type); check thermal breaker reset |
| Every **50,000 cycles** | Replace complete regulator assembly as preventive measure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Reading**: This is a **parallel power distribution architecture** where each door's motor is independently fed from the master switch, with individual thermal protection. The **ground return** path is critical — corroded ground straps at door hinges are one of the **top 3 failure causes** in field service.


> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schematic Interpretation**: Note the **DPDT switch topology** — the same two motor terminals are cross-connected to the battery and ground in opposite polarity depending on switch position. This is the **fundamental reversing circuit** that every examiner expects a student to draw correctly in the lab record.

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Window Up Command

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DRV as Driver
    participant SW as Window Switch
    participant FUSE as 30A Fuse
    participant TBR as Thermal Breaker
    participant MOT as DC Motor
    participant REG as Regulator
    participant GLS as Glass Pane
    DRV->>SW: Press and hold UP switch
    SW->>FUSE: Routes battery +12V
    FUSE->>TBR: Passes through to thermal breaker
    TBR->>MOT: Energizes motor terminal A
    MOT->>MOT: Spins clockwise
    MOT->>REG: Transfers torque via worm gear
    REG->>GLS: Lifts glass along channel
    GLS->>GLS: Glass reaches top
    MOT->>MOT: Stalls at top of travel
    MOT->>TBR: Current surges to stall value
    TBR->>TBR: Trips in 5 to 8 seconds
    DRV->>SW: Releases switch
    SW->>MOT: Circuit opens
```

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Note**: This sequence diagram is exactly how the **thermal protection mechanism** works. During your KTU lab exam, if asked *"Why doesn't the motor burn out when the glass reaches the top?"*, your answer must reference this **stall → current surge → thermal breaker trip** sequence.
