# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a power window system and list its main components.
**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A power window system is an electrically operated automotive comfort feature that uses reversible DC motors and mechanical regulators to raise and lower door glass via switch control. Main components are: (i) **PMDC reversible motor** (one per door), (ii) **Window regulator** (scissor, cable, or tape drive), (iii) **Control switches** (master + individual), (iv) **Thermal circuit breaker**, (v) **Fuse and wiring harness**, and (vi) **Door glass and channel**. It operates at **12 V DC** in passenger vehicles. **[3 Marks]**

### Question 2: What is the function of a thermal circuit breaker in a power window circuit?
**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A thermal circuit breaker (auto-reset type) protects the power window motor and wiring from **overcurrent damage** when the motor stalls. When the glass reaches the end of travel or is obstructed, the motor stalls and current rises to **stall value (24–30 A)**. The breaker's bimetallic strip heats up, trips, and breaks the circuit within **5 to 8 seconds**, preventing motor burnout and wire insulation damage. It automatically resets after cooling. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a power window system using a DPDT switch. Mention the role of the thermal circuit breaker. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A power window system uses a **Double Pole Double Throw (DPDT) switch** to reverse the polarity applied to a **Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) motor**, thereby reversing its direction of rotation.

**Circuit Operation:**

When the DPDT switch is in the **UP position** (refer to the schematic in Section 4.2):
- Current flows from battery positive → main fuse → UP contact → motor terminal A → through motor windings → motor terminal B → DOWN contact → ground.
- Motor rotates **clockwise**; the regulator lifts the glass.

When the switch is in the **DOWN position**:
- Current flows in **reverse polarity** through the motor.
- Motor rotates **counter-clockwise**; the regulator lowers the glass.

When the switch is released, it springs back to the **OFF (center) position**, breaking both poles of the circuit. The motor stops, and the **worm gear in the regulator** holds the glass in place through self-locking.

**Role of Thermal Circuit Breaker:**

The thermal breaker (rated **25 A**) is connected in series with the motor. Under normal operation, motor current is **5 to 8 A**. When the motor stalls (glass reaches top, frozen seal, or obstruction), current rises to the **stall value (24 A)**. The breaker's bimetallic element heats, bends, and opens the contacts within **5 to 8 seconds**. This protects:
- The motor windings from burnout
- The wiring harness from insulation damage
- Prevents fire hazard

After cooling (about 30–60 seconds), the breaker **automatically resets**, restoring operation. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Q.A (b)** List any **six common faults** of a power window system. For each fault, state the **probable cause** and the **corrective action**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Fault | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Window does not operate in any direction | Blown main fuse | Replace 30 A fuse after verifying no short circuit |
| 2 | Window operates slowly and noisily | Worn motor brushes, dry regulator pivots | Replace motor or lubricate pivots with lithium grease |
| 3 | Window moves only in UP or DOWN | Faulty DPDT switch contact, broken wiring | Test switch continuity; replace switch or repair wire |
| 4 | Glass drops inside the door | Broken regulator cable / snapped scissor arm | Replace complete regulator assembly |
| 5 | Master switch works, individual door switch does not | Failed individual switch or broken door jamb wire | Replace switch; repair wires in rubber boot |
| 6 | Window reverses on closing (anti-pinch) | Obstruction in channel, faulty pinch sensor | Clean channel; recalibrate anti-pinch module |

**[7 Marks: 1 mark per fault row, 1 mark for overall presentation]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a)** Explain the construction and working of an **X-arm (scissor-type) window regulator** with a labeled diagram. Mention its advantages over cable-type regulators. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
The X-arm (scissor) regulator consists of two arms arranged in a crossed "X" pattern:
- One arm is pivoted to the **door frame** (fixed pivot).
- The other arm is pivoted to a **sliding bracket** attached to the bottom of the glass.
- The arms cross at a central pivot point connected to the **motor's output gear (worm wheel)**.
- As the worm wheel rotates, it drives the central pivot, which causes the arms to fold and unfold in a scissor motion.

**Working:**
- When the motor rotates **clockwise** (UP command), the worm wheel drives the central pivot in a direction that **extends** the X-arm, pushing the sliding bracket upward along the door channel.
- When the motor rotates **counter-clockwise** (DOWN command), the X-arm **folds**, pulling the bracket downward.

**Advantages over Cable-type Regulator:**
1. **More compact** — fits thinner door cavities.
2. **Greater rigidity** — provides better glass stability during travel.
3. **Easier to service** — fewer parts, no cables to snap.
4. **Higher load capacity** — can lift heavier glass panels.
5. **Longer life** — no cable wear or stretch over time.

**[7 Marks: 3 for construction, 2 for working, 2 for advantages]**

---

**Q.B (b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure** for diagnosing a power window that is **not operating** using a **digital multimeter**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Tools Required:** Digital multimeter (DMM), trim removal tools, vehicle service manual.

**Step 1: Preliminary Check (1 Mark)**
- Verify battery voltage: DMM in DC Volts mode, probe battery terminals. Reading must be **> 12.4 V**. If low, charge or replace battery first.

**Step 2: Fuse Inspection (1 Mark)**
- Locate the power window fuse in the cabin fuse box (refer to owner's manual).
- Remove fuse; set DMM to **continuity mode** (beep).
- Place probes on both fuse blades. **Beep = good fuse; no beep = blown fuse**.
- Replace if blown, but **investigate cause** (short circuit) before operating.

**Step 3: Switch Test (2 Marks)**
- Remove the master switch panel (use trim tools).
- Disconnect switch connector.
- Set DMM to **Ohms (Ω)** mode.
- With switch in **UP position**, probe common and UP terminals → expect **0 Ω (continuity)**.
- With switch in **DOWN position**, probe common and DOWN terminals → expect **0 Ω**.
- With switch in **OFF (center)**, expect **infinite resistance (OL)**.
- Any deviation → switch is faulty, replace.

**Step 4: Voltage at Motor Connector (2 Marks)**
- Reconnect the switch connector.
- Set DMM to **DC Volts (20 V range)**.
- Have an assistant press the UP switch.
- Probe the two terminals at the motor connector.
- Expect **~12 V** (battery voltage). Reverse probes for DOWN command.
- If voltage is present but motor does not run → **motor is faulty**.
- If voltage is absent → **wiring fault upstream**.

**Step 5: Ground Path Verification (1 Mark)**
- Set DMM to **Ohms** mode.
- Probe motor body or connector ground terminal to a known good chassis ground.
- Expect **< 0.5 Ω**. Higher resistance → clean and tighten ground strap.

**Conclusion:** Based on readings, isolate fault to: **Fuse | Switch | Wiring | Motor | Ground**. **[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing DPDT with SPST switches** in circuit diagrams — this is a **2-mark deduction** in most valuation keys.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the self-locking worm gear** when explaining why the glass stays in position — at least **1 mark** is reserved for this.
> 3. **Stating "fuse" instead of "thermal circuit breaker"** for the in-line protection — examiners treat these as **different components** with **different functions** (fuse is one-time, breaker is resettable).
> 4. **Not specifying numerical values** (12 V, 30 A, 5–8 s) in answers — KTU 2024 scheme rewards **quantitative answers** with higher marks.
> 5. **Drawing the regulator incorrectly** — the X-arm must show **two crossed arms with a central pivot**, not a single straight arm.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Operating voltage:** **12 V DC** (passenger vehicles); **24 V DC** (commercial vehicles)
- **Motor type:** **PMDC reversible motor**, rated **60–150 W**
- **Normal current:** **5–8 A**; **Stall current:** **24–30 A**
- **Fuse rating:** **30 A** (main); **Thermal breaker:** **25 A** (per motor)
- **Switch type:** **DPDT momentary** with center-OFF
- **Regulator types:** **X-arm (scissor)**, **Cable drive**, **Flexible tape drive**
- **Self-locking feature:** **Worm and wheel gear** prevents glass from falling
- **Lock-out switch:** Disables rear switches (child safety feature)
- **Top failure points:** **Door hinge ground strap**, **door boot wires**, **regulator cables**, **motor brushes**
- **Diagnostic priority order:** **Battery → Fuse → Switch → Voltage at motor → Motor → Ground**
- **Lubricant:** **White lithium grease** for pivots; **silicone spray** for rubber channels
- **Thermal breaker reset time:** **30–60 seconds** after cooling
- **Glass travel time:** **3–5 seconds** (full open to full close)
- **Anti-pinch feature:** Reverses glass if obstruction detected (modern systems)
- **KTU exam trick question:** *"Why is PMDC preferred over wound-field DC motor in power windows?"* — Answer: **High starting torque, reversible by polarity reversal, compact size, no field winding losses**.

---

A neatly drawn DPDT schematic alone can fetch **2 to 3 marks** even if the rest of the record is average.
